spans.text:
Simone Weil
Phil. 177b: Simone Weil
Spring, 2025
Tues./Fri. 12:45 – 2:05, Shiffman 125
Prof. Palle Yourgrau
yourgrau@brandeis.edu
Office hours will be conducted on Zoom. Email me to schedule a meeting.
2
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description:
Simone Weil (1909 – 1943), revolutionary and mystic, is without doubt one of the
greatest Christian thinkers – and one of the greatest Christian writers --since St.
Augustine, as well as one of the most original philosophers of the twentieth century. She
has always existed, however, on the margins of academe. No one, it seems, knows quite
what to do with her. She died very young and most of her work was assembled and
published after her death based on her extensive Notebooks. Nevertheless, she has
acquired something of a cult status among a small but significant number of writers,
religious thinkers, social reformers, and academics, including her first champion, the
great existentialist philosopher, Albert Camus, and no less a poet than T.S. Eliot. Iris
Murdoch, philosopher and novelist, was also a great admirer and borrowed some of
Weil’s key ideas.
Born before WWI, Weil died toward the end of WWII, having begun as a Marxist labor
organizer (though trained as a philosopher) and ended as Christian mystic. Like
Augustine, she attempted in her philosophical writings, and in her life’s activities, to
bring together Athens and Jerusalem, to unite, that is, the Greek philosophical tradition,
in particular Plato, with Biblical religion, in particular, the Christian Gospels. Her attempt
to reconcile human suffering with divine perfection is as profound as it is disquieting.
Indeed, much of her writing is discordant and disturbing, a slap in the face no less than
a challenge to rethink our lives. Socrates comes to mind, as well as the ancient Hebrew
prophets. We are dealing, here, with a figure of Biblical proportions who, for some
reason, fate has delivered to us in our own lifetime.
It behooves, us, then, to address, with the full resources of contemporary philosophy
the question of what Weil achieved, in her life no less than her work, as well

3
as to raise a cautionary note with regard to her failings. It is an investigation that is at
once philosophical, literary, religious, and practical.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Themes to be discussed include, but are not limited, to the following:
1) Weil’s biography. Secular upbringing; Jewish heritage. Her relationship with her
brother, the great mathematician, André Weil. Her early disengagement from all
physical contact; her alienation from food, from her body, more generally. Early
sympathy with the working class: “I was a Bolshevik from the age of 10”. Studies at
Lycee and Ecole Normale in philosophy. Thesis on Descartes, but love affair with
Plato. Encounter with Simone de Beauvoir; mutual suspicion. Teaching and labor
organizing. Gradual disaffection with Marxism as a revolutionary social program;
Oppression and Liberty; “fight” with Trotsky. Leaves of absence from teaching to
engage in factory work. “Soldier” against Franco in the Spanish Civil War; The Iliad:
Poem of Force. Injured in war; recovery, and “epiphany” in Assisi: Christian
revelation. Turn toward mysticism. Germany invades France: flight to Marseilles,
encounter with the Catholics Fr. Perrin and Gustave Thibon; Gravity and Grace;
Intimations of Christianity among the Ancient Greeks; flight to New York. Flight to
London and the Free French. The Need for Roots, tuberculosis, death.
2) Weil and Marxism. Her attempt to develop a new theory of alienated labor and
freedom from oppression. Theoretical split with orthodox Marxism: dispute with
Trotsky. Her experience working in factories. Oppression and Liberty. Discovery,
there, that she is a “slave”; later that Christianity is the religion of slaves (Nietzsche
agrees); identification, later, with that religion (for the very reason Nietzsche, and
many modern “liberated intellectuals”, reject it).
3) Reflections on war. What went wrong in the Spanish Civil War. Correspondence with
Georges Bernanos. What went wrong with the Marxist revolution. Comparison

4
with the Trojan War. The Iliad: Poem of Force. Pacifism; but then the Nazis. Gradual
skepticism of “political” solutions to social evils, in labor, in war, in government
(fascism, communism). Her final work on reconstructing France after WWII: The
Need for Roots: a “revolutionary” proposal --focus not on rights but on duties.
4) Critique of “separation” (nationalism, “tribalism”, “one’s own”) Critique of the
Jewish concept of “the chosen”. (Was Weil an antisemite ? See Cabaud, Steiner,
Mehlman, Rabi, Yourgrau, et al.) Critique of the Catholic concept of “no salvation
outside the Church”, and of “anathema sit”. (James Carroll, Constantine’s Sword)
Critique of the violence in the Hebrew Bible. Critique of the violence in Catholic
history (Inquisition, Crusades, etc..)
5) The importance of beauty. Rediscovery of Plato. (Scarry, On Beauty and Being Just;
Yourgrau, Simone Weil) God and the beauty of the world: missing in Christianity, but
present in Judaism. Refusal to allow “art” to monopolize beauty: back to Plato’s
Republic and “banishing the artists”. (Iris Murdoch: The Fire and the Sun: Why Plato
Banished the Artists) The moral obligation of artists. Critique of “art for art’s sake”.
Discovering beauty and finding God. (Intimations of Christianity Among the Ancient
Greeks)
6) Death and suffering. Suffering in Weil’s own life: empathy with the suffering of the
world. Death vs. suffering: Plato vs. Christianity ? (Iris Murdoch, The Sovereignty of
Good.) Is Weil’s emphasis on suffering, in her life and in her thought, a pathology ?
Is it linked with the “ideology” of Christianity ?
7) God and the Good. Weil’s axiom: God is essentially good. (Gravity and Grace;
Murdoch) Weil’s thesis: any assertion about God not acting in consonance with the
good is ipso facto false. Read the Bible via morality, not read morality via the Bible.
(Yourgrau, Simone Weil; Kant; Plato’s Euthyphro.)

5
8) Reimagining Christianity. What the incarnation of the divine means. Wyschogrod, “A
Jewish Perspective on Incarnation” and The Body of Faith: God and the People Israel.
Good and God: Is Platonic transcendence compatible with Christian immanence ?
Can one really reconcile Plato with Christianity, Athens with Jerusalem ? Can there
be more than one incarnation of the divine ? (Gravity and Grace; Yourgrau, Simone
Weil)
(The amount of time spent on each theme will depend on how the class discussion
proceeds.)
Paradoxes in Weil’s thought to be discussed. (Can they be resolved ?)
(1) Beauty should be gazed at, not “eaten”; at the same time, aesthetes de facto belittle
beauty, whereas it should be consumed or “eaten”.
(2) God changes nothing; yet we shouldn’t approach God, we should wait for God to
attend to us.
(3) True religion is universal; yet Weil inclines to a particular religion, Catholicism, and is
preoccupied by the symbol of the cross.
(4) One cannot have direct, unmediated contact with God; yet at Assisi, Weil says
“Christ” himself “came down and took me”.
(5) The real miracle in Christianity is that goodness itself (not simply its reflection) can
exist in the world; yet goodness, as such, does not exist in “the world of facts.” (6)
Collectivity, the “great beast” of Plato’s Republic, is a primary source of evil; yet in
The Need for Roots, Weil says that only the collective can provide for a nation its
spiritual roots.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning goals: Students will learn how to read Simone Weil’s books and essays in the
context in which they were written. They will learn how to evaluate the cogency of
Weil’s many, and controversial, ideas, especially in relationship to her attachment to
Greek and Biblical thought. They will come to know how Weil’s ideas relate to
contemporary thought about justice, ethics, religion, science, and mathematics.
Students will acquire the ability to compare Weil with more recent thinkers like Iris
Murdoch.

Course Description
Course Prerequisite(s):
• BIOL14a
• BIOL15b
Learning Goals:
By the end of this course students should be able to:
• Read scientific papers and analyze the data within them
• Present scientific information either individually or in groups
• Define and describe the four biomacromolecular structures and elucidate their importance in
structural biology.
• Describe the techniques of X-ray crystallography, NMR, Cryo-EM, as well as other techniques and
relay their importance and application in the field of structural biology research.
This course will employ various evidence-based learning strategies. We will employ case studies, group
work, lectures, debate, and student led-presentations on a regular basis. For this reason, attendance and
participation is required of all students to be successful in this course.
All students enrolled in BIOL102b must follow the rules and expectations below:
Attendance and Participation
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: • Class Attendance: You must attend the two weekly classes. Participation and attendance are vital to\nthe class and will be a large part of your final grade. Because of the discussion-based nature of this\ncourse, we will all be required to be prepared, engaged, and interactive during class time.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
• Active Engagement: Science is best learned by actively listening, taking notes, and following
explanations in real time. Because much of the material builds on equations, diagrams, and in-class
discussion, recordings will not be provided except for students with official accommodations. This
approach helps you develop the skills needed to follow and apply scientific reasoning, which are
essential for success in future classes, research, and professional work.
Device and Note-taking Policy
• Research shows that access to cell phones and laptops in class reduces focus and learning—not
only for the user but also for those nearby. For this reason, all cell phones and laptops must be
turned off and stored during any lectures and/or presentations.
• Evidence also indicates that handwritten notes improve learning and understanding compared to
typed notes. Students are expected to take notes by hand (pen or pencil). Tablets may be used
only for handwritten notes, with cellular and Wi-Fi disabled. If tablet use becomes distracting to
others, you may be asked to switch to paper.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: • Students with an approved accommodation for laptop note-taking must meet with the professor\nbefore the first lecture.\n• Computer/tablet use is allowed when working in groups to analyze papers.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
AI Policy
• AI and generative AI can be valuable tools in science, but they cannot replace your own
understanding—you must be able to recognize when these tools are incorrect.
• AI-generated content is not your own work and must always be cited if used.
• If you use AI in any submitted assignment, you must also submit the AI output along with your
work.
• For studying or unsubmitted work, you may use AI, but you are strongly encouraged to verify all
information to avoid learning incorrect material.

• Earned during the 8 in-person classes on Fridays between 9:35 AM and 10:55 AM.

spans.text:

• Due Mondays by 11:59 PM EST via Gradescope before each of the Group Presentation classes
o Not required before the Individual Presentation classes.
• In addition to the figure you are assigned to present. You will be assigned a different figure for
which you must write questions to ask the student who present that figure.
o You should write at least two questions, but quality is more important that quantity.
• Earn points for submitting on time, demonstrating effort, writing useful questions, and writing
questions of the correct scope and level.
• Late Figure Questions are not accepted.
• Lowest Figure Questions grade dropped.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Group Presentations
• Held on Tuesdays during class as specified on the Course Calendar below.
• You will present with your group.
o Presentations will be between 5 and 10 minutes long depending on the figure.
o Each student is graded individually.
o Each group should focus on the following:
1. Briefly set the audience up. What do they need to know before you get into the
data contained in the figure.
a. Note that the figures will be presented in the order in which they are
found in the paper (Figure 1, then 2, the 3), but that does not mean
Figure 1 sets up the audience to understand Figure 2.
2. Briefly introduce the techniques used to generate the data.
a. What does the audience need to know to understand the results?
3. What are the results from the figure? How do they fit into the conclusions?
4. What would you modify or clarify if you were the author making the figure?
Why?
a. No figure is perfect. There are always mistakes or things that can be
changed to make it easier to understand.
• At the end of your presentation, Dr. P will ask questions.
• Must be physically present to earn a grade.
• Earn points for presenting, being well prepared, not making egregious mistakes, and being clear
and easy to follow.
• Lowest Group Presentation grade dropped.
Individual Presentations
• You will perform two Individual Presentations using the same format as the Group Presentations.
o Think of the Group Presentations as practice for the Individual Presentations.
o The format will be the same, you will just present individually.

Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students, and I
want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability accommodations, you will
need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can contact them at 781-736-3537, email them
at access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student Accessibility Support home page. You can find helpful
student FAQs and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you
might be eligible for support from SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as you can
so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class. In order to coordinate
exam accommodations, ideally you should provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an
exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own effort.
Infringement of academic integrity by a student subjects that student to serious penalties, which may
include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the University or other
sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures
related to academic integrity. Students may be required to submit work via Compilatio or similar software
to verify originality. A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic integrity as they apply to a
specific course or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or
assignment before submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to
the Department of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be
found at Brandeis Library Guides - Citing Sources.
All written work and assignments must be completed independently unless specifically told otherwise. It
is acceptable to use the words or ideas of another person, provided the source is properly
acknowledged. You must use proper citations and quotation marks to indicate the source of any phrases,
sentences, paragraphs or ideas found in published volumes, on the internet, or created by another
student. Note that the use of full quotations is prohibited. You must cite using the specific format
provided on Moodle.
All assignments submitted are checked with Compilatio software. Any assignment that is flagged as
having a high similarity or identity to any other work, whether from another Brandeis student or from
another source, that is not cited will be submitted to the Office of Student Rights and Community
Standards.
If any assignment is thought to be produced by any person or any software other than the student which
submitted, an in-person meeting with the faculty meeting will be scheduled. During this meeting an oral
explanation of the assignment, the thought process behind the assignment, and the ideas within the
assignment will be assessed. If a student is unable to orally defend the assignment, the assignment and
student will be reported to the Office of Student Rights and Community Standards.
Any assignment found to be plagiarized will be an undroppable zero.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

Fall 2025 (Draft)

spans.text:
COURSE DESCRIPTION AND PREREQUISITES
The course introduces fundamental methods and frameworks for portfolio financial modeling.
Topics covered include mean-variance- covariance portfolio optimization, Black-Litterman
approach to portfolio optimization and other portfolio models. The course is designed to be
hands-on implementation of these models using real data and hence requires solid understanding
of matrix and array functions and how to implement them in excel or any computational
software.
The course culminates with group project applying modeling framework developed in the course
on modeling real institutional portfolios and presentations.
This course should appeal to students with career objectives in portfolio/asset management and
related careers goals.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Prerequisite: FIN 201a or FIN 203a. Students are expected to know basic excel. Meets for one-\nhalf semester and yield half-course credit.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
LEARNING GOALS:
• Develop Portfolio Modeling Skills Using Excel and VBA/Python
• Develop Skills for Implementing Mean Variance Covariance Portfolio Optimization
• Develop Skill sets for Black-Litterman Approach to Portfolio Optimization
• Develop Framework for Asset Allocation, Portfolio Risk, Return objectives and
Constraints for institutional portfolios.
• Application of portfolio modeling skills developed in the course to real institutional
portfolio modeling project and presentation.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Required Textbook
Financial Modeling, by Simon Benninga, 4th edition, The MIT Press, Cambridge, MA
I may assign other additional readings on some of the topics during the semester.
Regular reading of the Wall Street Journal, the Economist, Financial Times and other financial
press is strongly recommended.
spans.label: MATERIAL

the semester. We will take class attendance. Unless there is a documented medical and family
emergency where students may be excused upon providing documentation, any student who
misses a class, his/her final percentage point will be reduced by 1% for each class missed.

• Portfolio Financial Modeling Project 35%
• Presentation of Financial Modeling Project 15%
• Financial Modeling Case/Assignments 30%
• Class Participation 20%
Total 100%
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Please read the following description of what is expected in each of the above sections used for
determination of the total course grade.
Modeling Assignments
The best way to learn financial modeling is by doing it! There will be three modeling
assignments that are designed to give students an opportunity to develop portfolio financial
2

modeling skills at each stage based on real market data. Each of the assignments are designed to
be building blocks of an integrated portfolio modeling project.
Portfolio Modeling Project and Presentation
A final portfolio modeling project is designed to apply and integrate the skills developed in the
course to real world institutional portfolios modeling. Therefore, successful completion of this
course requires a final portfolio financial modeling project and presentation. You will form a
group of two and select institutional portfolios that I will approve early in the semester. I will
provide a detailed guideline and framework for this project in due course.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Class Participation
The teaching methodology of this course combines lectures, cases and textbook readings as
stated above and hence students are expected to come prepared to each class and actively
participate in class discussion
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Workload Expectation
Success in this two- credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum
of 9 hours of study per week in preparation for class (readings, papers, discussion sections,
preparation for exams, etc.)
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Accommodation:
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students,
and I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability
accommodations, you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS) (781-736-3470,
access@brandeis.edu). You can find helpful student FAQs and other resources on the SAS
website, including guidance on how to know whether you might be eligible for support from SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as
you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodation for this class. To
coordinate exam accommodation, ideally you should provide the accommodation letter at least 48
hours before an exam
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of
academic integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the
student's own effort. Infringement of academic integrity by a student subject that student to serious
penalties, which may include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the
University or other sanctions. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for
all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Students may be required to submit work
via TurnItIn.com or similar software to verify originality. A student who is in doubt regarding
standards of academic integrity as they apply to a specific course or assignment should consult
with the faculty member responsible for that course or assignment before submitting the work.
Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the Department of Student Rights
and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be found at Brandeis Library
Guides - Citing Sources.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Breaks during class meetings: This is a 3 hour weekly class meetings. I will give you 10
minutes break in the middle of the 3 hour class. Students are expected to promptly come back to
the class after those 10 minutes. My TA tracks class attendance after the break as well.
Office Hours: You may see me during my regular office hours or by appointment (which you
should request by email).

spans.text:
Calculus:
Calculus is one of the greatest intellectual achievements of humankind.
The beautiful idea at the heart of this subject allows us to explore both
the infinite and the infinitesimal. This gives us the tools to model and then
analyze phenomena that change, i.e. pretty much anything!
Learning Goals for Math 10a:
1. Identify and apply key ideas and skills of Differential Calculus. This
will be done by mastering the Outcomes detailed on the Brandeis
Moodle page.
Figure1: TheFourWords
See more on “Mastery Grading” below.
at the Heart of Calculus
2. Transfer familiar concepts to unfamiliar contexts. (link)
3. Hone your problem solving skills.

Perusall.

spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Mastery Grading
What is Mastery Grading?
Mastery Grading is a grading structure that is designed to maxi-
mize student learning. It rewards good study habits like hard work,
persistence, repeated practice, review and reflection.
Here’s how it works:
OurcoursehasalistofContent Outcomes–thesearemathemat-
ical facts and skills that comprise the content we are learning in this
class. (Content outcomes include things like “Evaluate trig functions
of special angles.” and “Solve a word problem that involves a log scale.” – see the end of this syllabus
for a complete list.)
Our Friday Assessments give you the opportunity to demonstrate mastery of the content we have
coveredsofarinthecourse. Problemswillbegradedonacredit/no-creditbasis. Yourworkwillbeheld
to a high standard: demonstrating clear mastery of the material as determined by the grader. If you
demonstrate mastery on a particular outcome you no longer need to do those problems on subsequent
FAs.
Re-dos: If you do not earn credit on a particular outcome, it does not count against you. But this
means you need to review/study/practice that concept or skill further so you’re ready to try again on
the next FA.
This structure is known as “high-frequency, low-stakes” assessment: we have a high frequency
of tests (8 total) but they are low stakes because getting something wrong doesn’t count against you.
Withthisgradingstructure, youcanbeconfidentateverypointinthesemesterthatyouhavestrong
skills in all the outcomes you mastered, and you will know exactly which skills and ideas need more
work.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Why do we like it?
There are so many reasons why we like this system better than a traditional grading structure. Here
are just a few:
1. Everyone can do well. There’s no curve, so there’s no limit on how many students can get A’s.
Our goal is for everyone to learn the material and succeed in the class. We believe you can do it
and we designed this course to help you succeed.
2. It avoids the “bad day” catastrophe. In a traditional low-frequency, high-stakes testing system,
one bad day can tank your entire grade. In this system, you can make up for a bad day next time.
3. Studentscanlearnatdifferentpacesandstillsucceed. Ifyou’restrugglingwithaparticularconcept
and don’t get it right away but keep working on it, you’re rewarded for learning it eventually just
the same as someone who learned it immediately. So keep working – persistence is rewarded.
4. Highstandards: thecredit/no-creditgradingmeansyouknowyouarebeingheldtohighstandards
and can (and should!) be really proud of each and every credit you earn.
5. Unveiling of hidden expectations: our Practice Assessments can help you prepare directly for each
Outcome even before they are first released but it is always possible that you may discover other
expectations when first encountering a skill in a test environment. The re-do structure allows you
to learn from one test and apply those lessons to the next test to demonstrate your newfound
learning.
6. Clarity about learning: In this system, you have a detailed list of all the things you will learn
throughoutthecourse. AsyoumasterdifferentOutcomes, youhaveaclearunderstandingofwhat
you have already learned and what you still need to work on.
7. This structure rewards good study habits, like regular practice, review of past work, and a focus
on full understanding of the material.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
How to succeed in this course:
This structure may be new to you. Succeeding may
require you to adjust your study habits. Since this
structure rewards good study habits, we hope you
will reflect on your approach and adjust as neces-
sary. This should help you to build better study
habits that you can apply to future classes as well.
Here are some tips to get off to a great start:
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
1. Attend class. This is essential. All learning is social and this is your primary opportunity to
meet classmates with whom you can discuss the material both in and out of class. It is also your
best opportunity to hear how the expert (your teacher) presents the material, ask questions in
real time, set a routine, and keep up with the class.
2. Do your homework. Mathematical intuition is not inborn; you have to develop it. Homework
is a great way to do this. These assignments are designed to help you build that intuition. All
assignments have a series of easier problems that build up to harder problems so that you can
recognize patterns and deeply ingrain your learning. The assignments also include challenging
problems and problems that approach the material from different ways, so this is a great way to
prepare for the Challenge Outcomes.
3. Attend RAPS (Review, Advising and Preparation Stations). These will take place on
the Fridays when we don’t have FAs. Review your past work, compare to our provided solutions,
talk to your classmates and our staff to make sure you fully understand the concepts and the
techniques. Then practice, practice, practice to ingrain that learning.
4. Talk to people. There is compelling research showing that students who form social groups
around academics are much more successful in college math classes than those who work alone.
You will have opportunities to meet and talk to other students in class and RAPS, as well as in
tutoring and your instructor’s office hours. Utilize all these resources and form your own study
groups.
5. Utilize office hours and tutoring. Upper class students and alum regularly regret not making
better use of resources like these. Add them to your calendar and attend regularly, even if you
don’t have questions. These are great places to work on homework: they eliminate distractions
so you can focus and there’s an expert on hand if you get stuck or have questions. Check your
Brandeis Moodle page for details on instructor office hours.
6. Utilize Practice Assessments. These provide sample practice problems, grading rubric infor-
mation, and additional resources (usually videos and more practice problems) on the last page.
7. Review and reflect. Reflection is the foundation of learning. Reviewing past mistakes and
understandingwhatwentwrongisthekeytocorrectinganymisunderstandings, avoidingcommon
mistakes, and meeting expectations. We provide solutions to all FAs so you can compare your
work to our solutions.
spans.label: DESCRIP

Grades:
Letter Grade:
Each skill mastered on FAs counts toward either your Fundamental Outcomes or General Outcomes
score in the chart below. See the last page of this syllabus for a full list of Outcomes.
At the end of the semester, your letter grade will be calculated using the following table.
Fundamental General
Letter Grade
Outcomes Outcomes
A+ 9 32
A 9 30
A− 9 29
B+ 8 28
B 8 27
B− 8 26
C+ 7 25
C 7 24
C− 7 23
D+ 6 21
D 6 20
D− 6 19
Minimums: You have to get both the Fundamental and General Outcomes scores for any given
grade. In particular, if you have 7 points on the Fundamental Outcomes, the highest grade you can
earn is a C+, even if your General Outcomes score is higher than 25. The scores listed are minimums,
e.g., with a score of 28 on the General Outcomes, the highest grade you can earn is a B+ (since 28 is
less than the minimum of 29 points for A-).
E Grades: Failure to meet either of the D− standards will result in an E.

spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: More on grading →
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Friday Assessments
• Closed Book, No Calculators, Provided Formula Sheet: TheFAsareclosedbook,meaning
that no notes, books, or internet-connected devices are allowed during the FA. You are also not
allowed to use a calculator. However, we will include a formula sheet with some basic information
to help alleviate the burden of memorization and mistakes due to anxiety-induced blanking out.
You will have the formula sheet ahead of time so you know what is on it.
• Makeups for FAs: For most FAs (when the schedule allows), we will provide a makeup exam
during the FA recitation period following the regularly scheduled test. If you need to miss an FA,
you can take the corresponding makeup test (but you would have to miss that week’s RAPS).
In this case, you can just show up to the makeup the following week (no need to contact us in
advance). If you took the regularly scheduled test, you may not take the corresponding makeup.
Note that there may be one or two FAs that do not have an associated makeup. If you happen
to miss that FA you will not have a makeup for that test, though you will still have multiple
opportunities to try those problems on subsequent FAs. See schedule later in the syllabus for
precise details.
• Regrade Requests: We are all human and sometimes make mistakes, so please check your test
and submit a regrade request on Gradescope if, e.g, you forgot to select a page or selected the
wrongpage; youthinkthegradermighthavemissedsomethingorclickedthewrongrubricbutton;
or if you don’t understand why you didn’t get credit. One of our instructors will take another look
at your work and will make a final decision on whether you earned credit on that problem. If you
did not earn credit, the grader will try to provide additional feedback so you can better prepare
for the next FA. The grader’s decision on your regrade request is final. Regrade requests are
due by midnight on the Wednesday after you took the FA. Late regrades requests will
not be considered; you can always try that problem on the next FA.
• Final: We will hold a final during the registrar’s scheduled final exam period. The exact date
and time is determined by the registrar after the add/drop period. Please do not make end-of-
semester travel plans until you know the final exam schedule.
Our final will consist of one additional FA. (It is not a traditional comprehensive final.)
The final is the end of the semester. There is no extra credit or additional work after the final. All
homework and participation assignments must be completed by the last deadline for your section
but certainly before the final.
Homework and Participation:
These are the last two General Outcomes. They are the only Outcomes that are not tested on FAs.
Instead, these are cumulative throughout the semester. You earn points for each assignment and these
are all added together (not averaged). You have to meet a certain minimum score to earn the point for
each of these two Outcomes.
• Homework (General Outcome #31):
You will earn the point for this Outcome if you accumulate at least 200 HW points.
There will be 25 homework assignments. Each assignment is worth 15 points: 5 points for com-
pleting the assignment and 10 points for accuracy (we will grade 5 of the questions, each worth 2
points). There is a total of 375 possible points; the threshold of 200 is a little more than half of
the total possible points.

My name is Mac. I was born in Seoul, Korea, adopted by an
American family and grew up in Chandler, Arizona. I went
to college in Chicago and grad school at Brandeis. I’ve spent
the last three years at Boston College and now I’m excited
to be back at Brandeis! In my spare time, I watch a lot of
sports, movies, and TV shows. Mostly a basketball guy, but
the Diamondbacks are good this year, so I’ve been watching
a lot of baseball. You may see me very happy or very sad at

spans.text:
Calculus:
Calculus is one of the greatest intellectual achievements of humankind.
The beautiful idea at the heart of this subject allows us to explore both
the infinite and the infinitesimal. This gives us the tools to model and then
analyze phenomena that change, i.e. pretty much anything!
Learning Goals for Math 10b:
1. Identify and apply key ideas and skills of Integral Calculus. This will
be done by mastering the Outcomes detailed on the Latte page. See
more on “Mastery Grading” below.
Figure1: TheFourWords
2. Transfer familiar concepts to unfamiliar contexts. at the Heart of Calculus
(link)
3. Hone your problem solving skills.
4. Develop and leverage a learning community for math.

spans.text:
Mastery Grading
What is Mastery Grading?
Mastery Grading is a grading structure that is designed to maxi-
mize student learning. It rewards good study habits like hard work,
persistence, repeated practice, review and reflection.
Here’s how it works:
OurcoursehasalistofContent Outcomes–thesearemathemat-
ical facts and skills that comprise the content we are learning in this
class. (Content outcomes include things like “Evaluate trig functions
of special angles.” and “Solve a word problem that involves a log scale.” – see the end of this syllabus
for a complete list.)
Our Friday Assessments give you the opportunity to demonstrate mastery of the content we have
coveredsofarinthecourse. Problemswillbegradedonacredit/no-creditbasis. Yourworkwillbeheld
to a high standard: demonstrating clear mastery of the material as determined by the grader. If you
demonstrate mastery on a particular outcome you no longer need to do those problems on subsequent
FAs.
Re-dos: If you do not earn credit on a particular outcome, it does not count against you. But this
means you need to review/study/practice that concept or skill further so you’re ready to try again on
the next FA.
This structure is known as “high-frequency, low-stakes” assessment: we have a high frequency
of tests (8 total) but they are low stakes because getting something wrong doesn’t count against you.
Withthisgradingstructure, youcanbeconfidentateverypointinthesemesterthatyouhavestrong
skills in all the outcomes you mastered, and you will know exactly which skills and ideas need more
work.
Why do we like it?
There are so many reasons why we like this system better than a traditional grading structure. Here
are just a few:
1. Everyone can do well. There’s no curve, so there’s no limit on how many students can get A’s.
Our goal is for everyone to learn the material and succeed in the class. We believe you can do it
and we designed this course to help you succeed.
2. It avoids the “bad day” catastrophe. In a traditional low-frequency, high-stakes testing system,
one bad day can tank your entire grade. In this system, you can make up for a bad day next time.
3. Studentscanlearnatdifferentpacesandstillsucceed. Ifyou’restrugglingwithaparticularconcept
and don’t get it right away but keep working on it, you’re rewarded for learning it eventually just
the same as someone who learned it immediately. So keep working – persistence is rewarded.
4. Highstandards: thecredit/no-creditgradingmeansyouknowyouarebeingheldtohighstandards
and can (and should!) be really proud of each and every credit you earn.
5. Unveiling of hidden expectations: our Practice Assessments can help you prepare directly for each
Outcome even before they are first released but it is always possible that you may discover other
expectations when first encountering a skill in a test environment. The re-do structure allows you
to learn from one test and apply those lessons to the next test to demonstrate your newfound
learning.
6. Clarity about learning: In this system, you have a detailed list of all the things you will learn
throughoutthecourse. AsyoumasterdifferentOutcomes, youhaveaclearunderstandingofwhat
you have already learned and what you still need to work on.
7. This structure rewards good study habits, like regular practice, review of past work, and a focus
on full understanding of the material.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
How to succeed in this course:
This structure may be new to you. Succeeding may
require you to adjust your study habits. Since this
structure rewards good study habits, we hope you
will reflect on your approach and adjust as neces-
sary. This should help you to build better study
habits that you can apply to future classes as well.
Here are some tips to get off to a great start:
1. Attend class. This is essential. All learning is social and this is your primary opportunity to
meet classmates with whom you can discuss the material both in and out of class. It is also your
best opportunity to hear how the expert (your teacher) presents the material, ask questions in
real time, set a routine, and keep up with the class.
2. Do your homework. Mathematical intuition is not inborn; you have to develop it. Homework
is a great way to do this. These assignments are designed to help you build that intuition. All
assignments have a series of easier problems that build up to harder problems so that you can
recognize patterns and deeply ingrain your learning. The assignments also include challenging
problems and problems that approach the material from different ways, so this is a great way to
prepare for the Challenge Outcomes.
3. Attend RAPS (Review, Advising and Preparation Stations). These will take place on
the Fridays when we don’t have FAs. Review your past work, compare to our provided solutions,
talk to your classmates and our staff to make sure you fully understand the concepts and the
techniques. Then practice, practice, practice to ingrain that learning.
4. Talk to people. There is compelling research showing that students who form social groups
around academics are much more successful in college math classes than those who work alone.
You will have opportunities to meet and talk to other students in class and RAPS, as well as in
tutoring and your instructor’s office hours. Utilize all these resources and form your own study
groups.
5. Utilize office hours and tutoring. Upper class students and alum regularly regret not making
better use of resources like these. Add them to your calendar and attend regularly, even if you
don’t have questions. These are great places to work on homework: they eliminate distractions
so you can focus and there’s an expert on hand if you get stuck or have questions. Check your
Brandeis Moodle page for details on instructor office hours.
6. Utilize Practice Assessments. These provide sample practice problems, grading rubric infor-
mation, and additional resources (usually videos and more practice problems) on the last page.
7. Review and reflect. Reflection is the foundation of learning. Reviewing past mistakes and
understandingwhatwentwrongisthekeytocorrectinganymisunderstandings, avoidingcommon
mistakes, and meeting expectations. We provide solutions to all FAs so you can compare your
work to our solutions.

Minimums: You have to get both the Fundamental and General Outcomes scores for any given
grade. In particular, if you have 8 points on the Fundamental Outcomes, the highest grade you can
earn is a C+, even if your General Outcomes score is higher than 25. The scores listed are minimums,

spans.text:
Friday Assessments
• Closed Book, No Calculators, Provided Formula Sheet: TheFAsareclosedbook,meaning
that no notes, books, or internet-connected devices are allowed during the FA. You are also not
allowed to use a calculator. However, we will include a formula sheet with some basic information
to help alleviate the burden of memorization and mistakes due to anxiety-induced blanking out.
You will have the formula sheet ahead of time so you know what is on it.
• Makeups for FAs: For most FAs (when the schedule allows), we will provide a makeup exam
during the FA recitation period following the regularly scheduled test. If you need to miss an FA,
you can take the corresponding makeup test (but you would have to miss that week’s RAPS).
In this case, you can just show up to the makeup the following week (no need to contact us in
advance). If you took the regularly scheduled test, you may not take the corresponding makeup.
Note that there may be one or two FAs that do not have an associated makeup. If you happen
to miss that FA you will not have a makeup for that test, though you will still have multiple
opportunities to try those problems on subsequent FAs. See schedule later in the syllabus for
precise details.
• Regrade Requests: We are all human and sometimes make mistakes, so please check your test
and submit a regrade request on Gradescope if, e.g, you forgot to select a page or selected the
wrongpage; youthinkthegradermighthavemissedsomethingorclickedthewrongrubricbutton;
or if you don’t understand why you didn’t get credit. One of our instructors will take another look
at your work and will make a final decision on whether you earned credit on that problem. If you
did not earn credit, the grader will try to provide additional feedback so you can better prepare
for the next FA. The grader’s decision on your regrade request is final. Regrade requests are
due by midnight on the Wednesday after you took the FA. Late regrades requests will
not be considered; you can always try that problem on the next FA.
• Final: We will hold a final during the registrar’s scheduled final exam period. The exact date
and time is determined by the registrar after the add/drop period. Please do not make end-of-
semester travel plans until you know the final exam schedule.
Our final will consist of one additional FA. (It is not a traditional comprehensive final.)
The final is the end of the semester. There is no extra credit or additional work after the final. All
homework and participation assignments must be completed by the last deadline for your section
but certainly before the final.
Homework and Participation:
These are the last two General Outcomes. They are the only Outcomes that are not tested on FAs.
Instead, these are cumulative throughout the semester. You earn points for each assignment and these
are all added together (not averaged). You have to meet a certain minimum score to earn the point for
each of these two Outcomes.
• Homework (General Outcome #31):
You will earn the point for this Outcome if you accumulate at least 200 HW points.
There will be 25 homework assignments. Each assignment is worth 15 points: 5 points for com-
pleting the assignment and 10 points for accuracy (we will grade 5 of the questions, each worth 2
points). There is a total of 375 possible points; the threshold of 200 is a little more than half of
the total possible points.
Late HW Policy: If you submit your homework late (after the grader grades that set), you can
still earn the completion points but not the accuracy points for the particular problems (i.e., you
can earn at most 5 points out of 15 for that assignment).
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
• Participation (General Outcome #32):
You will earn the point for this Outcome if you accumulate at least 30 Participation points.
Your participation for this class will be taking attendance during the class. You can earn up to 3
point during each week’s class on 13 weeks, for a total of 50 possible Participation points.

doc_id: 243POL 119A 1 Seminar Red States, Blue States Understanding Contemporary American Voters and Parties Lucy Goodhart
course_name: 243POL 119A 1 Seminar Red States, Blue States Understanding Contemporary American Voters and Parties Lucy Goodhart

spans.text:
Professor Lucy Goodhart Fall 2024
Location: Rabb Graduate Center, 85-355 Room: Olin-Sang 124
Phone: (781) 736-2742 Time: Mon/Weds, 2:30 – 3:50 pm
Email: lgoodhar@brandeis.edu Office Hours: Tues 1- 3 pm
POL119: RESEARCH SEMINAR
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
RED STATES, BLUE STATES: AMERICAN VOTERS AND PARTIES
Learning Goals: The extraordinary drama of the current presidential campaign, and new strains in
American politics, have many scratching their heads. The core goal of this seminar is to become more
knowledgeable about voting and party behavior in the US, so that we can come understand what drives
growing partisanship, increasing polarization, the emergence of populism and the politics of identity.
There are two intellectual aspirations for the course that follow from this. The first goal is to read, and
critically appraise, selections from the scholarly literature on voters and parties so that we understand the
root causes of contemporary political developments. We start with the behavioral and “spatial” analysis of
voter choices and then address core theoretical results about where parties will position themselves given
voters. From there, we start on more focused material on the increasing polarization of US parties, the
realignment of party electorates and the populist issues that have contributed to party realignment. We
review this scholarship, and popular works, to critically consider why these developments should have
occurred in the US and what they mean for trust, stability, and effective democracy.
The second intellectual goal is to become familiar with the research tools that help you to conduct your own
research on these topics. Thus, the seminar includes multiple sessions on research methods. In addition,
and since your research paper will consider data, most often accessed digitally, we will also review the
steps to analyzing the validity and reliability of primary and secondary data. Based on that review, and an
associated assignment, this course satisfies the requirements for digital literacy.
The course is designed to meet the following four objectives.
1) To increase our understanding of contemporary American voter and party behavior.
2) To develop and reinforce habits of digital literacy.
3) To foster confidence and develop skills in public speaking and presentation.
4) To teach the basics of independent research in political science, with a focus on deriving
questions from theory and conducting quantitative and qualitative analysis.
Expectations: POL 119A is a Four-Credit Course (with three hours of class-time per week). Success in
the course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of nine hours of study per week
in preparation for class including readings, responses to readings, and research assignments. There are no
pre-requisites for the class, but it is very helpful to have taken a prior course in research and statistics and
almost essential to have some academic background in American Politics if you are not familiar with the
field. Because interaction for the colloquium is intensive, the class size is capped at eighteen students.
Modality: POL 119A is an in-person class. Zoom participation will be available for those who are
unable to attend because of immediate health issues, but the class will be conducted as a seminar and
Zoom may not capture class interaction well. Office hours will be in-person, but you can also request a
Zoom meeting.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Textbook: Akan Malici and Elizabeth S. Smith, 2018, Political Science Research in Practice (Second
Edition). I will set a number of chapters from this text for the methods slam assignment. All other
material for the course can be found on the class Latte site. In addition, we read many chapters from two
books written in response to Trump: Morris Fiorina’s 2017 book, Unstable Majorities: Polarization,
Party Sorting and Political Stalemate, and Alan Abramowitz’s 2018 book, The Great Alignment: Race,
Party Transformation and the Rise of Donald Trump. Relevant chapters are on Latte, but you may want
to purchase the books secondhand.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: COURSE ADMINISTRATION
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Academic Integrity: You are expected to follow the University's policies on academic integrity:
http://www.brandeis.edu/svpse/academicintegrity/. Suspected instances of dishonesty will be referred to
the Director of Academic Integrity. You may also have your assignments reviewed by Compilatio to verify
originality. During the course, you will work together to refine your empirical research, but are expected
to write up that research independently. If your work appears to have been generated, in whole or part, by
AI, I will give you one opportunity to re-write an assignment. Repeated indications of text generated by
ChatGPT or other LLM’s will be referred to the Director of Academic Integrity.

spans.text:
Missed Classes or Assignments: Please note that I do not give “extra-credit assignments.” Attendance
in class is strongly encouraged and will influence your participation grade. If you know that you cannot
attend class on a particular day please email me in advance to let me know. If you know you may be
delayed in submitting an assignment, for a given reason, please let me know in advance, via email.

carry your phone with you in 'silent' mode. You are permitted to use laptops for academic purposes. If
your screen use distracts you or others, your participation grade will be reduced and permission to use your
laptop will be withdrawn.

school: School of Arts, Humanities, and Culture
course_name: 261FA 178B 1 Seminar on Chinese Calligraphy and Practice Aida Wong

spans.text:
Hengshan Calligraphy Museum, Taiwan
FA 178b: Seminar on Chinese Calligraphy: History and Practice
Spring 2026
Mondays and Wednesdays: 2:30-3:50pm
Schwartz 103
Professor Aida Yuen Wong
Email: aida@brandeis.edu Responses can be expected in 24-48 hours, except during holidays
and weekends when a longer response time is possible.
Office: Room 212, Mandel Center for the Humanities
Office hours: Thurs 11:30am-1:30pm (or by appointment)
Zoom by appointment only: https://brandeis.zoom.us/my/aywong
Email: aida@brandeis.edu
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description and Objectives
This seminar examines the art and history of Chinese calligraphy. The main goal is to introduce
students of different Chinese-language levels (not limited to native-speakers) to canonical works
of calligraphy as well as the enthusiasm and creativity these works have inspired through the
ages. From anonymous oracle bones and stone inscriptions to famed masterpieces, such as Wang
Xizhi’s “Lanting Pavilion Preface,” and from original renderings to copies of others’
compositions, this course showcases the kaleidoscopic range that makes calligraphy a visual-
linguistic art form beyond “words.” The multifaceted functions of stylized writing—such as for
political, religious, and expressive purposes—will also be explored.
In addition to acquiring knowledge of history, students engage in hands-on study through
copying and creating calligraphy. We will discuss and reflect on calligraphic aesthetics through
the ages. Students will gain a better appreciation from concept to product. This course which
crosses between art history and studio art aims to train visual thinking and improve hand-eye
1

coordination. Students will also learn the rudiments of the main script types (seal, clerical,
running, regular, and cursive) and to produce calligraphy for evaluation.
A calligraphy supply list is provided. Students are responsible for bringing the tools to relevant
class meeting, usually on Wednesdays. A laptop will be needed occasionally for class activities.
This “oral communication” course requires two Powerpoint presentations: one on assigned
readings and the other related to the final research paper. There is also a short
presentation about the final art project.
Separate from these, each student will create an original work of calligraphy, applying the ideas
and skills learned while developing a theme that brings the text, style, and script choice together
as a coherent unit. The material, size, and format are open. The work will be presented in class
with the concept explained briefly, approximately 5 minutes. A write-up of this explanation (1-2
pages) will be due on the same day.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Success in this course requires at least nine hours of work for every three hours of class time.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Requirements
1. Attendance and Participation 25%
Active, earnest, thoughtful and prepared: 22% or up
Mostly passive but diligent and attentive: 20% or up
Little apparent sign of engagement and preparedness: below 20%
Two or more unexcused absences will significantly affect your grade; explain your
absences in writing.
2. Reading Introductions (PPT) (20 minutes each including Q&A and 1 per student; dates to
be assigned) 10%
3. Calligraphy Homework (weekly on average) 25%
4. Final Art Project: Original Work of Calligraphy (Due last day of class) 10%
5. Research Paper with Written and Oral Components 20+10=30%
Powerpoint presentation on your topic. 15 minutes. (Graded for clarity, time
management, and organization).
The written component is an elaboration, expansion, and refinement of the oral
presentation. More instructions will be given around the middle of the semester. (Graded
for research effort, presentation such as proper footnoting and illustration, and writing
style.)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
8-10 text pages, illustrations are required (not part of the text-page count), 12 pt
2

font, Times New Roman, 1” margins, double-spacing, footnotes and bibliography in
Chicago Style).

April 15: Final Art Project due (Bring to Class)
April 20, 22, 27, and 29: Oral Presentations with PPT of Final Papers (approx. 15 mins each)

spans.text:
You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. The University policy on academic
honesty is distributed annually as section 5 of the Rights and Responsibilities
handbook. Instances of alleged dishonesty will be forwarded to the Office of Campus Life for
possible referral to the Student Judicial System. Potential Sanctions include failure in the course
and suspension from the University. If you have any questions about my expectations, please
ask.

spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
About the Readings
Weekly readings are available on MOODLE (assignments are listed in the calendar below).
Before coming to class students should have already done the readings assigned for the day.
Students will be asked to discuss the main points and be ready to pose questions regarding their
contents and assumptions. Students are expected to approach the materials thoughtfully and
critically. Some of the readings are meant to provide the relevant historical background for
understanding the topic of the week, while others advance specific viewpoints. Peer assessment
will be solicited.

spans.text:
Calculus:
Calculus is one of the greatest intellectual achievements of humankind.
The beautiful idea at the heart of this subject allows us to explore both
the infinite and the infinitesimal. This gives us the tools to model and then
analyze phenomena that change, i.e. pretty much anything!
Learning Goals for Math 10b:
1. Identify and apply key ideas and skills of Integral Calculus. This will
be done by mastering the Outcomes detailed on the Moodle page. See
more on “Mastery Grading” below.
Figure1: TheFourWords
2. Transfer familiar concepts to unfamiliar contexts. at the Heart of Calculus
(link)
3. Hone your problem solving skills.
4. Develop and leverage a learning community for math.
Prerequisite:
Math 10a, equivalent credit, or a satisfactory score on the math depart-
ment’s calculus placement self-test:
https://communities.brandeis.edu/course/view.php?id=5563
We want you to take the math class that is the best fit for you. We are happy to help you figure out
which class that is. Please contact the course coordinator, Becci Torrey (rtorrey@brandeis.edu), if you
have any questions

spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Mastery Grading
What is Mastery Grading?
Mastery Grading is a grading structure that is designed to maxi-
mize student learning. It rewards good study habits like hard work,
persistence, repeated practice, review and reflection.
Here’s how it works:
OurcoursehasalistofContent Outcomes–thesearemathemat-
ical facts and skills that comprise the content we are learning in this
class. (Content outcomes include things like “Evaluate trig functions
of special angles.” and “Solve a word problem that involves a log scale.” – see the end of this syllabus
for a complete list.)
Our Friday Assessments give you the opportunity to demonstrate mastery of the content we have
coveredsofarinthecourse. Problemswillbegradedonacredit/no-creditbasis. Yourworkwillbeheld
to a high standard: demonstrating clear mastery of the material as determined by the grader. If you
demonstrate mastery on a particular outcome you no longer need to do those problems on subsequent
FAs.
Re-dos: If you do not earn credit on a particular outcome, it does not count against you. But this
means you need to review/study/practice that concept or skill further so you’re ready to try again on
the next FA.
This structure is known as “high-frequency, low-stakes” assessment: we have a high frequency
of tests (8 total) but they are low stakes because getting something wrong doesn’t count against you.
Withthisgradingstructure, youcanbeconfidentateverypointinthesemesterthatyouhavestrong
skills in all the outcomes you mastered, and you will know exactly which skills and ideas need more
work.
Why do we like it?
There are so many reasons why we like this system better than a traditional grading structure. Here
are just a few:
1. Everyone can do well. There’s no curve, so there’s no limit on how many students can get A’s.
Our goal is for everyone to learn the material and succeed in the class. We believe you can do it
and we designed this course to help you succeed.
2. It avoids the “bad day” catastrophe. In a traditional low-frequency, high-stakes testing system,
one bad day can tank your entire grade. In this system, you can make up for a bad day next time.
3. Studentscanlearnatdifferentpacesandstillsucceed. Ifyou’restrugglingwithaparticularconcept
and don’t get it right away but keep working on it, you’re rewarded for learning it eventually just
the same as someone who learned it immediately. So keep working – persistence is rewarded.
4. Highstandards: thecredit/no-creditgradingmeansyouknowyouarebeingheldtohighstandards
and can (and should!) be really proud of each and every credit you earn.
5. Unveiling of hidden expectations: our Practice Assessments can help you prepare directly for each
Outcome even before they are first released but it is always possible that you may discover other
expectations when first encountering a skill in a test environment. The re-do structure allows you
to learn from one test and apply those lessons to the next test to demonstrate your newfound
learning.
6. Clarity about learning: In this system, you have a detailed list of all the things you will learn
throughoutthecourse. AsyoumasterdifferentOutcomes, youhaveaclearunderstandingofwhat
you have already learned and what you still need to work on.
7. This structure rewards good study habits, like regular practice, review of past work, and a focus
on full understanding of the material.
2
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
How to succeed in this course:
This structure may be new to you. Succeeding may
require you to adjust your study habits. Since this
structure rewards good study habits, we hope you
will reflect on your approach and adjust as neces-
sary. This should help you to build better study
habits that you can apply to future classes as well.
Here are some tips to get off to a great start:
1. Prepare for class. Read the sections in the book before class. Take notes, try problems and ask
questions. Being prepared for class will help you to get the most out of our precious class time.
2. Attend class. This is essential. All learning is social and this is your primary opportunity to
meet classmates with whom you can discuss the material both in and out of class. It is also your
best opportunity to hear how the expert (your teacher) presents the material, ask questions in
real time, set a routine, and keep up with the class.
3. Do your homework. Mathematical intuition is not inborn; you have to develop it. Homework
is a great way to do this. These assignments are designed to help you build that intuition. All
assignments have a series of easier problems that build up to harder problems so that you can
recognize patterns and deeply ingrain your learning. The assignments also include challenging
problems and problems that approach the material from different ways, so this is a great way to
prepare for the Challenge Outcomes.
4. Attend RAPS (Review, Advising and Preparation Stations). These will take place on
the Fridays when we don’t have FAs. Review your past work, compare to our provided solutions,
talk to your classmates and our staff to make sure you fully understand the concepts and the
techniques. Then practice, practice, practice to ingrain that learning.
5. Talk to people. There is compelling research showing that students who form social groups
around academics are much more successful in college math classes than those who work alone.
You will have opportunities to meet and talk to other students in class and RAPS, as well as in
tutoring and your instructor’s office hours. Utilize all these resources and form your own study
groups.
6. Utilize office hours and tutoring. Upper class students and alum regularly regret not making
better use of resources like these. Add them to your calendar and attend regularly, even if you
don’t have questions. These are great places to work on homework: they eliminate distractions
so you can focus and there’s an expert on hand if you get stuck or have questions. Check your
Brandeis Moodle page for details on instructor office hours.
7. Utilize Practice Assessments. These provide sample practice problems, grading rubric infor-
mation, and additional resources (usually videos and more practice problems) on the last page.
8. Review and reflect. Reflection is the foundation of learning. Reviewing past mistakes and
understandingwhatwentwrongisthekeytocorrectinganymisunderstandings, avoidingcommon
mistakes, and meeting expectations. We provide solutions to all FAs so you can compare your
work to our solutions

We will cover the following sections from our textbook this semester:

2.2 Determining Volumes by Slicing

5.4 Comparison Tests

6.4 Working with Taylor Series

rey@brandeis.edu) or Academic Services (https://www.brandeis.edu/academic-services/index.

Outcomes (G’s) score in the chart below. See the last page of this syllabus for a full list of Outcomes.
(There are also three General Outcomes that are cumulative: Homework, Participation and Practice
Assessments.)

Minimums: You have to get both the Fundamental and Total Outcomes scores for any given grade.
In particular, if you have 8 points on the Fundamental Outcomes, the highest grade you can earn is a

Failure to meet any of the D-range standards will result in an E.

Makeups for FAs: If you miss a particular FA, you may take the makeup for that FA, which
will be held during the Friday recitation period following the regularly scheduled test (i.e., during
RAPS the following week). In this case, you can just show up to the makeup the following week
(no need to contact us in advance). If you took the regularly scheduled test, you may not take
the corresponding makeup. You may take a maximum of 3 makeup tests during the semester.
(So don’t do this lightly; if you need to miss an FA because you’re sick, you’ll want to have this
option available.) See schedule later in the syllabus for precise details.

spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Regrade Requests: We are all human and sometimes make mistakes, so please check your test
and submit a regrade request on Gradescope if, e.g, you forgot to select a page or selected the
wrongpage; youthinkthegradermighthavemissedsomethingorclickedthewrongrubricbutton;
or if you don’t understand why you didn’t get credit. One of our instructors will take another look
at your work and will make a final decision on whether you earned credit on that problem. If you
did not earn credit, the grader will try to provide additional feedback so you can better prepare
for the next FA. The grader’s decision on your regrade request is final. Regrade requests are
due by midnight on the Wednesday after you took the FA. Late regrades requests will
not be considered; you can always try that problem on the next FA.

Final: We will hold a final during the registrar’s scheduled final exam period. The exact date
and time is determined by the registrar after the add/drop period. Please do not make end-of-
semester travel plans until you know the final exam schedule.
Our final will consist of one additional FA. (It is not a traditional comprehensive final.)
The final is the end of the semester. There is no extra credit or additional work after the final. All
homework and participation assignments must be completed by the last deadline for your section
but certainly before the final

spans.text:
Friday Assessments
• Closed Book, No Calculators, Provided Formula Sheet: TheFAsareclosedbook,meaning
that no notes, books, or internet-connected devices are allowed during the FA. You are also not
allowed to use a calculator. However, we will include a formula sheet with some basic information
to help alleviate the burden of memorization and mistakes due to anxiety-induced blanking out.
You will have the formula sheet ahead of time so you know what is on it
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Homework, Participation, and Practice Assessments:
These are the last three General Outcomes. They are the only Outcomes that are not tested on FAs.
Instead, these are cumulative throughout the semester. You earn points for each assignment and these
are all added together (not averaged). You have to meet a certain minimum score to earn the point for
each of these two Outcomes.
• Homework (General Outcome #31):
You will earn the point for this Outcome if you accumulate at least 200 HW points.
There will be 25 homework assignments. Each assignment is worth 15 points: 5 points for com-
pleting the assignment and 10 points for accuracy (we will grade 5 of the questions, each worth 2
points). There is a total of 375 possible points; the threshold of 200 is a little more than half of
the total possible points

still earn the completion points but not the accuracy points for the particular problems (i.e., you
can earn at most 5 points out of 15 for that assignment).

Resources to Help you Succeed
Study Hours: Thursdays 2:30-3:30 PM or by appointment in Goldsmith 102
This is time that I have set aside explicitly to help you do the homework, review course material,
and prepare for your Friday Assessments. Please make use of this resource! You don’t have to have
questions prepared; you can just show up. If the above timing does not work (or if you need office hours
to be on Zoom), please email me to set up another meeting time.
Practice Assessments:
We have Practice Assessments for each Content Outcome to help you prepare for the Friday As-
sessments. Complete solutions are provided and many files also have helpful videos and/or links to
additional problems. These practice assessments are optional (but strongly recommended!) and are for
your use only. They have no effect on your grade.
Past FAs and Solutions:
Past FAs and Solutions will be posted here so you can compare your work with our solutions.
Review, Advising and Preparation Stations (RAPS): Staff will be on hand to help you review
your last FA, prepare for the next one and develop optimal study strategies. We’re here to help you
identify mistakes, correct misunderstandings, and make a plan to prepare for the next FA.
Tutoring:
The department provides some drop-in tutoring at no cost. The tutors are math graduate students
or advanced undergrads. Please take advantage of this excellent resource!
Other Course Information
Calculators:
Calculators are not allowed during quizzes or Friday Assessments. You should have access to a
scientific calculator for homework (you can use a free online one), but you do not need a graphing
calculator

Academic Honesty:
You are expected to be familiar with, and to follow, the University’s policies on academic integrity.
You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis University Rights
and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Allegations of alleged
academic dishonesty will be forwarded to Student Rights and Community Standards. Sanctions for
academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension from the university. Citation and
research assistance can be found on the university library website

Accommodations:
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of
all students, and I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require
disability accommodations, you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support
(SAS). You can contact them at 781-736-3470, email them at access@brandeis.edu, or
visit the Student Accessibility Support home page. You can find helpful student FAQs
and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you
might be eligible for support from SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as
you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class. In order to
coordinate exam accommodations, ideally you should provide the accommodation letter at least 48
hours before an exam
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Expectations for student contributions to coursework:
Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will contribute at least 9
hours of study time per week in preparation for class.
Timely communication:
Use your Brandeis email to reach out to me. I am usually able to respond quickly to most messages,
within 24 hours, although during the weekends and over holidays it could take me longer. If I reach
out to you, with a query or comment or in response to an email from you, I would appreciate it if you
would acknowledge receipt of my message and/or respond with 24 hours, unless it’s during weekend or
over a holiday. Note that we will use your Brandeis email address, so you need to check it regularly.
All course announcements can be found in the Course News & Announcements page on Moodle.
Available Resources:
Many resources are available to help with the academic and non-academic factors that contribute to
student success (finances, health, food supply, housing, mental health counseling, academic advising,
physical and social activities, etc.). Please explore the links on the Support at Brandeis to find out
more about the resources that the University provides to help you and your classmates achieve success.
Name/Pronouns:
If your name and/or pronouns differ from those in your official Brandeis records, please let me know.
Thanks!

spans.text:
Friday Schedule for the Semester:
Our Friday meetings will alternate between:
• Friday Assessments (FAs): Opportunities to demonstrate mastery on material covered so far
in the course, and
• Review, Advising and Preparation Stations (RAPS): Staff will be on hand to help you
review your last FA, prepare for the next one and develop optimal study strategies.
Makeups for FAs: If you need to miss an FA, you can take a makeup on the following Friday (but
you have to miss the RAPS session). Note that:
1. You can take a maximum of 3 makeups for the semester. (So don’t do this lightly; if you need to
miss an FA because you’re sick, you’ll want to have this option available.)
2. You cannot take a makeup if you took the regular FA.
3. There is no makeup for the makeup: if you miss the regular FA and its corresponding makeup,
you just reset and move on to the next FA.
4. Notes on particular FAs/Makeups:
• There is no makeup for FA #1. If you miss FA #1, you’ll just start on FA #2.
• The makeup for FA #7 is on the Study Day (Thursday, Apr 30).
• FA #3 is the Friday before February break.
• FA #6 is the Friday at the end of Passover break (first day back after that break).
