doc_id: 243JOUR 114B 1 Arts Journalism, Pop Culture, and Digital Innovation Josh Wolk
course_name: 243JOUR 114B 1 Arts Journalism, Pop Culture, and Digital Innovation Josh Wolk

spans.text:
JOURN 114B: Arts Journalism, Pop Culture, and Digital Innovation
Monday/Wednesday: 2:30-3:50 p.m.
Classroom: Brown 316 (note change! Not Schwartz)
Professor Josh Wolk: joshwolk@brandeis.edu
Office: Brown 315
Josh Wolk’s Office Hours: Tuesdays, 10am-12pm, 1:30-2:30pm
(sign up for a slot here or email to arrange an appointment on another day if necessary)
NOTE: Every student must meet with me at Office Hours at least once by Sept. 24
Purpose
To learn how to report and offer smart commentary on all things entertainment and fine arts, and
explore new ways to do so
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description
The entertainment beat has exploded in the digital age of pop-culture fandom. In the 1950s pop
culture was the ugly stepchild of arts coverage, with a giant chasm between the scholarly
reviews and interviews of newspapers and the gossipy fluff of movie magazines. But today the
internet is overstuffed with TV recaps, Q&As, and hot takes on the smallest tidbit of Hollywood
news. But with so many writers blogging about the same topics big and small, how do you
create smart, compelling content that gives readers/viewers/listeners something valuable,
informative, and distinctive? In this course, we’ll look at the evolution of entertainment
journalism in all its forms, from profiles to reviews to essays, and discuss how to evolve it even
further for an inundated and yet ever-hungry audience of savvy film/TV/music obsessives. In
this intensive course, you will write profiles of artists, entertainment analysis, and criticism,
honing your voice while always buttressing it with insight, nuance, curiosity, perspective, and
context. We’ll explore how to use digital multimedia opportunities to create new, surprising
avenues of coverage. And we’ll be visited by accomplished feature writers, TV beat reporters
and critics — both veterans who have seen the entertainment journalism change, and new
writers who have evolved the concept of the celebrity profile and reviews.
Learning Objectives
After this course you should be able to:

● Understand how entertainment journalism has changed over the years, especially in the
recent digital age
● Become a more discerning consumer and creator of culture coverage, able to notice the
differences between: aggregation and original reporting; spin and objectivity; and
hypothesizing and adhering to facts
● Write reviews, recaps, and analysis that balance your opinions and voice with insight
and deep knowledge of your subject
● Write a feature that captures a trend, movement, culture, or group, and incorporates a
multimedia angle that expands it from just a text piece
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Attendance, Preparation, and Communication
Attendance is mandatory and part of your final grade, as is handing your work in on time.
Consider this class a newsroom, and a journalist must be able to: comprehensively gather facts
(hence: come to class); listen to their editor (in this case: me); and meet deadlines
(assignments). You are allowed 4 absences this semester, and I ask that you let me know as
soon as possible when you anticipate not being present, and you’re expected to catch up on
what you missed. The best way to reach me is email.
You are expected to arrive at each session fully prepared, having completed the day’s readings
and assignments and formulated thoughts on each one. Our classes will be filled with
discussions, and everyone should be involved (hence “participation” being a major part of your
grade; see below). We will also have many guests, and you must read the selections of their
work (which will be posted in Moodle) in advance: I will ask everyone to submit five questions for
each guest by 5pm the day before their appearance.
And in this seminar, mutual respect is imperative. We will be learning the importance of
grounding arguments in facts, which will involve discussions of our own opinions. I expect
students to be engaged, thoughtful, and constructively critical, but also respectful of each
other’s feelings and diverging opinions. The same goes when we workshop ideas or drafts.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Office Hours
Office Hours are Tuesdays, from 10am-12pm, and 1:30-2:30 in Brown 302A. Use the sign-up
sheet to reserve a 20-minute slot. Everyone in the class is required to meet with me at least
once by Oct. 1.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Materials and Resources
Reading assignments are in the syllabus, but there may be changes during the semester if an
interesting and more relevant piece arises. I will put any changes or additions through Class
Announcements, which will go straight to your email.

You should generally keep up with an array of arts and entertainment sites like Vulture, EW.com,
RollingStone.com, and HollywoodReporter.com, as well as arts sections in any number of
newspapers and through social media accounts. And of course, take mental note of everything
you watch, listen to, or read: We’ll be discussing all of our culture habits, so I will want to hear
what you’re into, good and bad!
Note: You can get a subscription to the New York Times and the Wall Street Journal through
Brandeis.
Click on Create your (free) NYTimes.com account to open the resource.
Click on Create your free Wall Street Journal account to open the resource.
Other important resources:
Your writing should all follow the rules of the A.P. Stylebook
Here is the Guide to research databases for journalism students, from Brandeis Library
Read the Code of Ethics from the Society of Professional Journalists
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Homework Submission\nEmail all assignments to me via either Word doc or Google doc. Please title each doc:\nLASTNAME.ASSIGNMENT (i.e. “Wolk.Q&A”)
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Deadlines
Journalism is all about meeting deadlines! Hitting your due dates is critical in this class. If you
have an emergency that may cause you to be late on an assignment, alert me in advance and
we can discuss an extension. But lateness without giving me a heads-up for extenuating
circumstances will cause your grade to drop a half-grade for every day past the deadline.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Plagiarism and AI
Plagiarism is strictly prohibited in this class. Work is expected to be entirely your own, and any
evidence of plagiarism will result in an automatic failure for the assignment. As for AI: As we will
discuss in class, AI can be a useful tool in information gathering for journalists, but it can also be
disastrous if you let it take over your writing. More than that, however, this class will make you a
better writer only if you actually write, rather than cede the task to a computer. Any use of AI
writing will also result in an F.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Assignment Length
In journalism, editors assign articles with a word count (500 words; 1500 words, etc.). If you
want to see smoke pour out of an editor’s ears, watch a journalist file something at double the
assigned word count and say, “I simply can’t find anything to cut! It’s all necessary!” I will assign
homework the same way, and you must go no more than 20% over the assigned word count. If
you do, your grade will go down one half-level. To be a journalist is to have to be ruthless about
what you keep in your article, and what you cut.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Privacy
Our guests will be talking candidly, both about themselves and stories they’ve covered. These
visits shall be considered off the record, and you will not record, write, or otherwise publicly
share their words outside of class. And this same respect should be given to your classmates.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Grades
Semester grades will be determined based on a combination of:
20 percent: Class participation (attendance, contribution to discussions, deep familiarity with
assigned reading, work on in-class exercises)
20 percent: Reflections, questions, and other short items that don’t involve reporting. Basically
the assignments that are not listed in the next category
35 percent: Reported assignments: 300-word aggregation piece, Q&A, scene, review, opinion
piece. Note: Pitches for these assignments will be counted as part of the same assignment
Note: You always have the opportunity to rewrite a reported assignment for a better
grade. When looking at a rewrite, I will always read with an eye to whether you gave thought to
and applied my feedback.
25 percent: Quality and execution of your final project, a 1,200-word feature about a
wide-spanning topic
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Academic Honesty
You are expected to be familiar with, and to follow, the University’s policies on academic
integrity. You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis

University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic
integrity. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to Student Rights and
Community Standards. Sanctions for academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or
suspension from the university. Citation and research assistance can be found on the university
library website.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students,
and I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability
accommodations, you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can
contact them at 781-736-3470, email them at access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student
Accessibility Support home page. You can find helpful student FAQs and other resources on the
SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you might be eligible for support from
SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon
as you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class. In
order to coordinate exam accommodations, ideally you should provide the accommodation letter
at least 48 hours before an exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Expectations for student contributions to coursework
Each course credit carries an expectation that in order to be successful a student needs to
engage in approximately 45 hours of combined in-class and out-of-class work and preparation,
where “out-of-class work” comprises readings, papers, discussion sections, problem sets, etc.
Based on this rule, instructors of 4-credit semester-long courses can expect up to 9 hours of
study time per week.
● Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will
contribute at least 9 hours of study time per week in preparation for class.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Course Plan
Note: Guests’ schedules may change, and some listed as “TBD” still need to be
confirmed, so some of the appearances listed below may be reshuffled.
Also, you will see some readings listed as “TBA.” They will be added throughout
the semester, and you will be alerted in plenty of time.

Wed, Aug. 28: Introductions/The Evolution of Journalism
We’ll start with a big-picture look at how entertainment coverage has evolved over the decades,
from the early days of image-buffing fluffy movie magazines to today’s voluminous delivery of
online coverage (from deep-dive to snark to puff) that can suit every pop-culture obsession.
We’ll also share our own cultural interests, preference, and reading/watching destinations for
entertainment journalism and criticism, and go over the arc of the class.
Homework:
1. Send me one thing in pop-culture that you love (movie, TV show, album, book,
play, podcast…). It could be old, it could be new—it just has to be something
you’re passionate about. It’s the kind of thing where, when you meet someone
who also loves it, you instantly know you will be good friends. (Put aside any
self-censoring worries of looking “classy”: Your pick can be high or low culture,
just as long as you adore it.) Come prepared to tell the class what or who you
love and why you love it/them, doing so in a way that would intrigue someone
who has never heard of it/them, and make them want to sample it/them. And be
prepared to do so in no more than 1 minute.
Email me the name of your pop-culture object by Tuesday, Aug. 27 at 9am.
(No need to send me anything in writing: Just the name/title in advance.) Also,
please send me the phonetic pronunciation of your name.
(Side note: While you don’t have to talk about something obscure, try to steer
clear of something that is a completely mainstream smash adored by billions of
people, like Taylor Swift or Deadpool.)
2. Email me two sources (website, social media account, etc.) that you look to for
your arts/entertainment news, but for different reasons: Maybe they have
different attitudes, POVs, or approaches. What’s important is that they aren’t
alike.
For each source, write one paragraph (150-200 words) explaining why you
read/watch it, and how you would define its approach. Due Tuesday, Sept. 3 at
9am
3. Read:
a. Hutchinson, Pamela, “Photoplay Magazine: The Birth of Celebrity
Culture,” The Guardian, Jan. 26, 2016
b. “People Magazine,” Encyclopedia.com
c. “Britney Spears and the generational shift in celebrity coverage,” Nieman
Lab, May 29, 2024

Mon, Sept. 2: NO CLASS
Wed, Sept. 4: The different types of arts coverage
Twenty years ago, there was a clear line between entertainment news and reviews: The critics
got to opine, but nobody else. Today, blogs often mix and match the two, with snark or gushy
cheerleading infusing blogs’ news coverage, and writers wear their fandoms on their sleeves. In
this class we’ll survey all forms of arts coverage and see how and why they evolved.
Homework:
● Read the following two news articles, and then look at the Google search results
for the topic to see how many sites aggregated the same news:
○ “Hayley Williams Dedicates Song to Edward Cullen,” Vulture, Aug. 17,
2024
■ Search results for “Hayley Williams Robert Pattinson song”
○ “Taylor Swift Meets with 2 Southport Stabbing Victims at ‘Eras Tour,’”
TMZ, Aug. 19, 2024
■ Search results for “Taylor Swift Southport Victims”
● Compare four of the sites that aggregated the same stories. Write a 300-word
analysis observing how some of them differ…or don’t. Are there some that seem
to do more original writing? Some that seem like they are just aggregation only?
(due Mon, Sept. 9 at 9am)
Mon., Sept. 9: Blogging
A brief history lesson of blogging, and how this writing embraced the “I” in the age of the
internet. While blogs like EW.com have some original interviews, reviews and features, the bulk
of their feed is aggregation, which is the rewriting of news that has first been reported
elsewhere. If you Google the subject of a news item, you’ll get pages and pages of results from
countless other outlets that re-reported the same thing. We’ll talk about why sites do this, the
best way to do it…and whether they should.
Homework: Current blog items will be posted closer to class
Wed, Sept. 11: Blogger guest: Vulture’s Jason Frank (Brandeis ‘22)
Jason took this class in Fall ‘21, after which he became a blogger for Vulture. He will talk about
the pace of blogging, how he picks what to cover, and how the voice of an outlet dictates what
he writes about and how. We’ll also discuss how bloggers supplement the news to add value
while aggregating what’s already been reported.

Homework:
● Find a piece of fresh news, and aggregate it from its original source. (Don’t
aggregate aggregation!) Write a 300-word news piece that cites the source
correctly, and adds a little something to the news so it’s not just a straight copy.
(Due Mon, Sept. 16 at 9am)
● For our upcoming Interview unit, read the following David Marchese interviews.
Pick two questions from each and explain why you think they were good or bad
(due Mon, Sept. 16 at 9am)
○ “Elizabeth Banks Thinks This Interview Is Dangerous for Her,” NY Times,
Sept. 25, 2022
○ “Grief Broke Marlon Wayans. Comedy Put Him Back Together,” NY
Times, May 4, 2024
Mon, Sept. 16: The art of the interview
How do the best interviewers engage a subject and get their guard down? We’ll talk about the
key to an effective interview: preparation, listening, and follow-ups.
Homework: Read profiles by Karen Valby, our guest on Wednesday.
- TKTKTK
- Submit five questions or Karen by Wed at 9am
Wed, Sept. 18: Guest: Karen Valby
Karen Valby has written cover stories and profiles for Vanity Fair, Fast Company, and
Entertainment Weekly, and is one of the most disarming interviewers I’ve ever met. She’ll join us
by Zoom to discuss how she gets her subjects’ guards down, and her approach to the
interviewing process.
Homework:
1. Think about someone in the arts that you’d like to do a Q&A with. Make sure they
are attainable, but not someone who you know personally. You’ll be pitching
them next week, so definitely start thinking early.
2. Read the following profiles and pick two moments from each where you feel
like the writer showed you something interesting about the subject. Copy the
quote/text that you liked, and then write 2-3 sentences on each explaining why
they caught your eye. (due Mon, Sept. 23 at 9am)
a. “Justin Bieber Would Like to Reintroduce Himself,” Caity Weaver, GQ,
Feb. 11, 2016
b. “Brad Pitt Opens Up His Dream World,” Ottessa Moshfegh, GQ.com,
June 22, 2022
Mon, Sept. 23: Interviews: Gushy vs. probing

Arts journalism is not known for being hard-hitting. But even by those standards, there are
softballs and there are the softest balls. Today, celebrities gravitate toward safe spaces for the
most friendly interviews, and there are podcasts galore in which stars talk to other stars in the
most sympathetic ways. We’ll compare two features (one fawning, one not) and consider: Do we
really want probing interviews of stars?
Homework:
1. Send a 150-word pitch for your Q&A subject. Let me know who your subject is,
and why they’re interesting. (Due Wed, Sept. 25 at 9am)
2. Read:
a. “Rob Lowe Explains the Origin of ‘Literally,’ His Parks & Recreation
Catchphrase,” Vulture, Sept. 11, 2012
b. “Sam Richardson Answers All of Our Questions About ‘Veep,’” Vulture,
Mar. 22, 2022
Wed, Sept. 25: The micro interview
Often today artists are interviewed to talk about one thing and one thing only: It could be to
reminisce about a single old TV episode they were in, or an iconic photo that was once taken of
them. What makes these zoomed-in interviews so interesting and popular?
Homework:
1. Do your Q&A and submit it at 500 words, not including a 75-word intro. (Due
Wed, Oct. 2 at 9am)
2. For Monday’s class, read the following two profiles and cite two details the writers
describe that got your attention (details, not quotes). Write 1-2 sentences on
each as to how they affected your view of the profile subject. (due Mon, Sept. 30
at 9am)
a. “Regular, Degular, Shmegular Girl from the Bronx,” Allison P. Davis, The
Cut, Nov. 13, 2017
b. “Nick Nolte Has a Drawer Full of Torniquets,” Daniel Voll, Esquire, Oct.
1999
Mon, Sept. 30: Capturing “color” of a scene
In a profile and feature, you don’t just want to print the words of your subject(s), you want your
reader to “see” them and get a sense of how they act, work, and generally behave. A reporter
shouldn’t just be taking notes on quotes, they should also be jotting down behavior and
environment. We’ll talk about developing a reporter’s eye for this kind of detail.
Homework:

1. Read the following profiles. Think about the difference between observing
someone in person vs. on Zoom
a. “The Dog-Bite Lawyer Turned Stay-at-Home Mom Turned Stand-Up
Comedian,” New Yorker, July 22, 2024
b. “Rae of Light,” Vanity Fair, June 2021
Wed, Oct. 2: Observation time
We’ll break into groups and spread out to various campus sites, observing and collecting telling
details that would be great for a story.
Homework:
1. Read the following reviews written by Wednesday’s guest, TBD
2. Observe a scene and write a 500-word “story” about it that captures what you
observed. No interviews: Just you painting a written picture of what you saw as a
fly on the wall. Capture behavior, details of the environment, snippets of
conversation. (due Wed, Oct. 9)
Mon, Oct. 7: Reviewing (Everyone’s a critic)
We’ll look at how film criticism evolved from a low-class beat, until Pauline Kael and her peers
gave film reviews respectability. We’ll also look at how the line between journalist and
critic—once a hard barrier—has become porous, and whether that hurts a writer. And we’ll talk
about what makes a good review, and the art of the pan: While it’s easier to mock things, what is
helpful to your reader vs. gratuitous mockery?
Homework:
1. Make sure you’ve finished the reviews by Wed’s TBD guest
2. Submit five questions for TBD by Tuesday night at 6pm
Wed, Oct. 9: Guest Critic: TBD
We will be visited by a critic, who will discuss how they approach something they have to
review: Do they go in cold, or learn all they can about it? Do they prefer to review people they
already like? Is it a problem if they know they hate someone’s past work and have to review
them? And how do they evaluate an artist or piece of art?
Homework:
1. Listen to the following podcast episodes, especially the one by our Oct. 21 guest,
Brian Raftery
a. “The Hollywood Hack, Episode 1: The World Will Be Full of Fear,” by
Brian Raftery for The Ringer

b. “60 Songs That Explain the ‘90s: ‘Hard Knock Life,’” The Ringer
c. “Still Processing: Plastic Off the Sofa”
2. Send me five questions for Brian by Tues, Oct. 15 at noon
3. Write a 500-word review of a single thing. (as in, an album but not a singer’s
entire catalog; a book but not an author’s collected works; a TV show this
season, not its entire run. (due Mon, Oct. 21 at 9am)
Oct. 14 and 16: NO CLASS
Mon, Oct. 21: Podcasts as arts journalism
We’ll look at the different ways that podcasts can cover the arts. There are some podcasts that
feel like sports radio but for pop culture: A bunch of hosts argue about music or TV. Some are
the vision of one person who acts like an audio essayists. And then there are deeply reported
podcasts that are like magazine features for your ears.
Our guest will be Brian Raftery, who has made multiple podcast miniseries for the Ringer,
including how Hollywood dramatized the Vietnam war over the years. His most recent miniseries
is about the 2014 hack of Sony Pictures email.
Homework:
1. Explore the NY Times’ “100 Best Books of the 21st Century” package, looking at
all the sections and offshoots
2. “Touchstones: Wong Kar Wai’s ‘In the Mood for Love,” NewYorker.com, Sept. 1,
2023
3. “How Taylor Tomlinson Nailed Her Closing Joke,” NYTimes.com, Feb. 13, 2024
4. Submit five questions for New York Times Book Review editor Gilbert Cruz (due
Wed, Oct. 23 at 9am)
Wed, Oct. 23: Multimedia innovation, with guest Gilbert Cruz, Editor of the New York Times
Book Review
Beyond just adding video to a story, outlets have been creating new formats to help expand the
way they tell a story: More than just a flashy add-on, these designs can make the storytelling
more vivid, and better convey the writer’s ideas. Gilbert Cruz will Zoom in to talk about how the
Times thinks about multimedia storytelling.
Homework: Features will be posted later in the semester
Mon, Oct. 28: What (and who) makes a good story?

Your final major project will be to write an enterprise feature: More wide-ranging than a profile of
one single person, this will be a story that could be about a trend, or a community, or a
movement, or a scene… It will involve at least five sources and two scenes, and be 1200 words.
It should also include some sort of multimedia element: photos, video, audio, you name it. Today
we’ll discuss just what kind of stories can sustain that length, and how to find them.
Homework: Think about some topics. Submit three starter ideas and be prepared to
discuss them in small groups for feedback (submit Wed, Oct. 30 at 9am)
Wed, Oct. 30: Enterprise stories, continued
We’ll break into your groups to discuss your initial ideas, and give feedback. Often speaking
your ideas aloud can spark thoughts in others and send you in directions you didn’t expect.
Homework:
1. Incorporating your group’s feedback, submit two ideas to me by Sunday, Nov. 3
at 6pm
2. Sign up for office hours on Nov. 5 (or another time, if necessary) to discuss and
finalize your final project idea
Mon, Nov. 4: Enterprise story guest TBA
We’ll be visited by a writer who specializes in the kind of story that you’ll be writing
Homework:
1. Start working on your feature. Your first draft will be due Nov. 20
2. Opinion pieces to be read: TBD
Wed, Nov. 6: Opinion pieces
While reviews are about a single project (play, album, concert, TV show, etc.), an opinion piece
can be about a trend, or someone’s body of work, or anything else that is relevant in the
moment. However, an opinion piece is not just an excuse to start ranting: You must use the
same journalistic care that you would for a news article, because a reader noticing a major fact
that debunks your argument can render your piece irrelevant before it even gets going. An
effective opinion piece can make someone who wildly disagrees with your premise still walk
away thinking, “I gotta admit, they have a point…even though I hate the point.”
Homework:

1. Write a 300-word opinion piece on the topic of your choice. Make sure to check
your argument for holes! (Due Mon, Nov. 11 at 9am.)
2. Send me three social-media accounts you follow for entertainment/arts news,
and why you like them. (No more than two sentences on each account.) (Due
Sunday at 6pm)
Mon, Nov. 11: Social media
Social media is used by every site as insurance that their content gets out there. YouTubers and
TikTokkers are delivering news (and opinions) in short, more opinionated ways. And stars use it
to speak directly to their fans without having to worry about accidentally saying the wrong thing
to a journalist: It’s their ultimate tool for crafting the public image they want. We’ll discuss what
this means for those who cover the arts, what social-media arts journalism can do well, and
what it can’t.
Homework: Keep working on your feature
Wed, Nov. 13: How to come up with a follow-up story.
The best sites include original news, features, and thoughtful reviews. But to flesh out coverage,
the sites must also be topical, which involves not just aggregating (re-reporting) other sites’
news breaks, but thinking of timely ways to advance a worthy story. We’ll discuss the good and
bad ways in which that plays out across the internet, and how to make a re-reported story your
own.
Homework:
1. Keep working on your feature: Your first draft is due a week from today: Nov.
20
Mon, Nov. 18: Understanding the business and history
When covering an area of the arts, it’s important to know how the business works, and to have
historical perspective. When we read about streaming services cutting shows, for example, it’s
important to look at what TV was twenty years ago to see if we’re actually getting less TV than
ever. And when we report on a new pop star blowing up, we should know what trends led to
their popularity.
Our guest will be Kristen Baldwin, long-time editor and critic at Entertainment Weekly (and
EW.com), who will speak to the importance of a long view of the business.
Homework: Your first draft is due Wed, Nov. 20
Wed, Nov. 20: Is news interesting just because someone else printed it?

As we’ve discussed, if a bit of news or a juicy quote is published by one outlet, it’s usually
published by a thousand. So let’s take a step back: Should everyone rush to follow the leader?
We’ll look at the range of stories that get aggregated across the Internet and ask ourselves
whether they really deserve this attention.
Homework:
1. You’ve submitted your first draft today. Exhale!...
Mon, Nov. 25: Discussing first drafts
We’ll discuss how you’re feeling about your first drafts. What went well? What walls did you hit?
What surprised you? What else do you have to do?
Wed, Nov. 27: THANKSGIVING BREAK, NO CLASS
Mon, Dec. 2: Best and worst lists
All Taylor Swift songs: Ranked! The 10 Best Succession Episodes! The 25 Best Hate-Watch
Movies Ever! Lists have been a staple of arts coverage for decades because people love to
rank things, and people love to argue about the way other people rank things. We’ll talk about
what makes a good list, and try making one of our own.
Homework:
1. Start working on your revise of your feature (due Dec. 13)
2. Read:
a. Grady, Constance, “What Do We Do When the Art We Love Was Created
By a Monster?,” Vox, Jun 25, 2019
b. Ringwald, Molly, “What About ‘The Breakfast Club?’” The New Yorker,
April 6, 2018
c. “My Time with Kurt Cobain,” Michael Azzerad, The New Yorker, Sept. 22,
2021
Wed, Dec. 4: Separating the art from the artist
How do we cover the work of a once-heralded artist whose behavior is revealed to be
monstrous? Can we ignore their films, books, or music, even if they heavily changed the form
and influenced the modern work we still appreciate? And how do we write about “classic”
entertainment that hasn’t aged well? Can we allow for art that reflects the less evolved
behaviors and stereotypes of a past time? We’ll also look at how journalists can define a
celebrity’s narrative based off of one aspect of their lives, and never let go of it.
Homework: Keep working on your feature revise

Mon, Dec. 9: Wrap-up
We’ll discuss how things are going with your revises, and discuss if you still follow the same
entertainment sources you did at the beginning of the semester. Plus, we’ll all offer one-minute
recommendations of what the rest of the class should listen to or watch on their holiday break.
Homework: Final draft of your feature will be due Dec. 13 at noon
Assignment Submission List (Summary)
Tues, Aug. 27 at 9am: The name of one pop-culture item (podcast, movie, singer, musical, etc.)
that you want to recommend in one minute, as well as the phonetic spelling of your name
Tues, Sept. 3 at 9am: Pick two of your favorite sources for arts/entertainment news. Write one
paragraph each (150-200 words) explaining why you read/watch it, and how you would define
its approach.
Mon, Sept. 9 at 9am: A 300-word analysis of the difference between four of the websites that
aggregate the same two news tidbits
Mon, Sept. 16 at 9am: A 300-word news aggregation piece, with a headline.
Mon, Sept. 16 at 9am: Pick four quotes from the David Marchese interviews and explain why
you think they are good or bad
Wed, Sept. 18 at 9am: Five questions for guest Karen Valby
Mon, Sept. 23 at 9am: Read the Justin Bieber and Brad Pitt profiles and pick two moments
from each where you feel like the writer showed you something interesting about the subject.
Copy the quote/text that you liked, and then write 2-3 sentences on each explaining why they
caught your eye.
Wed, Sept. 25 at 9am: A 150-word pitch for your Q&A subject. Let me know who your subject
is, and why they’re interesting.
Mon, Sept. 30 at 9am: Pick four details that got your attention in the Cardi B and Nick Nolte
profiles, and write 1-2 sentences on each.
Wed, Oct. 2 at 9am: Your 500-word Q&A with a 75-word intro.
Tues, Oct. 8, at 6pm: Five questions for our TBD critic guest

Wed, Oct. 9 at 9am: Your 500-word scene

Wed, Oct. 23 at 9am: Five questions for New York Times Book Review editor Gilbert Cruz
Wed, Oct. 30 at 9am: Three starter ideas for your final feature and be prepared to discuss them
in small groups for feedback

Sun, Nov. 10 at 6pm: Two sentences each on three social-media accounts you follow,
explaining why you like them

Fri, Dec. 13 at noon: Final draft of your enterprise feature

course_name: 243LGLS 129A 1 Transitional Justice Global Justice and Societies in Transition Melissa Stimell

spans.text:
LGLS 129a: Global Justice and Societies in Transition
Legal Studies Program
Fall 2024
Instructor: Professor Melissa Stimell
Email: stimell@brandeis.edu Cell Phone: 339-206-2266
Office Hours: Tuesdays and Thursdays 12:30-2 and by appointment
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description
This course introduces the concept and practice of transitional justice, a growing
interdisciplinary field of study placed at the intersection of international law and justice, conflict
and peace studies, human rights, and politics. Broadly defined, transitional justice is a set of
practices that arise following a period of conflict that aim directly at confronting past violations
of human rights.
This course will examine various transitional justice mechanisms, including; criminal
prosecutions, truth commissions, reparations and the contributions of art and culture. The focus
will be on understanding the nature of political and moral dilemmas that countries confront when
applying these mechanisms. The course will examine broad theoretical questions, as well as
specific examples with an emphasis on practical implications. What is the role of transitional
justice in punishment, conflict resolution and forgiveness? Where should justice take
place? What is the role of art in reconciliation?
Learning Goals
This course will help students meet the following learning goals:
Knowledge: develop critical awareness of the gap between theories of transitional justice and
practical applications; distinguish political perspectives from related concerns.
Skills: increase oral and written facility in expressing arguments under political constraints;
synthesize and evaluate information from diverse sources in competing philosophies; create
models of solving challenging problems of social justice through utilizing transitional justice
mechanisms.
Justice: grasp the challenges of achieving justice, forgiveness and reconciliation through
transitional justice; test one’s own commitments to justice, forgiveness, and reconciliation
against current national and international problems; compare justice arguments over time and

place; contribute to the creation of a just society through the thoughtful application of transitional
justice.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Academic Integrity: You are expected to be honest in all your academic work. The University
policy on academic honesty is Section 5 of the Rights and Responsibilities Handbook. Instances
of alleged dishonesty will be forwarded to the Office of Campus Life for possible referral to the
Student Judicial System. Potential sanctions include failure in the course and suspension from
the University. If you have any questions about my expectations, please ask.
Academic integrity is central to the mission of educational excellence at Brandeis
University. Each student is expected to turn in work completed independently, except when
assignments specifically authorize collaborative effort. It is not acceptable to use the works or
ideas of another person-be it a world-class philosopher or your lab partner-without proper
acknowledgment of that source. This means that you must use footnotes and quotation marks to
indicate the source of any phrase, sentences, paragraphs or ideas found in published volumes, on
the internet, or created by another student.
Violations of University policies on academic integrity, described in Section 3 of Rights and
Responsibilities, may result in failure in the course or assignment, and could end in suspension
from the University. If you are in doubt about the instructions for any assignment in this course,
you must ask for clarification.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Readings
All readings and films are available on Moodle and should be accessible to all students. If you
have a problem accessing the materials, please contact me immediately.
Moodle is the Brandeis learning management system: moodle.brandeis.edu. Log in using your
UNET ID and password.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Grading
Attendance and participation are important and will valued at 15% of your final
grade. Participation may take the form of in-class discussions, forums on LATTE, in-class brief
writing assignments and the written final project critique. If you are unable to attend class, please
notify me via email.
Each of the four individual assignments throughout the semester will be valued at 15% with the
final team project valued at 25%. Please consult the assignments for further details. No late
papers will be accepted.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Course Announcements: Course announcements through Moodle and email will be used for
classroom communications; such as updates and reminders.
Meeting with Professor
If you would like to contact me, please reach out via email. I will respond within 24 hours. If it
is urgent, you may text me on my cell number. My regular office hours are Tuesdays and
Thursdays 12:30-2. If you cannot meet during those times, I am happy to make alternative
arrangements.

spans.text:
Course Outline
Session 1: Thursday Aug. 29: What is Transitional Justice and its Underlying Values Welcome!
Session 2: Tuesday Sept. 3: What is Transitional Justice Continued and Sierra Leone i – A Case
Study of Transitional Justice Mechanisms
To read:
o Guidance Note from Secretary General: United Nations Approach to
Transitional Justice
o “What is Transitional Justice?” from International Center for Transitional
Justice (ICTJ)
o 4 types of justice (retributive, distributive, restorative, procedural) and short
essay ("Current Implications") considering these in light of 2020 racial justice
movements on Beyond Intractability website
o Tuvalu chapter 1 p.20-46 in 1001 Voices on Climate Change: Everyday
Stories of Flood, Fire, Drought, and Displacement from Around the World,
2021, by Devi Lockwood.
o Ishmael Beah’s 2007 memoir A Long Way Gone: Memoirs of a Boy
Soldier. First, read the chronology of Sierra Leonean history on pp. 219-226
so you have historical context before starting the memoir. Then read Chapters
1-3 (pp. 5-25) and 12-21 (pp. 100-218).

Content Warning: The memoir contains graphic descriptions of violence and
suffering.
To watch:
o Myanmar: Rohingya Await Justice, Safe Return (2020) 2 minutes
To listen:
o A Federal Probe into Indian Boarding School Gravesites Seeks to Bring
Healing (2021) 3 minutes
o Listen to Beah’s story “The Sounds of My Village” from The Moth (15
min). (This will fill in for some of the chapters you will skip in the memoir.)
To prepare:
o Complete the survey below on which form of justice seems most important to
you in relation to the noted complex conflicts. Due by Tuesday, September
3 at noon.
o Come to class prepared to share the thoughts and feelings that led you to
select this form of justice as most appropriate.
Session 3: Thursday Sept. 5 | Sierra Leone ii – Criminal Prosecutions with a Focus on the Crime
of Recruiting Child Soldiers
To read:
o Sierra Leone Introduction Power Point
o Martha Minow on Forgiving Youth
o Benjamin Novogrodsky, After the Horror: Child Soldiers and the SCSL
To watch:
o War Don Don (1 hour, 25 minutes)
o The Child Soldier Dilemma (10 minutes)
To prepare:
Reply briefly to Session 3 discussion forum questions below by Thursday, September 5
at noon.
o What aspects of child soldiering strike you as the most harmful to
those recruited? Were there any positive aspects to Ishmael’s
experience as a soldier?

o What are your thoughts about Ishmael’s responsibilities for the
acts he committed?
o What do you imagine might be the long-term impacts of child
soldiering on Ishmael’s - or any other child soldier’s - life?
o What is Minow’s central view on extending forgiveness to youth
who commit serious crimes? How does she feel they can best
recover from their participation in violent acts?
o How does Minow’s stance compare to that of Novogrodsky when
he discusses the treatment of child soldiers by the Special Court for
Sierra Leone?
o Issa Sesay, the accused person whose trial is followed in “War Don
Don,” is accused of the recruitment of child soldiers, among other
crimes. What impression do you have of Sesay after watching the
film?
Session 4: Tuesday Sept. 10 | Sierra Leone iii – Truth and Reconciliation Commission
To read:
o Foreword and Chapter 1 of the Sierra Leone TRC Report, version for
secondary schools (p 1-17).
o The 2005 United States Institute of Peace report “Rethinking Truth and
Reconciliation Commissions: Lessons from Sierra Leone” by Rosalind Shaw
(12 pages)
o Lucinda Rouse's 2019 article “Liberia and Sierra Leone: different lessons on
how not to deliver post-war justice" in The New Humanitarian
To prepare:
Reply to Session 4 discussion forum questions below by Tuesday, September 10 at
noon.
Session 4 discussion forum
• What does the Sierra Leone TRC report claim as the accomplishments of
its process? What are the long-term impacts of the TRC's
recommendations, according to Rouse?
• What does Rosalind Shaw believe the TRC "got wrong" about how Sierra
Leoneans prefer to deal with social offenses?
Session 5: Thurday Sept. 12 | Sierra Leone iv – Local Approaches to Transitional Justice. ZOOM
Class
Guest Speaker: Joseph Kaifala
Joseph Kaifala is the inaugural winner of the Davis Projects for Peace Alumni Award for
his work in Sierra Leone.

To read:
o “Linking Justice with Reintegration? Ex-combatants and the Sierra Leone
Experiment.” in Localizing Transitional Justice: Interventions and Priorities
after Mass Violence (2010) by Rosalind Shaw and Lars Waldorf
o Scroll through the work of Joseph Kaifala
To watch:

reconciliation rituals (watch the 52 minute version)

To prepare:
Reply to Session 5 discussion forum questions below by Thursday, September 12 at

Session 5 discussion forum

violence in Sierra Leone to that of the Special Court and the Truth and
Reconciliation Commission.

Disarmament, Demobilization and Reintegration (DDR) program that was

know about his work or his experiences as a Sierra Leonean citizen before,

Session 9: Thursday Sept. 26 | Criminal Prosecutions iii-The Role of the Defense Counsel and
Building a Case for the Defense. ZOOM CLASS 2:30
Guest Speaker: Beth S. Lyons
Beth S. Lyons has been a defense counsel on two cases at the International Criminal
Court (ICC) and on three cases at the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda
(ICTR.) She was a member of the defense team on the Ongwen case at the ICC. In
February 2014, she and Lead Counsel Chief Charles A. Taku (with their team) won an
acquittal for their ICTR client, Major F.X. Nzuwonemeye, in the Prosecutor v.
Ndindiliyimana (“Military II”) case. Previously, she worked as a Legal Aid criminal
defense and appeals attorney in New York City. Since 1997, she has been an Alternate

Representative to the United Nations for the International Association of Democratic
Lawyers (IADL) and is an associate member of its Bureau
To read:
o "Making a Murderer in Uganda" by Michela Wong, Foreign Policy,
1/20/2016
o ICC case sheet on Prosecutor v. Dominic Ongwen
o Read about the role of the defense counsel at the ICC website
To watch:

case. Watch from the beginning to 9 minutes 30 seconds, and from 20
minutes until the end at 32 minutes to hear the highlights of arguments by
the Prosecution, Victims’ Counsel, and Defense, all of which reference
Ongwen’s dual status as a victim of child soldier recruitment by the Lord’s
Resistance Army, and his perpetration of that same crime - along with many
other crimes - as an adult.
o Sentencing of Ongwen (2 min 30 seconds)
o Statement by Beth Lyons on culpability of child soldiers (1 min 30 seconds)

To read:

Establishment of Hybrid Tribunals (developed in 2019)

To prepare:
Reply to session 12 discussion forum question by Thursday, October 10 at noon.

Session 12 discussion forum
• What does Kelsall means when he says there may be "cultural dissonance"
between international criminal justice and some of the sites where it is
enacted? Provide an example from one of the readings for this session.
• The ICC will only prosecute those alleged to have committed so-called
"international crimes" if a domestic jurisdiction is unable or unwilling to
do so. What might be the advantages of domestic prosecutions given the
wide range of languages and cultures that are relevant to certain cases?
Are there advantages to international prosecutions?
Session 13: Tuesday Oct. 15 | Criminal Prosecutions vi – The Role of the Prosecutor and
Building a Case for the Prosecution. ZOOM Class
Guest Speaker: Gregory Townsend
Gregory is currently a Lecturer in international law at The Hague University of Applied
Sciences. He started his legal career as a deputy public defender in Los Angeles. In 1998,
he joined the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda, where he worked for the
registrar and clerked for a judge before joining the prosecution. He later became a
prosecutor for both the U.N. peacekeeping mission in Kosovo and the ICTY, the
International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia . From 2008 to 2010 he served
as head of office for the Special Court for Sierra Leone in The Hague on the trial of
Charles Taylor. He joined the Special Tribunal for Lebanon in 2010 as chief legal adviser
to the prosecutor and helped craft the first-ever, international indictment for the crime of
terrorism against five members of Hezbollah for the assassination of Prime Minister
Rafik Hariri. In 2014, he returned to the ICTY as chief of the Registry's Court Support
Services Section. Gregory is on the list of counsel to represent victims before the
International Criminal Court and the Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of
Cambodia.
To read:
o Office of the Prosecutor- The International Criminal Court
To watch:
o Ad Hoc Tribunal Oral History Project, Video Clip with Carolina de Lima,
IVTY Victim and Witness Unit
To prepare:
Reply to Session 13 discussion forum questions below by Tuesday, October 15 at

Session 18: Tuesday Nov. 5 | Symbolic Reparations ii-Repatriation of Looted Art and Antiquities

Dr. Ashely Thompson is a professor at the School of Oriental and African Studies at the

Session 19: Thursday Nov. 7 | Symbolic Reparations iii: Contributions of Arts and Culture .
ZOOM Class

Cynthia is the former director of the program Peace Building and the Arts at Brandeis

To watch, all groups:

GROUP 1: DANCE

Session 20: Tuesday Nov. 12 | Symbolic Reparations iv-Art and Activism
Guest Speaker: Daria Pugachova
Daria Pugachova is an artist, performer and art activist born in Rivne, Ukraine. Daria
uses participatory practices to unite communities and integrate art into daily life. She
explores the themes of the transformation of place and society through the direct presence
of the artist.
To read:
Read about Daria's work below and see her Cities of War Project.
Daria Pugachova is an interdisciplinary artist and art activist from Ukraine. Her artistic
approach prioritizes the presence of the artist and direct interaction with audiences in
public spaces. Daria studied architecture at the Kyiv National University of Construction
and Architecture, and played drums for many years in the band Panivalkova.
Daria’s work has been featured in exhibitions in Ukraine, Bulgaria, Germany, Norway,
Poland, Romania, Slovenia, Switzerland, and the United States. Her latest projects are
based on her experiences of war, exploring topics of freedom and limitation, and this is
what she’ll focus on in her artist talk here.
Daria was one of five finalists for the 2024 INSPIRE Art Award of the Peace Research
Institute Oslo – selected from among artists from around the world who have responded
to the lived experience of violent conflict and/or displacement in their art, in any and all
media.
For her Cities of War project, Daria shipped a plexiglass cube from Ukraine to The
Netherlands. She set it up in front of the International Court of Justice as they were
debating whether Russia was committing genocide in Ukraine. She climbed inside the
cube, and, with a black marker, started writing (in English): “The war in Ukraine is still
going on. The war in Ukraine is still going on. The war in Ukraine is still going on.” She
did this for five hours until the clear walls of the cube were black, filled with writing.
She, inside, disappeared, just as Ukraine is disappearing from world headlines, and as
aspects of Ukrainian culture and heritage are being targeted for destruction/disappearance
by the Russian military. Plus, as she says, “The war paints [Ukrainians’] lives into
blackness.”
In our inaugural year of programming (2023-2024), Global Community Engagement
opened with the production of a play about Ukraine. We are devastated that the war there
is still going on, and honored to bring Daria Pugachova to campus at this time.
A CEC ArtsLink artist-in-residence at the Blaffer Art Museum in Houston, Texas, this

Fall, Daria will visit Brandeis upon the conclusion of that residency.
Session 21: Thursday Nov. 14 | Symbolic Reparations v: Memorials
Guest Speaker: Jose Falconi
Jose Falconi is Assistant Professor of Art and Art History at the University of
Connecticut. He is connected to the Symbolic Reparations Research Project, which
analyzes and provides policy recommendations on the role of art and aesthetic
memorialization in symbolic reparations for victims-survivors of human rights violations
in the Americas.

o Minimizing Risks of Doing Harm
o Robin Greeley, Fernando Rosenberg and others: “Repairing Symbolic
Reparations: Assessing the Effectiveness of Memorialization in the Inter-
American System of Human Rights” International Journal of Transitional

spans.text:
Welcome
History changes because the questions we ask change. When we are confronted with new or
rare occurrences, one of the first places we look is to our past. I believe that there is a lot of
explanatory power in the past, and that examining what went on before can explain where we
are now.

also supporting the Brandeis Principles of Free Speech and Free Expression. Harassment,
discrimination, disrespect, or other violations of this ethic will not be tolerated.

spans.text:
American Economic History
Course goals
By the end of this course, students will be able to:
● Discuss periods of American history, reflecting on the scale and costs involved in each
● Use the framework of American history to illuminate economic concepts
● Use the framework of economics to illuminate subjects in American history
● Apply opportunity cost, willingness to pay, and other economic concepts in empirical
situations
Skill development
Along the way, students will develop the following skills:
● Reading and understanding academic articles
● Summarizing and presenting information
● Clearly explaining research studies/results to specific audiences.
● Discussing and participating in small- and large-group discussions
Class achievement
Completing this course with a passing grade will satisfy:
● Economics major lower-level elective
● Economics major oral communication requirement
● Brandeis Diversity Equity and Inclusion in the US (DEIS-US) requirement
Class format
The class is organized into seven modules, listed at the end of this syllabus. Each module will
feature one main article and several supporting articles. The format of each module:
1. Lecture(s) on the historical period and the economic concepts covered in the main
article
2. Class discussion/activities on the articles and the historical period
3. Student presentations on the main article
4. Student presentations on supporting articles
5. Quiz on the module contents
6. Feedback on the module
Assessment
Version 1.0, 8/21/2025 2
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
American Economic History
Your performance in this course will be evaluated using the following criteria:
Quizzes (around 6) (39% overall, drop lowest 3)
At the end of each module, students will complete an in-class assessment to reinforce the
material covered during the module. These assessments should take approximately 15 minutes
to complete. The lowest 3 will be dropped. No makeup or remote quizzes will be given.
Article main idea (5%)
Students will choose one article from the class article list (or choose another article with
instructor approval). Students will read the article and email the instructor the main idea of the
article. Successfully stating the main idea on the first attempt will earn full credit. Subsequent
attempts, if necessary, carry a 2 percentage point deduction for each (minimum of 0%).
Once the student successfully states the main idea of the article, they can schedule the oral
exam.
Oral exam (25%)
Students will describe their chosen article to the instructor. The presentation will take 15
minutes, followed by instructor questions. Students can present during office hours or by
arranging a separate time with the instructor.
This presentation will demonstrate the student’s understanding of the material in the article,
including:
● The thesis/main idea of the article
● The logical argument the article makes
● The data the article uses to support its argument
● The data analysis methods used
● The results of the analysis
● The implications of the analysis
● The importance of the article’s argument
● The student’s opinion of the article’s argument
Students will have the option to choose a second article at the end of the course.
Presentation to class (26%)
After achieving a satisfactory score on the oral exam, above, students will present their chosen
article to the class in a 15-minute presentation. Students should expect that this presentation
will be completely different from their oral exam presentation. Students will earn their grade on
the presentation based on the following criteria:
● Coherent structure of the presentation
● Communication of key points of the article
● Engaging but not distracting visual presentation
● Class engagement with the presentation
Version 1.0, 8/21/2025 3

American Economic History
● Originality
Presentation policies:
● Students must present to instructor before presenting to class
● Presentations to class may be pre-recorded with permission of instructor (subject to a
3-point deduction from the verbal section of the rubric)
Students will have the option to present a second article at the end of the course.
Module feedback (5%)
At the end of each module, there will be two opportunities to provide feedback:
1. In class, after the quiz is administered
2. After class, through email to instructor or teaching assistant
Feedback will be graded on the check minus/check/check plus system. Minimal engagement
with the material from the module will earn a check plus.

spans.text:
Course Plan
Module 1: Introduction
How will this class work? What is economic history? What is America? Why are we talking so
much about race? How do I present to the class? How do I synthesize an article? What if
I forgot everything I once knew about economics?
Module 2: Pre-contact economic history
Native Americans before European contact.
Module 3: Enslavement
The institution and impact of chattel enslavement in the US.
Module 4: Race in the 20th and 21st centuries
The development, evolution, and persistence of race-based structures in the United States.
Module 5: Immigration and Anti-immigration
Arrival and life of immigrants from Europe, Asia, and Latin America and the responses to their
arrivals.
Module 6: Financial crises
Featuring the 2008 Financial Crisis and the Great Depression, with an examination of the long
history of financial panics and crises in the United States
Module 7: Topic to be announced based on student interest

spans.text:
The Resistance Mix-Tape: Music and Social Justice
University Writing Seminar (UWS) 64B
Instructor: Ethan King (he/him/his)
Email: ethanking@brandeis.edu
Office Location: Rabb Graduate Center 226
Course Time & Location :
Sec 1: MWTh | 12:20 - 1:10 PM | Olin-Sang 112
Sec 2: MWTh | 1:20 - 2:10 PM | Olin-Sang 112
Student Hours: MW 2:15 – 3:30 PM and by appointment
Course Librarian: Lisa Zeidenberg, lzeidenb@brandeis.edu
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description
All University Writing Seminars (UWS) introduce students to the power of writing as a means of
communication and a process of thinking and understanding. As students complete a series of
writing assignments, they will engage in a process of reading, drafting, reviewing, revising, and
working in peer groups and individually with their instructors.
This course considers music as a radical political tool for social justice. We will listen to
politically resonant songs in order to explore how music not only actively shapes our culture, but
also offers modes of resistance to regimes of power. Guided by different genres of music and
different social justice issues, students will examine how music fights power, encourages
activism, and effects social change. This course is designed in such a way that students may
participate fully regardless of the level of their prior musical knowledge or experience. All of the
music we will be examining involves texts that are linguistic, sonic, and, in the case of music
videos, visual. Our study of these musical texts will be enhanced through various critical and
theoretical approaches to the intersections of race, gender, and class. For final research papers,
students will be encouraged to develop a research topic about music and social justice issues that
are meaningful to them.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Four-Credit Course. Success in this 4
credit hour course is based on the
expectation that students will spend a
minimum of 9 hours of study time per
week in preparation for class
(readings, papers, discussion sections,
etc.).
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
UWS Goals and Outcomes
The University Writing Seminar introduces you to the power of writing as a means of
communication and as a process of thinking and understanding. You will formulate meaningful
arguments, support them with observations and evidence, and convey them clearly and
persuasively. You will gain the tools to be a successful and confident writer in any discipline and
become an active participant in critical conversations across the University and beyond. Most
importantly, these skills will prepare you to engage as an informed citizen in a global society.
Students will be able to:
● Understand writing as a recursive process of thinking and communication
● Articulate elements of effective writing and integrate them into their own work
● Participate in critical conversations by responding to openings, problems, or
contradictions in existing scholarship
● Assess their own and others’ writing with respect to audience and purpose
● Generate original questions and pursue independent research
● Identify and evaluate sources and use them responsibly
● Develop awareness of disciplinary differences in writing and adapt their writing to
different genres and contexts
Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Statement
In this class, we are seriously committed to supporting diversity and inclusion among all
classroom community members. We proactively strive to construct a safe and inclusive
environment by respecting each other’s dignity and privacy. We treat one another fairly and
honor each member’s experiences, beliefs, perspectives, abilities, and backgrounds. Our class
provides a safe space for free inquiry and open exchange of ideas. We will be talking about a
variety of topics: some you may agree with and some you may not. We will foster a supportive
and safe environment for all students to share their opinions. You should expect to have your
ideas, work, and arguments challenged. That’s where learning happens! I insist that all students
be respectful and considerate during these discussions. Bullying, hateful ideas, violent language,
belittling, racial slurs, and other disrespectful language or behavior will not be tolerated.
*A note on language: Some material we will
listen to uses potentially harmful or offensive
speech. We can acknowledge the role such
language plays within the context of a song, but
we will not repeat such language ourselves.
Repeating racist, misogynist, or homophobic
terms aloud can be counterproductive to the
work of community building and social justice
that our class strives for.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Required Materials\nThe Brandeis Writing Program has implemented a zero cost program to promote equity for all\nstudents. All required texts/media will be available online on our Moodle course page.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Assignments
The Lens Essay. For this assignment, you will use
the lens of an essay by Kevin Quashie to analyze
one of the following music videos: Beyoncé's
“Formation,” Kendrick Lamar’s “Alright,” or
Childish Gambino’s “This Is America.”
Research Proposal and Research Essay.
For this assignment, you will design an independent
research project related to the intersections of music and
social justice. Your essay will present a focused argument
supported by academic research that links your chosen
musical topic to broader societal issues.
The Writing Process . Writing is a progressive task. Composing an essay the night before it is due
does not facilitate the development of writing and analytical skills. Thus, each major assignment
will be broken down into smaller assignments that enable more focused critical thought from
you, and more focused feedback from your peers and me.
● Writing “Soundchecks.” Most classes will begin with a “soundcheck”: listening to a song
and responding to a brief prompt to warm up our writing and critical thinking skills.
● Pre-Draft Assignments . Each essay will be preceded by a series of writing assignments
designed to help you develop your ideas and your skills for your essay.
● First Drafts . You will be required to turn in a first draft of each Major Assignment. I do
not expect these drafts to be polished, but I do expect them to be complete .
● Peer Review . You will form a group with two of your peers upon completing the first
draft of an essay. You will carefully read each other’s drafts, and you will provide
actionable feedback on their writing. These activities will help to increase your sense of
audience awareness. After all, you’re writing for an audience, not just me!
● Conferences . Each student will have two conferences with me over the course of the
semester. One conference is to discuss the first draft of your lens essay and the other is to
discuss your research proposal. Missing a conference is equivalent to missing a class.
Comparative Genre Analysis. Between our Lens and Research Essay Units, you will work in
small groups to conduct a Comparative Genre Analysis. Scholars from different disciplines make
different choices when writing. These choices aren’t random, but reflect what people value in
their writing. Understanding that different disciplines produce different genres of writing will
help you better understand what you read. It will also help you have more awareness of your own
writing in the future.
Final Portfolio. At the end of the semester, you will compile all of your written work into a
single document. This means that even after you have turned in an assignment, you must
keep an electronic copy of it until the end of the semester. You will introduce the portfolio
with a cover letter that describes how you have developed as a writer over the course of the
semester and how you intend to use the skills you have acquired in this class in the future.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Grading and Evaluation
This course will employ a form of grading
called Process and Engagement Based
Grading. This system emphasizes the process
of writing and personal progress over
reaching a one-size-fits-all conventional
grade. You will be graded solely on the work
that you put in and on your regular
attendance and participation. You will still
receive feedback on your work in the course
from your peers and from me, so you will
still know how your writing is developing,
but your grade will not be based on reaching
an objective level of “success.”
My hope is that by removing the anxieties and fears often attached to conventional grading, you
will have an opportunity to take more risks with your work, to pursue what interests you, and to
develop and work hard toward your own goals. Because this kind of grading focuses on the
process of writing, on the consistent completion of work, and on multiple opportunities for
engagement, it provides every student with an equal opportunity for class success regardless of
previous academic background.
Each assignment will be worth the following for your overall grade:
Lens Essay 25%
Research Proposal and Essay 45%
Writing Community Membership 20%
Portfolio 10%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Late and Missed Assignments . Consistent, timely submission of work is crucial for your
success in this course and for receiving valuable feedback. To provide flexibility while
maintaining fairness, we will use the following policy for late assignments:
The final revision of each major essay will automatically include a 48-hour grace period
after the deadline. Note that this extension only applies to the final revision of each major
essay. All other late items will receive the following deductions each day they are late:
Pre-Draft Assignments: ¼ credit deduction per day late
First Drafts: ½ credit deduction per day late
Final Drafts (after grace period): 1 full credit deduction per day late
If you anticipate difficulty meeting a deadline, please communicate with me as soon as possible.
We can discuss potential accommodations on a case-by-case basis.
Writing Community Membership
This is a discussion-based workshop class, which means that it’s a collaboration amongst you,
your peers, and me as we work towards the common goal of improving your writing. Therefore,

5
your role as a member of our writing community is crucial to this course. This membership is
primarily demonstrated through two main components: participation and attendance.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
The first step to participation is attendance: you can’t participate if you’re not
here. However, things do happen. You are permitted to miss no more than 5 classes without an
impact on your grade. UWP policy states that there are no distinctions between excused and
unexcused absences. However, if you are unable to attend class, I encourage you to communicate
with me, so that we can get you back on track with the materials you missed. Each additional
absence after a 5th absence that is not discussed with me will lower your grade for participation
and attendance. Students who miss 9 or more classes will receive a failing grade for the class.
If you have a special obligation that will require you to miss classes (e.g., religious observances,
athletics obligations, etc), please talk with me at the beginning of the semester. If there are
extraordinary circumstances that cause you to miss more than the allowed number of classes,
please contact me so we can discuss how to best handle the situation. You are responsible for all
material that you miss, including assignments completed in class.
Important Resources and Policies
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Writing Center. I encourage you to make use of the Brandeis University Writing Center , which
offers free writing support to all Brandeis community members in the form of in-person and
Zoom face-to-face and e-tutoring document-drop appointments as well as workshops. The
Writing Center’s team of expert consultants is on hand to help you with your writing project at

6
any stage, from brainstorming, to outlining, to revision. Every semester, the University Writing
Center also holds a series of UWS workshops on each of the three major assignments. All UWS
students are welcome to register for these workshops online.
Zotero Training
Zotero is a free, easy-to-use tool to help you collect, organize, cite, and share research. All
students are required to attend one of the library's Zotero Training Sessions during the semester.
Student Accommodations. Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming
and inclusive of all students, and I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may
require disability accommodations, you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support
(SAS). You can contact them at 781-736-3470, email them at access@brandeis.edu , or visit the
Student Accessibility Support home page . You can find helpful student FAQs and other resources
on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you might be eligible for
support from SAS. If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me
with a copy as soon as you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations
for this class.
Mental Health. I greatly value your experience in this class, and it is my duty to facilitate a safe,
caring, and productive learning environment. I recognize that you may experience a range of
emotional, physical, and/or psychological issues, both in and out of the classroom, that may
distract you from your learning. If you are experiencing such issues, please do not hesitate to
come see me—I am here to listen. We can discuss what resources are available to you. For
example, the Brandeis Counseling Center (BCC) is a multicultural counseling center that addresses
the diverse psychological needs of our university students.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Academic Honesty. You are expected to be familiar with, and to follow, the University’s policies
on academic integrity. You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please
consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to
academic integrity. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to Student
Rights and Community Standards. Sanctions for academic dishonesty can include failing grades
and/or suspension from the university. Citation and research assistance can be found on the
university library website .
Use of AI Tools . In this class, we will use writing as a way to sharpen both our critical thinking
and analysis skills as well as to develop skills that allow us to clearly communicate our ideas.
While it is appropriate to use artificial intelligence (AI) for tasks like checking spelling or
grammar and for brainstorming ideas, you may not use AI to generate full sentences, paragraphs,
or papers. For example, you may not use AI to generate pre-drafts and drafts/revisions,
reflections, in-class exercises, peer letters, or communications with your instructor. This is
because claiming the work of others as your own, whether created by another human or by AI, is
regarded as plagiarism and therefore will be subject to the penalties outlined above in the
“Academic Honesty” section of the syllabus. If you have any questions about appropriate or
inappropriate use of AI, please don’t hesitate to ask.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Participation (10%). Participation includes coming to class on time and being prepared with all
required readings and completed assignments, taking part in daily discussions, contributing in
small group work, engaging in in-class writing tasks, and actively listening to your peers. The
more thoroughly you complete your homework assignments, the more prepared you’ll be to
contribute to class. During class, please do not have other websites or applications open on your
device. Because we only meet for 50 minutes per class period, I think we owe it to ourselves and
to each other to be present during that time.
Attendance (10%).
spans.label: GRADE

school: School of Arts, Humanities, and Culture

spans.text:
Laptop computer and cell phone use
Laptop and cell phone use during a language class is a distraction. I rely on your maturity to keep your
devices turned off and put away for the duration of class. Using devices during class time or leaving class to
use them in the halls will result in a lowered participation grade. Please refer to the Participation Grading
Rubric on Moodle for details.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: During class, you will be asked to communicate in French to the best of your abilities. Do not be\ndiscouraged by errors, challenges, or corrections – this is an important part of the process. Indeed, if you\nare not making mistakes and are already speaking in perfect French, this is not the right class for you!
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: If you\nfind that you have questions about placement, please do not hesitate to speak to me about options.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description:
French 32 is designed to help students improve their command of French, specifically to develop their
speaking skills and to enrich their knowledge of Francophone society, boosting them to the ACTFL
intermediate level. The class will work on all skill areas (speaking, listening, writing, reading, culture) while
addressing themes pertinent to the modern Francophone world that generate discussion or controversy,
such as the role of artificial intelligence in our lives, gender identity, racial identity, and a variety of cultural
and historical topics. Using a variety of authentic French language media, we will work on your ability to
discuss personal and social topics, support ideas and opinions with facts and personal experience, read
short cultural and literary texts, participate in class discussions, and express your opinions in writing.
Assessment is based on daily homework assignments, a series of oral and written exams/ projects, a record
of attendance and participation, and a series of final assignments.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Please note that in this class, called “Controverses,” we will learn vocabulary and grammar structures that
allow you to engage in healthy debate with francophone peers. As does Brandeis, I consider the diversity
that students bring to the course an invaluable resource. I will present materials and activities that are
respectful of diversity: gender, sexuality, physical ability, age, socioeconomic status, ethnicity, race, and
culture. Each day, we view images and read texts that may reflect other realities and worldviews, which is the
purpose of the humanities.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
This semester you will
- develop analytic tools to examine and better understand francophone culture, particularly issues of
controversy that concern young adults (see above).
-improve your confidence in using spoken French in informal and formal situations.
-expand your vocabulary and grammar skills to communicate effectively in different situations while
reviewing “Novice” vocabulary topics, such as family, home, and food.
-improve your pronunciation by focusing on the most difficult sounds of the language (/r/, nasals, etc.).
-express your ideas about past, present, and future events and your opinions and beliefs in a clear, concise,
and persuasive manner in written and oral forms.
-read, analyze, and discuss short literary works, images, films, advertisements, and articles at an intermediate
low-mid level.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Outcomes:
We will measure your ability to reach the above goals by requiring you to
-incorporate pertinent cultural material into class presentations, writings, projects, and exams.
-present phonetically and grammatically accurate verbal reports and conversations.
-manipulate new and old vocabulary and grammar in different modalities (written and oral).
-compose short reports, posters, reactions, opinions, and analyses.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Social justice, attitudes, and behaviors: Learning French will enable you to gain a deeper understanding of
different regions of the francophone world and support your participation as informed citizens in a global
society.
This course will specifically
• expand your knowledge and awareness of traditions and artistic expressions of different
francophone cultures.
• encourage you to reflect on your culture, and weigh its similarities and differences with those of
other cultures.
• better understand what connects people worldwide – linguistically, culturally, and otherwise –
through study of texts, film, and music.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Credit Hours: Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a\nminimum of nine hours of study time per week in preparation for class, not including exam preparation.
spans.label: ADMIN

Course Requirements
Homework Assignments
“Cahier d’activités” = “Student activities manual”): You must complete and correct the grammar exercises in

either on paper or as a pdf.

A missed in-person assessment (ex. a chapter\ntest, quiz, presentation, interview) without notice will not be rescheduled at a later date.

spans.text:
Participation and Attendance
Because language is a social and interactive phenomenon, attendance and participation are essential to your
success in this class. Learning to speak another language demands in-class engagement. Participation is
noted on a daily basis.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Your attendance counts for 10% of your final grade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
You are allowed four absences
for any reason; no justification or explanation will be required. Three tardy arrivals/ early departures for
class equal one absence. Each absence that exceeds the allowed number will result in a lower grade in two
ways: specifically, each additional absence will result both a lowered attendance grade and 1% subtracted
from the final course grade. Eight or more absences will result in a discussion with your professor and the
Director of Languages to ascertain whether you can continue in the class, and it may result in a failing
course grade. Students in situations that will require them to be absent for an extended period should
contact their Academic Advisor; the Advisor will contact your professor about excused absences. If you
miss the first two classes of the semester with no official accommodation, you may be automatically
dropped from the class.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
*Absences due to sports activities, pertinent accommodations as determined by SAS, or religious
obligations will be considered separately. If you must be absent from class for those reasons, please let me
know in advance when possible. In the event that you will miss an in-class assignment or test, please let me
know at once 1 week in advance.

New this semester! Proficiency in Language Transcript Notation after
completing two courses beyond the language requirement (FREN 32)
• Successful completion of FREN 105 (or your course, if appropriate) entitles you to receive

the Proficiency in Language Transcript Notation, which is a note on your transcript that
formally documents your achievement in language learning at Brandeis.
• The Notation explicitly recognizes your success at demonstrating an Intermediate-High level
of language proficiency and cultural understanding in French beyond the Brandeis Core
requirement, and it officially commends you on your efforts to expand and maintain your
multilingualism.
• If you are interested in the Transcript Notation, please contact Prof. Harder
(harder@brandeis.edu), the Director of the language program in French. She will register
you for the notation.
