spans.text:
Academic Integrity
Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic
integrity. A student shall not submit work that is falsified or is not the result of the student's own effort.
Using phrases, ideas, or even fragments from undocumented Internet sources, native speakers, ChatGPT

other A.I., or other “helpers” is not a reflection of your abilities and will not end up helping you learn the
material.
Please consult Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to
academic integrity. A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic honesty as they apply to a
specific course or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or assignment
before submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the
Department of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be found at
Brandeis Library Guides - Citing Sources .
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Privacy

websites: we will use the free learning site Quizlet for vocabulary and Edpuzzle for some video assignments;
we will also use the Kahoot! quiz platform. You will rely on Moodle for most assignments and will submit

doc_id: 253FREN 126B 1 La place de la nature dans le monde culturel francophone Hollie Harder
course_name: 253FREN 126B 1 La place de la nature dans le monde culturel francophone Hollie Harder

spans.text: Room TBA\nMonday, Wednesday, Thursday 11:15 a.m. -12:05 p.m.\n(in person)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
In this course, you will deepen your knowledge and understanding of the role of nature in the
Francophone world by examining some of the cultural, political, literary, and artistic
principles that contributed to creating its unique place in the world.
For example, we will discover how, during the Renaissance, the purpose of nature seemed
limited to satisfying the needs, desires and pleasures of human beings. We will try to make
sense of what drove French culture to popularize the notion of le jardin français with its
conical trees and geometrical shrubbery, we will explore what made people around the
Francophone world withdraw into nature as though it were a protective haven, and
subsequently abuse it, especially in the former French colonies in the Caribbean and in
Western Africa, for the purpose of productivity and profit. You will examine your own
0

attitudes toward nature and how your "culture of nature" compares to that of the
Francophone world.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals and Outcomes
Core skills
Specific learning goals and outcomes for each unit are outlined on Moodle. By the end of the
semester, you will be able to
• communicate ePectively with others about concrete and abstract topics that you
have explored or researched (interpersonal communication), exchanging ideas with
others without mistakes that impede understanding and transmission;
• present information and express your thoughts to others in written and spoken French
(presentational communication) without mistakes that impede communication;
and
• quickly understand main ideas and arguments when reading and listening and
explain key supporting information that clarifies larger cultural notions (interpretive
communication)
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Knowledge
Specific learning goals and outcomes for each unit are outlined on Moodle.
By the end of the semester, you will know
• about more complex intellectual, social, political, artistic, and cultural aspects of the
French-speaking world (intercultural investigation), for example
-- current policies and practices that are emblematic of the role that nature plays
in French-speaking cultures
--the evolution of thought and the development of practices that have led to current
notions about nature and the environment

--the culturally unique ways in which attitudes toward nature and the environment
have progressed in the Francophone world during the twenty-first century
• how to interact appropriately in nuanced situations with French speakers
(intercultural interaction).
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Global Engagement and Social Justice through World Languages
and Cultures at Brandeis
FREN 126B contributes to the Brandeis Core curriculum through its role in
the Global Engagement / World Languages and Cultures requirement.
Proficiency in another language is a cornerstone of global citizenship, and
it is essential for ePective communications and collaboration among
people of diPerent cultures. Learning another language also plays a
central role in cultivating a sense of social justice and in deepening and
broadening sensitivity to issues of inclusion and exclusion.
The process of learning another language inspires open-mindedness and adaptability,
awakens curiosity and creativity, fosters innovative paths of inquiry and discovery, and
builds a deeper understanding of yourself and others.
This course will help you to develop and test your ability to think critically. Through the
analysis and evaluation of texts, situations, films, and images, and you will piece together
meaning from your examination of the products, practices, perspectives, and power
structures you find in Francophone cultures. This process of reflection will challenge you to
identify your own social, cultural, and political traditions and assumptions, and it may
prompt you to reevaluate your own ways of thinking.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Teaching and Learning Strategies in FREN 126B
This course is student-centered and depends on learner engagement, interaction, and
participation.
In a traditional classroom, the teacher is often the center of the learning experience and
takes the "active" role, while the students assume a more "passive" or receptive role.
In this course, you will take an active role every day in your learning, interacting with
fellow students and your professor in order to build an intellectual community.
This course is based on a flipped-classroom model.
You will often interact with new information first as homework. We will use class time to
discuss any questions you have about the new material and then put the new (and already-
familiar) components into practice. This hands-on experience in class enables you to focus
on the processing part of learning (synthesizing, analyzing, applying, evaluating, etc.) as you
use language to question and communicate.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Academic Integrity and Generative AI
Taking a course in a language other than English requires a
special commitment to academic integrity. Our goal in this
course is for you to improve your skills in speaking, reading,
listening, and writing in French; using AI tools in the form of
ChatGPT and translation tools where you outsource the work
of this course prevents you from mastering French.
EAective learning requires you to work through diAiculties
that you may have in expressing your ideas and in understanding what you hear and
read. EAective learning requires you to use resources to figure out solutions to better
internalize them so you can mentally retrieve them and use them in the future.
All the work you do in this course must be your own. It is not acceptable to ask that an entity
(such as a tutor, a fellow student, a friend, a relative, or an AI model) help you to edit,
correct, translate, or write any part of an assignment.
Representing work generated by artificial intelligence as one's own work is academically
dishonest. Please properly cite any sources that you use.
Assignments that are partially or completely written by an AI model will not be accepted, and
they will be treated as plagiarism.
The goal of this course is for you to enhance your learning and understanding. ChatGPT and
other AI tools are not a replacement for your own critical thinking and original ideas, and
inappropriate use of them will prevent you from making progress in this course.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Brandeis Academic Integrity Code for oral and written tasks in a foreign-language class
Academic work in language classes requires you to use
language creatively to express your thoughts and opinions.
The language you use must be verifiably your own.
I encourage you to:
● use class materials as a source of language models (culturally and linguistically
appropriate ways of expressing ideas);
• use dictionaries and grammar tables (including digital resources) to check forms
(such as verb endings, adjectives, etc.), spelling, and meanings;
● search authentic sources to identify expressions and phrases you may need for your
own writing;
● proofread your own work; read it aloud and hear how it sounds as well as how it looks.
I encourage you to use class materials and dictionaries in preparing the drafts and final
versions of your essays. However, please do not:
• consult native speakers or others about your work
• use Al models or translators (such as Google Translate) while composing or
correcting your work.
Essays that in style, syntactic, and lexical complexity do not correspond to your level of
language proficiency will stand out as being suspect, and you will be asked to provide an
explanation.
PLEASE TAKE NOTE: DOCUMENT THE HELP YOU GET ON ASSIGNMENTS
If you consult outside sources to complete an assignment, please indicate the type and
source of help you received. For example:
• I used the online Larousse French-English dictionary [www.larousse.fr] to find “to
expect,” “lighthouse,” “to challenge,” and “with regard to” for this exercise;
• I used the Wikipédia site on “greetings in French”
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salutation] to help me explain my ideas.
• Please use the Brandeis Library Guide when you are citing AI for this course:
https://guides.library.brandeis.edu/citeai
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and
inclusive of all students, and I want to support you in your learning.

doc_id: 261FIN 181A 1 Banks and Financial Stability Carol Osler
course_name: 261FIN 181A 1 Banks and Financial Stability Carol Osler

spans.text: Professor Osler
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Mondays & Wednesdays, 2:30-3:50, Lee Hall
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: cosler@brandeis.edu Office hours: Wednesdays, 10:30-12:00\nPersonal website: here Office: Sachar 205
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
OVERVIEW
Banks have been the beating heart of world commerce for over 500 years. A sound banking
system is essential for robust economic growth. By investing depositor funds wisely, banks
help individuals increase their wealth and reduce their risk. Bank profits reflect the expertise of
bankers in assessing and managing risk. They are good at identifying potential deadbeat
borrowers, for example, and they know how derivatives can protect a bank from interest-rate
swings.
The banking industry is inherently unstable due to the risk of bank runs and fraud. The failure
of a single US bank in 2023, Silicon Valley Bank, triggered a massive contraction in the start-
up sector. The failure of a single dominant bank can stop an entire economy in its tracks, as the
Dominican Republic learned in 2003-2004. Because the banking industry is so economically
important and so unstable, it is more regulated than any other industry. The best way to regulate
banks is hotly disputed, however.
This course introduces you banks and the banking industry. We’ll discuss the structure of a
normal bank, the risks it faces, and how it manages those risks. We’ll also discuss why the
banking industry is potentially unstable, why and how it is regulated by governments, and why
some regulatory strategies are controversial. More broadly, we’ll study the origins of banking
crises and why they occur frequently.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Prerequisites: Financial Management (Fin 103) or Financial Economics (ECON 171a) and\nBUS 6a, Financial Accounting (neither finance nor accounting can be taken concurrently with\nthis course). Comfort with basic Excel skills.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
LEARNING GOALS
1. Understanding of a bank’s balance sheet and bank performance is measured.
2. Understanding of the risks facing a bank and how they manage those risks.
• Credit risk  Interest-rate risk
• Liquidity risk  Currency risk
3. Understanding of the inherent instability of fractional-reserve banking systems.
• Retail and wholesale bank runs  Fraud
4. Understanding of how and why banks are regulated and the risks that arise when banks are
not properly regulated. Familiarity with associated controversies.
• Deposit insurance  Bank equity as an option
• Capital adequacy  Gamble for resurrection

• Loan-loss reserves  Bank charters
• Liquidity reserves
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
COURSEWORK
1. Class participation
 Attendance: This is an in-person class; attendance will be taken.
 You can miss at most two of the semester’s 26 classes without penalty. If you are ill, let me
know in advance so I can try to set up a Zoom link.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
2. Assignments: Practice is essential for developing any skill, including the skills involved in bank risk
management. At least once each week you will practice using the concepts just covered in class.
Guidance for assignments is provided via examples or detailed instructions.
 Each assignment is designed for you to practice using specific analytical tools and methods
demonstrated in class. Success thus depends on studying the text or lecture notes while
working on the assignments. No credit will be given when the lecture notes are ignored.
 The single most helpful suggestion for doing well in this course is to study the lecture
notes while doing the assignments. The surest way to do poorly is to assume you can do
these otherwise.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
 Your own work: You may not rely on AI or classmates. You must explain everything in your
own words and do the calculations on your own. If not, the assignment gets zero credit.
 You are welcome to work with others to understand the problems or to master Excel techniques
(but not to write up the answers).
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: 3. Quizzes: There will be a 5-10 minute quiz at the beginning of each class. Most question will cover\nthe most recent class; other questions will cover earlier material.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 4. Team Bank Analysis: You and another student will analyze a bank of your choice.\n Further information on the bank analysis is provided at the end of this syllabus and on Moodle.

spans.text: 5. Midterm exam: Wednesday March 11. Closed book

spans.text:
ACADEMIC INTEGRITY: You, as a member of the University community, must maintain the highest
standards of academic integrity. You will not submit work that is falsified, that copies anyone else’s text
for any reason unless it is in quotations and cited (plagiarism), that is the result of someone else’s effort.
You may not use AI for any purpose except polishing your prose. You will cite your sources, including
any AI for polishing prose, and provide complete, verifiable references. If you violate this or the
University standards for academic integrity the assignment will get zero credit. In addition, you could fail
the course or be suspended from the University (see Brandeis University Rights and Responsibilities). I do

not hesitate to forward potential instances of academic dishonesty to the Department of Student Rights and
Community Standards.
Please reach out to me before submitting a given assignment if you are unsure about integrity standards.
Guidelines for citations and references are provided on the course Moodle site.

spans.text:
PROJECT DETAILS
I. 4-Minute Presentation
You will introduce your classmates to an important type of bank asset, liability, customer
type, or regulatory body. You will provide the following:
 Definition
 Function and importance for the banking industry
 Brief history: Origins and past growth, current size measures
 Something intriguing – Scandal? A recent dramatic change?
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: II. Team Bank Analysis\nYou a partner will choose a bank and analyze it throughout the semester.\n• Everything you need to know is on Moodle under “Guidelines for Bank Analysis.”

use someone else’s words or AI-sourced content. This prohibition holds regardless of
whether quotation marks or citations are provided.
• You must cite your sources and provide complete, verifiable references, including whether
you used AI to polish prose.

spans.text:
Course Description
Congratulations—you have made it to the next level! Russian 30 will launch you into a much higher
orbit within the language universe and open new horizons for you! While continuing to develop your
ability to communicate effectively and accurately in Russian and to explore Russian culture, you will
expand your vocabulary, sharpen your pragmatic skills, and complete your understanding of the
Russian grammatical system.
Russian 30 is the first semester of the two-semester sequence of Intermediate Russian. Although Brandeis
requires only three semesters of a foreign language, we strongly encourage you to complete this sequence
and take Russian 40 when it is offered next year!
spans.label: DESCRIP
DESCRIP

spans.text:
Required Materials
1

Brandeis University, GRALL Intermediate Russian 30A
Fall 2025
For the first few weeks, we will continue using the textbook Russian Stage One: Live from Moscow:
Volume II (your textbook from Russian 20) and the accompanying workbook, as we still need to finish
chapters 11, 12 and 13. Then we will start working with a new textbook—Этажи: Intermediate Russian
by S. Nazarova and E. Dengub (Georgetown University Press 2021).
You will find all the unit plans, homework assignments, links to instructional videos, readings, study
guides, and other materials related to the course on the Russian 30 course page on Moodle.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Your responsibilities
Statement on Brandeis academic integrity
Academic integrity is central to the mission of educational excellence at Brandeis University. Each
student is expected to turn in work completed independently, except when assignments
specifically authorize collaborative effort. Violations of University policies on academic integrity,
described in Section Three of Rights and Responsibilities, may result in failure of the course or on
the assignment, or in suspension or dismissal from the University. If you are in doubt about the
instructions for any assignment in this course, it is your responsibility to ask your instructor for
clarification.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text: Engagement, participation, and punctuality\nYou are expected to contribute to a respectful and positive learning environment.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Cell phones and social media
I rely on your maturity to know that any use of cell phones and social media sites during our class
meetings is disrespectful and distracting. Phones should be turned off and out of sight, while class
is in session. Likewise, please close down all social media applications and turn off notifications on
your computers.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Group dynamics
This class relies heavily on all of you working together. Some of you are faster at grasping new
grammatical concepts and memorizing new vocabulary, while others require more time to
process language-related information. Personality types also play a significant role in how people
learn languages: some may be reluctant to speak in front of other people, while others are more
outgoing and will not hesitate to experiment. Please be patient with each other and always remain
polite! It is important to keep in mind that you NEED each other to activate what you have learned
in class and at home—so, please be grateful for having your classmates, who can help you with
this task!
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Notice of accommodation
Brandeis seeks to welcome and include all students. If you are a student who needs
accommodations as outlined in an accommodations letter, please talk with me and present your
letter of accommodation as soon as you can. In order to provide accommodations during a test, I
must see the letter more than 48 hours in advance. If you have any questions about documenting a
disability or requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility Support (SAS) at
781.736.3470 or access@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text: Communication
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Please make sure your Brandeis e-mail address is active and check it frequently! I will communicate a lot
of important information about the course to you via the Course Announcement function on our Moodle
page or by directly e-mailing you.
You can always contact me by e-mail with questions related to the course. I will try to respond within 24
hours. Please note that I will not check my e-mail after 10:00 p.m. EST!
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Evaluation Structure
Your final grade will be calculated as follows:
• Class participation
o 46 classes out of 50 possible x 5 points each = 230 points
• Recitation participation
o 10 recitations out of 12 possible x 5 points each = 50 points
• Homework = 240 points total
o daily homework: 44 out of 48 possible x 5 points each = 220 points
• Weekly quizzes = 100 points total
o grammar and vocabulary quizzes: 10 out of 13 possible x 10 points each = 100 points
• Unit tests (4) x 50 points each = 200 points
• Final Exam/Task = 100 points
• Final Project = 100 points
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Grading Scale for the Final Course Grade
Letter Grade Percentage
A 94-100%
A- 90-93%
B+ 87-89%
B 84-86%
B- 80-83%
C+ 77-79%
C 74-76%
C- 70-73%
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Components of Course Work Explained
Participation (230 points)
Your participation will be marked on a very simple scale:
5 points – you are on time, prepared (i.e., you have done your homework and are prepared to
work with new material in class), and actively participate in classwork
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
0 points – you are not prepared (i.e., you have not done your homework and, as a result, your
participation in classwork is limited)
Please note that you are allowed to miss up to 4 classes for any reason without penalty—the total
score for participation takes this into account!
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Recitation hour (50 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
This course requires that you meet with an instructor for one additional hour per week. It is called the
“Recitation Hour,” which in a language-learning course means that you will practice phonetics, do
selected homework exercises together, practice conversation, prepare for oral exams, and have an extra
opportunity to work with authentic language and culture materials. Recitations will also be reserved for
short weekly vocabulary quizzes.
The exact meeting times for the recitations will be scheduled at the beginning of the semester by common
consent. Recitations will start in the 2nd week of the semester. There will be a total of 12 recitations. You
must participate in at least 10 recitations, in order to receive a full credit for this portion of your final
grade.
Please note that your participation in the Recitation Hour is mandatory! It is as important as your
attendance of our regular class sessions, and it is subject to the same participation and classroom policy!
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Making up for Missed Classes
While you are expected to actively participate in each class and recitation session, we recognize the
inherent uncertainties of real life. If you have to miss a class for a legitimate reason and want to make up
for it, you need to contact your instructor. You will be expected to complete the list of tasks provided by
the instructor.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Textbook/workbook homework must be submitted on the day it is due. If you are not able to make it to
class, you should still submit your homework on time, in order to receive a full credit. The expectations
for your homework are as follows: it has to be complete (i.e., all the assignments should be done), it has to
be at least 90% correct, and it should not contain any systematic errors indicating that re-learning and re-
focusing is required.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Homework (220 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
There are three possible scores you may receive for your homework:
• full credit – 5 points (if your work meets the expectations outlined above)
• no credit – 0 points (if the homework was submitted more than a week after the date it was due or
if it does not meet the expectations of being at least 90% correct and complete)
• partial credit (if your homework does not meet the expectations outlined above, you will have the
option of correcting all circled mistakes and resubmitting the work NO LATER THAN BY THE END
OF THE UNIT. A full credit of 5 points will be given only when all corrections had been made and
the work was resubmitted. If you do not submit your corrections, you will receive a partial credit.
Only one resubmission per homework is allowed.)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Please note:\nIf your homework was submitted more than a week late, it will be accepted and checked for\nerrors, but you will NOT receive any credit for it. No resubmissions will be allowed for late homework.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: Corrections/resubmissions must be received by the last day of the unit (usually, the unit-test day).\nHomework resubmitted after the unit test will be accepted, but you will only receive a partial credit for it.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text: This 4-credit-hour course is based on the expectation that students will study on a\nregular basis and spend 10 to 12 hours per week preparing for classes (including\nwritten homework, instructional videos, preparation for quizzes and exams, etc.).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Vocabulary and grammar quizzes (100 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Every week, you will have grammar and vocabulary quizzes. Vocabulary quizzes will mostly take place\nduring the recitations. Grammar quizzes will take place in class. You should expect to be quizzed on any\nlexical and grammatical material that will have been covered until that point.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
There are three possible scores you may receive on a quiz:
• full credit – 10 points (if your work meets the expectations of being at least 90% correct)
• partial credit (if your work is below expectations, but still has some substance and is more
than 80% correct)
• no credit – 0 points (for missed quizzes and for work that has little substance and is less than
80% correct)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Unit tests (200 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: Tests will be scheduled at the end of each unit. You will receive specific instructions on how to prepare for\nsuch tests.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Final exam (100 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: There will be a final task due at the end of the semester during the Finals Week. The format of this final\nproject will be determined in collaboration with students.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Final Project (100)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Starting in the middle of September, every Thursday class will be held on zoom—we will be meeting
with students from the Pedagogical University in Yaroslavl, Russia, and chatting with them (in Russian!)
about various subjects. These conversations will be part of our regular class activities—we will prepare
for them together, you will receive homework and other assignments for these meetings, and your final
project will also be based on the information that you will have obtained in the course of these
encounters. You will be provided with detailed instructions for your final project early in the semester.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Please be advised that the Moodle gradebook provides you with a fairly
accurate but still approximate calculation of your grade. The instructor
reserves the right to adjust the total number of points and the grade at the end
of the semester. I invite you to think more about developing your proficiency
in the language rather than focusing on the mechanics of how the grade is
calculated—the ultimate goal for you is to gain an ability to communicate
effectively in Russian!
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
What to Do if You Need Extra Help
Please do not wait until problems start piling up! There are three people in the Russian Language
Program you can turn to for help: your instructor and/or a BUGS tutor. You can come to either of us with
your questions, but it also may be helpful to differentiate your sources of support:
o If you need another explanation of a particular concept or want to practice vocabulary,
pronunciation, conversation, seek help from your instructor (Ирина Евгеньевна
Дубинина) during office hours or by appointment;
o If you would like extra practice of grammar, vocabulary or conversation, sign up to meet
with Matvey Agronovskiy, BUGS tutor for Russian, magranovskiy@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Other Useful Contacts
The Russian Program’s Undergraduate Representatives for the 2024-25 academic year are Zoe Potolsky
(zoepotolsky@brandeis.edu) and Isabella Seemann (isabellaseemann@brandeis.edu) – note that
Isabella is away on a study-abroad in the fall. They can be a wonderful resource not only for your
questions about majoring/minoring in Russian, but also about Russian literature, studying habits, or
successful language-learning strategies.
The undergraduate advisor for the Russian Language Program is Professor David Powelstock
(pstock@brandeis.edu). He will help you plan out your Russian Studies major or minor and guide you in
questions related to study-abroad credits.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Statement on Success
Your academic success in this course depends heavily on your personal health and wellbeing. Stress is a
common part of the college experience, and it often can be compounded by unexpected setbacks or life

spans.text:
Course Description
Course Prerequisite(s):
MUS 35a, The History of American Popular Music, has no prerequisites, but does involve a good amount of
reading and writing. While students will be primarily evaluated for their ideas and understanding,
proficiency in writing will aid the successful completion of this course.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals:
In this course students will examine the historical context, stylistic development, and cultural significance
of rock and roll and other closely related genres, spanning from the birth of America popular music
through the present. Close attention will be paid to how political and social changes have interacted with
technological innovations through commercial music to challenge, affirm and shape ideas of race, gender,
class and sexuality in the United States. Ultimately the goal of this course is to provide students with the
vocabulary, analytical tools and historical background to understand the music they listen to encounter
as people in the 21st century. While the course is structure as a lecture, class sessions will be participatory
and will provide space for students to reflect on the music they hear and study in order to share their
personal experiences and observations. By the end of the course students will be able to:
• Engage in detail with the core musical characteristics of rock by analyzing its constituent elements
(rhythm, form, pitch structure, instrumental texture, and vocal style).
• Recognize and identify important musical styles and artists by ear.
• Contextualize the role of rock music within U.S. history and society.

• Demonstrate knowledge of relationships among subcultures and subgenres within the rock music
tradition.
• Build skills in effective writing about rock music and in appropriate modes of listening.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Credit Hours:
Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of nine
hours of study time per week in preparation for class (readings, papers, discussion sections, preparation
for the exams, etc.)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Requirements
Textbook
All students will need to acquire a copy of the eBook 6th edition American Popular Music by Larry Starr
and Waterman. There will be assigned reading from this text as well as articles and videos shared via
LATTE. All listening will be provided as a Spotify Playlist. Because of the online nature of this course,
assigned reading and listening make up a substantial portion of the course materials. Lectures are
supplemental to this materials.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: Participation/Attendance\nThis course is asynchronous so there is no required attendance at specific times. Instead students earn\ntheir participation grade by commenting on weekly musical discussion threads.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Assignments
This class will involve regular assignments in two broad categories: Written Assignments and Discussion
Prompts. These assignments will provide the opportunity for students to demonstrate their knowledge
and understanding of class materials while reflecting their personal experiences with music. Most weeks
will have one of these smaller assignments. Written Assignments will be short responses to listening
assignments and out of class activities. Discussion Prompts will be based on course readings will be
submitted on LATTE in Forums.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Larger Projects
In addition to regular assignments there will be two larger projects spread throughout the course. These
will consist of a Listening Guide and a Final Project. Detailed instructions for these will be provided on
LATTE.
spans.label: ASSIGN

want to support you in your learning. Live auto transcription is available for all meetings or classes hosted
on Zoom and you can turn it on or off to support your learning. Please check for Zoom updates to take

contact help@brandeis.edu

spans.text:
Course Plan
The basic plan for the “semester” is found below. Our course is divided into Units, focusing on a specific
time period or stylist development. Detailed reading instructions along with any Supplemental Articles
will be provided at the beginning of each Unit. Because we are covering a great deal of material this
schedule may change to accommodate the needs of the class as a whole:
Readings from Starr and Waterman’s American Popular Music - abbreviated APM
(Except where otherwise noted)
UNIT 1 6/2-6: APM Ch 1-4 Introduction and The Birth of American Popular Music
Discussion Prompt 1 Labeling Popular Music? – Due 6/6
UNIT 2 6/9-13: APM Ch 5-6 Race Records and Hillbilly Music
Written Assignment 1 What Music Represents You? – Due 6/13
UNIT 3 6/16-20: APM Ch 7 American Music after World War 2
Supplemental: Brown “‘Miss Rhythm’ Speaks Out: Ruth Brown, on R&B and Covers”
Discussion Prompt 2 Covers – Due 6/20
UNIT 4 6/23-27: APM Ch 8 Rock and Roll
Listening Guide Project – Due 6/27
UNIT 5 6/30-7/4: APM Ch 9 The 1960s and the British Invasion
Supplemental: Williams, “Understanding Dylan,”
Discussion Prompt 3 Bob Dylan’s Poetry – Due 7/4
UNIT 6 7/7-11: APM Ch 10 The Counterculture: Soul and Rock in the Late 1960s

Supplemental: Brian Eno, “The Studio as Compositional Tool”
Discussion Prompt 4 The Studio as Compositional Tool – Due 7/11
UNIT 7 7/14-18: APM Ch 11 Mainstream Music in the 1970s
Written Assignment 2 The Album – Due 7/18
UNIT 8 7/21-25: APM Ch 12 Outsider Music
Final Project Proposal – Due 7/21 (note - this is the beginning of the week!)
UNIT 9 7/28-8/1: APM Ch 13-14 Popular Music in the 1980s
Written Assignment 3 Interview – Due 8/1
UNIT 10 8/4-8: APM Ch 15 Popular Music and the Internet
Supplemental: Hyden, “It’s Time to Stop Using Rock as a Catch-All Term…”
Discussion Prompt 5 What’s in a Name? – Due 8/4 (the beginning of the week)
Final Project – Due 8/8

spans.text:
Course Description
Course Prerequisite(s)
It is expected that students have completed BUS 215, Python and Applications to Business Analytics or FIN
215, Python and Applications to Finance. No exceptions can be made because the course builds on the
core competencies built within that course.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Overview
Python is a one of the leading programming languages; developers use it for full-stack application
development. To create an application, a programmer needs to design, code, and test a front-end (if
required), a data model, and some business algorithms to make the application work. In business, these
applications help address real world problems.
This course is designed for business students who want to learn how to use Python to build applications.
Students will learn how to design a product, set up a coding environment, (optionally) develop test cases,
web-apps using python backend, apply business analytics with custom and library algorithms, follow
coding best practices.
The applications created in this course will help business analytics students who want to build
applications focused on analytics in the business world.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals
At the end of this course, the student should be able to design, code, test, and build applications. Specific
goals include:
• End-to-end application design
• Develop skillsets that are used in the life cycle of application development
• Enhance a student’s Python coding abilities
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Credit Hours
Success in this course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of seven hours of
study time per week in preparation for class (readings, discussion sections, preparation for the exams,
etc.).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Reference Materials
Mandatory
1. None
Recommended
1. Severance, C. Python for Everybody. Online at https://www.py4e.com/book.php [Lessons]
2. Web Scraping with Python by Ryan Mitchell. Online via Brandeis O’Reilly Library
3. Generative AI with LangChain by Ben Auffarth. Online via Brandeis O’Reilly Library
4. Streamlitopedia. https://pmbaumgartner.github.io/streamlitopedia/front/introduction.html
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
Course Requirements
Attendance
You are expected to attend all the classes. I will allow up to two class absences, no questions asked. Any
more absences must be communicated in advance via email. Note that the text and course video captures
cannot replace what you miss if you are absent, particularly when a session is devoted to case analysis. It
is your responsibility to catch up on any material you miss by reading the course material and borrowing
class-notes from others.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Assignments
There will be 2 programming assignments. Assignments will be posted on Moodle, and the submission is
also through Moodle. All deadlines are at 11:59pm of the submission day.
Getting help: You can visit the instructor and the TAs during student hours as well as set up an
appointment over email. Regarding help from fellow students, see the note on collaboration below.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Exams/Quizzes\nThere will be a Final exam for the course.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
There will be no make up for missed exams.
Note on Assignments and Exams: Midterm exams are comprehensive and cumulative. Last
minute conflicts and illnesses will be handled by rearranging the weights of the assignments to
reflect the missed work. (Weights will be moved to the other assignments ONLY for illnesses or
other unanticipated conflicts. Missing a homework/case assignment or quiz (without any reason)
will lead to zero credit for that part and the weight will not be shifted). You cannot skip midterm
exams – if you do you will get zero for the exam– the weight from the exam will not be moved to
the other components. There will be no makeup exams or assignments for this course, except
only under extraordinary circumstances, such as natural disasters.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Project
By second week of classes, you’ll choose a team and work on a course project. You will select a
business problem of your interest, and write a python application to solve the business problem you
have identified.
Check-in: You’ll check-in with instructor in the mid of the module to show the progress. This should be
an in-person meeting.
Evaluation: You’ll prepare a 5 minute presentation and a demo to showcase your project. It will be
evaluated by weighted voting by instructor and the class.
Teams: The Case Study can be done in groups of 2-3 students. We do not recommend single-member
teams given the amount of workload. You will sign up for groups through shared spreadsheet.
Submitting: You are asked to submit a single zip file, composed of all your code on or before university
scheduled final exam.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Participation
Each class will typically have a lecture component along with in-class activities such as mini case studies or
quizzes. Attending classes is highly beneficial, as active participation enhances the learning experience by
facilitating real-time interaction, discussions, and engagement with course material. Moreover, you get
class participation points (10% of your grade), rewarding students who consistently contribute to the
collaborative learning environment.

Important Policies and Resources
Late Assignment Policy
• No delay penalties, for medical/family/etc. emergencies (with written documentation, like
doctor's note).
• Each student is given ‘2 slip days’ total for the whole course duration, to accommodate for
coinciding deadlines/interviews/etc. That is, no questions asked, if the total delay is 2 days or less.
o You can use the extension on any assignment during the course (unless otherwise stated).
For instance, you can hand in one assignment 2 days late, or 2 different assignments 1 day
late each.
o Late days are rounded up to the nearest integer. For example, a submission that is 4
hours late will count as 1 day late.
o After you have used up your slip days, any assignment handed in late will be marked off
25% per day of delay.
• To use slip days:
o upload your solutions on Moodle to mark the time of submission
o You can upload your modified files multiple times at different points in time. However,
please note that we will use your latest upload date as the date of submission, even if you
have modified only a small part of your files.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Collaboration Policy
You are encouraged to discuss assignment problems with your fellow students. However, the work you
submit must be your own. You must acknowledge in your submission any help received on your
assignments. That is, you must include a comment in your homework submission that clearly states the
name of the student, book, or online reference from which you received assistance.
Submissions that fail to properly acknowledge any help from other students or non-class sources will
receive NO credit. Copied work will receive NO credit. Any and all violations will be reported.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Using Generative AI: I strongly recommend collaborating with fellow students when approaching
assignments, as opposed to exclusively depending on resources like ChatGPT, Gemini, or similar services.

While these tools can offer assistance, they cannot replicate the valuable learning journey that comes
from engaging directly with your assignments. If you find it necessary to utilize such tools, then you must
incorporate both the questions/prompts you posed to the AI agent and the corresponding responses as
an appendix to your assignment solution. This will greatly assist me in comprehending the progress of
your analytical reasoning as it develops throughout the duration of the course.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Bienvenue en Français 10 ! Welcome to French 10!
Welcome to your Beginning French class. This course will introduce
you to the basics of a language rich in history, spoken on all five
continents, and now one of the fastest-growing languages in the world.
As of 2022, there are reportedly around 321 million speakers of
French worldwide.1 Learning French will introduce you to a number of
foreign cultures and also broaden your range of real-world, practical
skills: French is an official working language of many international
institutions such as the United Nations, the European Union, and
FIFA – just to name a few.
In this course, you will learn about many French-speaking
cultures around the globe through music, cinema, dialogues,
and of course, the written word.
Our hope is that the skills that you develop in this course will stay with
you for years to come. Whether you are learning French to travel and
explore different corners of the world, to read texts and understand
films in their original versions, or to communicate with a broader range
of people, this course will help establish the foundation of your
journey.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
CONTACT INFORMATION
Professor: Loïc Lerme
Email: llerme@brandeis.edu
Office: Rabb 339a
Student Hours: Monday & Wednesday 1:20pm-2:15pm, and by appointment (Zoom or in person)
Class communication: I will communicate important course updates through MOODLE or
Brandeis email. Sign-ups for interviews will be organized on a Shared Google Doc. Please be sure
to check your email every day before class.
BUGS TUTOR: The French tutor will be Naomi Stephenson (naomistephenson@brandeis.edu)
Inclement weather policy: If there is a campus closure/snow day/delayed start/early closing, class
may be cancelled, so please check your email.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
COURSE MATERIAL
-Textbook (electronic): Promenades (see instructions below on how to buy the textbook along with
the workbook and how to access the “supersite”/electronic platform)
-Documents, videos, worksheets, links posted on MOODLE.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: If you have difficulty purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your Student\nFinancial Services or Academic Services advisor to discuss possible funding options and alternative\nsolutions.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
MOODLE
It is essential that you consult MOODLE regularly for the following resources:
1. weekly schedule (“Programme”) of deadlines for homework, assignments, assessments, readings
2. PDFs and links to various assignments
3. announcements from your professor
4. any other information deemed necessary for this course and the benefit of your learning process
(such as cultural events you can/must attend)
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
LEARNING GOALS
Goals: Core Skills
This course is designed to help you learn French at a basic, survival level and to introduce you to
different countries and cultures. You will speak and write in French to express yourself in
interpersonal situations (where you are exchanging information with other people) and in
presentational situations (where you are delivering information to others). You will also be able to
explain main ideas and key words in French materials that you read and hear (interpretive
communication). You will explore cultural aspects of the French-speaking world (intercultural
2

investigation) and learn to interact appropriately in basic situations with French speakers
(intercultural interaction).
During class, you will be asked to communicate in French to the best of your
abilities. Do not be discouraged by errors, challenges, or corrections – this is an
important part of the process. It is expected that you will make mistakes, and
indeed, if you are not making mistakes and speaking in perfect French, this is not
the right class for you!
By the end of the course, you will be able to:
• greet others and introduce yourself in a culturally appropriate manner.
• ask simple questions to clarify information given to you.
• describe your daily habits, hobbies, and interests, and ask questions to others about the
same.
• recognize and produce fundamental sounds of French phonetics, pronunciation.
• understand the main ideas and some short parts of dialogue in movies.
• identify key words and verb tenses used in song lyrics.
• express yourself by writing and speaking in short, simple sentences.
You will also be able to perform essential everyday tasks, such as:
o extend, accept, and refuse social invitations.
o order a meal at a restaurant.
o go shopping for clothes.
o pay for groceries.
o discuss the weather.
o describe past events in general terms.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Goals: Knowledge
You will come to be familiar with targeted elements of the French
speaking world, including:
• cultural customs and expectations in everyday situations
• key points in the history of France and former French colonies
• French and Francophone personalities including visual artists, musicians, entrepreneurs,
politicians, and chefs.
We want your knowledge to last for years and remain relevant to your goals and interests. The
knowledge and skills you gain in this course can enrich your academic pursuits in history,
literature, music, anthropology, or film. Learning French can also support your career goals,
whether they are related to the arts, to public service, politics, or to science. Or you may become a
life-long fan of French or Francophone cinema or music! Whatever path you choose, your
knowledge of French can open many exciting possibilities.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Social justice, attitudes, and behaviors
Learning a foreign language such as French will enable you to gain a deeper
understanding of different regions of the world and support your participation as
informed citizens in a global society.
This course will specifically:
• expand your knowledge and awareness of traditions and artistic expressions of French and
Francophone cultures.
• encourage you to reflect on your culture and weigh its similarities and differences against those
of other cultures.
• better understand what connects people worldwide – linguistically, culturally, and otherwise –
through study of texts, films, and music.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
COURSE STRUCTURE
We will cover the first seven chapters of Promenades. At the end of every 2 chapters, there will be
one oral and one written assessment.
Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum
of nine hours of study time per week in preparation for class (flash cards, grammar activities,
reading and listening comprehension activities, discussion boards).
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
ATTENDANCE
Learning to speak another language demands regular in-class participation. Your attendance counts
for 10% of your final grade. You are allowed four absences for any reason* (this includes sick
days); no justification or explanation will be required. However, each absence that exceeds the
allowed number will result in a lower grade in two ways: specifically, each additional absence will
result both a lowered attendance grade (-10% of the attendance grade per absence over four) and
1% subtracted from the final course grade. Eight or more absences will result in a meeting with
your professor and the Director of Languages to ascertain whether you can continue in the class,
and it may result in a failing course grade.
Students in situations that will require them to be absent for an extended period (more than the
allowed number of absences) should contact their Academic Adviser; the Adviser will contact your
professor about excused absences.
*Absence due to accommodations, sports activities or religious obligations will be considered
separately. If you must be absent from class for those reasons, please let me know in advance.
Merci !
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
PARTICIPATION
Your class participation, preparation and engagement are crucial to your success in this course. In-
class participation grading factors include:
- Do you arrive to class on time and ready to start work? (*phones put away during class time)
- Do you speak in French to the best of your ability during class activities? (*frequently used
questions memorized)
- Are you engaged throughout class, or do you leave for extended periods of time?
Out-of-class participation grading factors include:
-Do you prepare homework so that you are familiar with new vocabulary and grammar?
-Do you complete cultural readings/watch videos and prepare comments in French about them?
-Do you reach out to your instructor with questions, are you proactive when absent from class?
-Did you attend a French-related campus activity?
I will give you a participation grade out of 10 at the end of each week and will average these at the
end of the semester.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
HOMEWORK
Daily homework is posted on MOODLE.
There will be 2 types of homework in this class:
1) daily homework to complete BEFORE coming to class, usually consisting in some reading
alongside some activities to complete. Each reading for class is not particularly lengthy BUT
requires 2 things: a) some consistent effort to commit words and concepts to memory, and b)
willingness to work on pronunciation (speaking out loud). Practicing with a classmate, tutor, or
friend will also help immensely.
This course is based on a flipped-classroom model. You will interact with new information first as
homework, usually a short video tutorial or reading from the e-textbook, and then in class. Class is
conducted entirely in French, so it is essential to complete homework on time to be able to
participate. I will not correct this homework individually, but your preparation will help you
participate in class and ensure a high participation grade.
TIP: If you find the homework difficult, be proactive in contacting me or the BUGS tutor, so that
we can work together to find solutions. We are always happy to help.
2) end-of-chapter homework (GRADED). This homework will be submitted on the Promenades
supersite (part of the Vista Higher Learning platform) as a bundle at the end of each chapter.
These homework assignments are graded on: 1) completion on time; 2) accuracy of your answers.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Here are the details on your homework submissions:
- the Promenades supersite allows you to have 2 attempts at each submission of the chapter
homework
- after the first attempt, the platform will provide you with feedback on your errors so that
you can correct them/edit your answers BEFORE submitting them a second (final) time
- Late submissions: You are allowed to submit your homework late BUT for each additional
day, your homework grade for the chapter will be lowered by 5% (IT’S GONNA HURT!)
- Please consider this homework as part of each chapter’s review before the quiz. Therefore,
5

do not wait till the last minute to complete it.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
ASSESSMENTS
At the end of every 2 chapters, you will complete 1 writing and 1 speaking assessment to gauge
how are you doing with regard to the 4 core skills (writing, listening, reading and speaking). These

understanding a short dialogue. There will be a final cultural presentation scheduled in groups
during the last week of classes and a final written exam during the finals period. Please note that
you may not miss an assessment except in the case of an emergency. All assessments are taken in
person.

completing two courses beyond the language requirement (FREN 32)

of language proficiency and cultural understanding in French beyond the Brandeis Core
requirement, and it officially commends you on your efforts to expand and maintain your

spans.text:
IMPORTANT COURSE POLICIES
Academic honesty
You are expected to be familiar with, and to follow, the University’s policies on academic integrity. You
are expected to be honest in all your academic work. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Allegations of alleged
academic dishonesty will be forwarded to Student Rights and Community Standards. Sanctions for
academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension from the university. Citation and
research assistance can be found on the university library website.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students, and
I want to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability accommodations,
you will need to work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can contact them at 781-736-
3470, access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student Accessibility Support home page. You can find
helpful student FAQs and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know
whether you might be eligible for support from SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as
you can so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class, and so that
we can discuss a plan for the semester. In order to coordinate exam accommodations, ideally you
should provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an exam.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Respectful environment
Brandeis University is committed to providing its students, faculty, and staff with an environment
conducive to learning and working, where all people are treated with respect and dignity. Please
refrain from any behavior toward members of our Brandeis community, including students,
faculty, staff, and guests, that intimidates, threatens, harasses, or bullies.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Cell phone use
Cell phone use during class is a distraction. I rely on your maturity to keep your devices turned off
and put away for the duration of class. Using devices during class time or leaving class to use them
will result in a lower participation grade.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
AI generative tools
Taking a language course requires a special commitment to academic integrity. Our goal in this course is for
you to learn to speak, read, listen and write in French; using translation tools or other means in which
others do the work of this course for you is an obstacle to learning.
Effective learning requires you to work through difficulties that you may have in expressing your ideas and
in understanding what you hear and read. Effective learning requires you to use resources to figure out
8

solutions in order to better internalize them so you can mentally retrieve them and use them in the future.
All your coursework must be your own work. Help (such as editing, correcting, composing, or writing an
assignment) from anyone outside this course (a tutor, fellow student, friend, or relative), from a website,
from an online translation tool (such as Google Translate) or from an auto-correction tool (available for
example on Google Docs) is not permitted. Assignments that are not the product of your own work will not
be accepted.
Moreover, using phrases, ideas, or even fragments from undocumented Internet sources, native speakers,
or other “helpers” such as artificial intelligence (such as Chat GPT) and Chatbots in this course will be
considered cheating.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
For good quality vocabulary help, I suggest using online dictionaries such as:
- Lexilogos.com
- Wordreference.com
For grammar practice, I suggest www.francaisfacile.com

doc_id: 261BUS 297C 1 Leadership Internships in Social Impact Organizations Ian Roy
course_name: 261BUS 297C 1 Leadership Internships in Social Impact Organizations Ian Roy

spans.text:
Contact Details
Professor: Ian Roy
Email: ianroy@brandeis.edu
Office in the Library, Farber 2
Meeting Times
Classes
Wednesday 5:40-7:00PM - Lemnberg 54 Classroom
Office Hours
Office hours are by appointment. Use email or the booking link below
https://bit.ly/meetwithianroy
We can always meet on zoom, in my office, in the Lemberg Building, or in one of the BDI Labs
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description and Objectives
Course Description
“Real-world challenges. Real community partners. Leadership through action.”
Leadership in Social Impact Innovation is a two-semester course offering a total of 4 academic
credits, 2 each semester. Both semesters are required, not optional. The fall class is a traditional
academic offering with a weekly class meeting time in addition to the board fellow assignment.
The spring term is centered on experiential work, both the amplification of board fellow role
and participation in Deis Hacks, our 48 hour hackathon for social good.
This experiential learning graduate level class provides an opportunity for students to
individually assume high level strategic, tactical and/or leadership assignments in social impact
organizations. Students may become a non-voting board fellow as part of their assignment
and/or may be providing board level strategic and tactical consulting, depending on the nature
of the host organization and the scope of each student’s particular assignment. All
opportunities will provide a forum for students to develop and amplify their own unique brand
of high stakes contributions to real world social impact organizations. A limited number of
undergraduate business majors will be accepted into the class.
As part of their work, students will be exposed to high level organizational as well as business
leadership from the community. Most partnership organizations will be not for profits, but some
may be social for profit enterprises and there may also be foundations and other social funding
assignments. Students will emerge from this experience with a unique multifaceted lens into
the social innovation sector and their own personal abilities to impact entities, whether it be
commercial or not for profit oriented, at a high stakes level.

Each student will be paired with a social impact organization with a somewhat defined
responsibility. Through class discussions as well as individual mentorship with the instructor and
organization leadership, his or her assignment will be developed and advanced. The objective
is to not function as an intern but rather, tackle a challenge with potential strategic impact and
tactical feasibility at a senior level. Students will provide weekly communication to their host
organization as well as the professor as to their activities.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Participation in this course is by application only, to apply go to
https://students.global.brandeis.edu/register/Field_Project_Fall_2025
A phone interview may be a part of the selection process. Class is limited to 18 participants,
either at the undergraduate or graduate level.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
The primary student outcome is to gain high-level leadership, strategy and tactical experience
in real world organizations. As part of this class, students will be tackling roles rooted in one or
more of the academic tracks of marketing, real estate, entrepreneurship, finance and data
analytics. All assignments will be with organizations striving for social impact. The course counts
as elective credits for the Brandeis School of Business and Economics MBA and undergraduate
business degree and this course will fulfill the general work practicum requirement for the MA
students. In addition, with the approval of the relevant program director, this course may also
fulfill the work practicum and capstone requirement of the Real Estate, Data Analytics and
Marketing concentrations of the MA.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Credit Hours/Workload Statement
Success in this two-term, four- credit course is based on the expectation that students will
spend in addition to the weekly class time of 1 hour and 20 minutes in the fall term, another
4-5 hours weekly developing their work and in meetings with their host organizations.
In the spring semester, the weekly work around the board fellow assignment expands and there
is a heavy concentration of strategic and leadership involvement in preparation for and during
Deis Hacks. There will not be weekly class meeting during this term but there will however be
several boot camp type sessions and one on one mentoring times with Professor Miller during
the semester.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Learning Goals and Outcomes
▪ Opportunity to utilize classroom learnings in a real-world experience that focuses on
individual professional and personal growth
▪ Chance to be individually responsible for a high stakes role assignment with strategic
impact
▪ Class will function as a cohort and will include cross training and collaboration among
students
▪ Responsibilities will include a presentation(s) to the organization’s board and
management
▪ Exposure for students to high level decision-making for an organization
1

▪ Opportunity to advance personal leadership skills as well as tactics and strategy around
real-world challenges, opportunities and problems.
▪ Develop comfort level with functioning around the high-level leadership in
organizations that will likely include the board room.
▪ Enhance students’ resumes and contacts with experience working with real
organizations
▪ This responsibility can be noted on your resume with the organization’s name but be
careful to state your role and not suggest or state you were an employee of the
sponsoring organization. It provides very interesting topics to discuss at a job interview
and distinguishes you from the competition.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Prerequisites\nMaturity, self-motivation, desire to succeed and strong communication abilities in English.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Plan
This class differs in many ways from the conventional course, which will create a number of
challenges during the semesters. In particular, unlike the traditional class with its well-defined
student-instructor role, you must deal with multiple people relationships. First, my role is more
of a manager and mentor than a traditional lecturer and, depending on how well the
assignment is progressing, I will have anywhere from moderate to heavy involvement in your
assignment. You must also deal with your cohort class members who may have different ideas
and work styles. You will also have the sponsor host organization, with multiple people playing
different roles relating to yours. Your success in this course will depend on your ability to rise to
the challenge presented, operating independently, but with mentorship and high-level
guidance from the instructor.
In taking this class you will experience a number of real-world frustrations, including: Lack of
complete role clarity
▪ Delays in communications and deadlines
▪ Changing assignment scope: bigger, narrower, or different
▪ Organizations can be unsure of what they are seeking from your role
▪ Your assignment may not always seem important to your host
▪ Constituents not responding to calls and emails and requests
▪ Organization sponsors or leaders leave their organizations
I want to assure you that this is the reality of both the business and social sector worlds.
Leaders are very busy and, in some cases, various individuals in the client organization might
be in survival mode to keep their jobs. It is essential that you apply personal motivation and
individual leadership to your work – develop your path and demonstrate your impact.
This course will expose you to these real-world leadership/management challenges and
frustrations, and more importantly, teach you how to rise up to meet them. For example, you
will learn how persistence and diplomacy is central to functioning at the right level.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Requirements
Academic Integrity
You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Students may be
required to submit work to TurnItinl.com software to verify originality. Allegations of alleged
academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the director of academic integrity. Sanctions for
academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension from the university. Citation
and research assistance can be found on the university library website.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Communications
All communication outside of class will take place via email and LATTE. If you have another
student working with your non-profit board, please discuss with one another which platform
you are most comfortable using to communicate.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Assignments
All weekly class assignments will be posted on LATTE at the beginning of each week.
The on-going project assignments with your host organization might involve a variety of
high-level organizational challenges and opportunities, such as:
▪ Developing a social media strategy and plan to advance organizational impact and
foster community engagement
▪ Construct and early adoption of analytics for measuring an organization’s impact
▪ Anchoring the strategic plan for board leadership to achieve its objectives
▪ Assuming a role in shaping the institutional advancement of an organization to attract
increased resources
▪ Design and institute financial modeling for organizations with an asset development
orientation
▪ Constructing a strategic partnership between organizations to expand impact
▪ Real estate support to include acquisitions/dispositions, tax credits and advancement of
affordable housing programming
The assignments/projects will expose students to a wide array of organizational leadership
challenges that are found in all types of entities. Sponsoring organizations will range in size
from small and local to larger and multi-regional. They may be social enterprises organized as
commercial entities or social sector (not for profit) organizations. The scope of student activities
will include meetings at the organization and may include a non-voting board fellow role
depending on the host organization. Students will be individually accountable however, there
may be another student tackling a different role for the same sponsor organization.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: During the course of the year the fellows will assume a leadership role in a social impact\nhackathon in conjunction with the Brandeis MakerLab. DeisHacks will be held January 30-31st,\n2025 and participation is a requirement of the course.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
A high degree of maturity and self-motivation are important prerequisites for acceptance into
the program. Additionally, assignments will require extensive communication within an
organization and its community. Accordingly, English language skills and a strong comfort level
with social interactions at various levels are critical.
Participation
Attendance and active participation in class is essential. Interaction with the class cohort is an
important aspect of a successful experience and students will be expected to demonstrate
collaborative approach, often cross training one another in this course. Individual meeting
times with organizations will be situationally tailored to the host’s meeting schedule and are
critical to a successful engagement. Participation in the social impact two-week hackathon is
also a requirement of the class. The class time will include of a combination of class meetings
as well as individual meetings with the instructor. During both terms, there will be a series of
guest presentations from social entrepreneurs, not for profit executives as well as business
leaders active in board leadership. Students must attend class during the scheduled class time
with video cameras on (if attending virtually) , unless pre-approved by the professor (different
time-zone, etc).
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Portfolio\nPlease submit all weekly reflections in their respective folders on Moodle. All other material\nshould be submitted via Moodle unless stated otherwise.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Evaluation
The individual student grades will be based on contribution/participation, deliverables, host
feedback, extra effort, and work product.
35%: DELIVERABLES (QUALITY AND TIMELINESS)
▪ Development of project timeline
▪ Overall project for host organization
▪ Self-reflections/evaluation and weekly updates and weekly assignments
30%: FEEDBACK FROM HOST ORGANIZATION
▪ Timeliness
▪ Detail
▪ Initiative
▪ Follow through
▪ Creativity
30%: CLASS, BOOTCAMPS and HACKATHON ATTENDANCE AND PARTICIPATION
▪ Leadership in class
▪ Sharing ideas with class colleagues
▪ Initiative and engagement
5 %: EFFORT ABOVE AND BEYOND WHAT IS ANTICIPATED
▪ The individual student grade will consider such factors as: did the student demonstrate
motivation, commitment and collaboration, arrive on time to every class; did the
student demonstrate resourcefulness and creativity in generating ideas and strategies to
pursue the ideas; did the student meet deadlines; did the student demonstrate critical
4

thinking; did the student demonstrate mastery of the subject matter by the end of the
project; did the student effectively participate in class; was; did the student make
effective presentations.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Expectations
The following is an elaboration of what the instructor expects from student teams and how
grades will be determined (note that the first two items are of paramount importance to client
satisfaction and avoiding getting a low grade). Remember, in this course you are each an
ambassador of the Brandeis School of Business and Economics, the client organization you are
representing and yourselves – so always leave favorable impressions.
▪ Error-free work
o facts, figures, and spreadsheets must be error-free. The instructor and - - in
particular - - the host organization must not become the quality control
department. Consistent errors in work will lead to a loss of confidence and a low
grade in the course.
▪ Professional conduct
o You must maintain a high level of professionalism in undertaking your work
including: maintaining client confidentiality; showing up for meetings and
conference calls on time; dressing professionally and appropriately for meetings;
and fulfilling commitments (e.g., deadlines and deliverables).
▪ Self-motivation
o Students should be eager to pursue their role and project work as a matter of
self-interest in helping the organization as well as expanding their own personal
leadership growth. Accordingly, students should be self-motivated to work
throughout both terms pursuing deeper impact.
▪ Independence
o Your ability to work self-sufficiently is critical. To the extent that the scope of
work requires instructor input - - as most do - - then instructor/student
brainstorming is important, with the student then working independently week
to week.
▪ Team orientation
o A collaborative approach is an essential aspect of this class. Students are
expected to collaborate and share learning with one another through
discussions and presentations.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Essential Resources
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to welcome and include all students. If you are a student who needs
accommodations as outlined in an accommodations letter, please talk with me and present
your letter of accommodation as soon as you can. I want to support you. In order to provide
test accommodations, I need the letter more than 48 hours in advance. I want to provide your
accommodations, but cannot do so retroactively. If you have questions about documenting a
5

disability or requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility Support at
781-736-3470 or access@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Course Materials/Readings
Unlike most classes, this class does not have any exams. Therefore, active participation is
required to thrive in our class setting.
Materials recommended for purchase, including textbooks, case packets and online materials.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
The costs of textbooks and other course materials can present financial challenges for many of
our students. Please consider ways you can reduce costs for your students and promote
student success while complying with copyright law. Submit course reserve requests: Course
reserves help ensure that all students have access to materials. If you have extra copies of any
course texts, we encourage you to place these on reserve at the library for the semester.
Explore open educational resources and affordable materials with a librarian: OER are learning
resources such as articles, books, audio recordings, images, video, software and digital tools
that instructors can reuse, revise and redistribute. Contact Laura Laura Hibbler associate
university librarian for research and instruction, to discuss possibilities for reducing the expense
of course materials for your students through the use of OER and materials available through
library subscriptions. You are also strongly encouraged to add this sentence to your syllabus: "If
you are having difficulty purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your
student financial services or academic services adviser to discuss possible funding options
and/or textbook alternatives.

spans.text:
Privacy
This class requires the use of tools that may disclose your coursework and identity to parties
outside the class. To protect your privacy, you may choose to use a pseudonym/alias rather
than your name in submitting such work. You must share the pseudonym/alias with me and any
teaching assistants as needed. Alternatively, with prior consultation, you may submit such work
directly to me.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
AI use in class
Because this class is about innovation, I think it is key to leverage AI in meaningful ways. This
will be the future of work, and I think it is essential that you develop the skills to set yourself
apart from your peers by leveraging emerging technologies. However, for writing assignments
where you are asked to write in your own words, such as weekly updates, reflections, and
reports it is essential that you write it yourself and not use an AI to write it. You can use AI as a
sounding board, or as a critic to review and improve your writing - as long as you do the
critical thinking first. For example, It is okay if you send your writing to an AI and ask it
something like: “Can you critique how my writing reads to a non subject matter expert?” or
“What questions should I be considering that I have not covered on this topic?” - that is
appropriate use of AI. As long as it is your voice and not a copy/paste of AI content, it can be a
useful research tool. This is the essential skill we want to encourage and develop - but for this
skill to work, you have to do the critical thinking first.
spans.label: INTEGRITY
