spans.text:
Course Description
Skills and knowledge
Combining lectures, workshops, and screenings, this course examines the fundamentals of writing for
situation comedy and episodic drama. The course will examine story structure, characterization, scene
writing, and dialogue. Students will move from ideation to writing a detailed outline and the first draft
of an original pilot script.
This class is both workshop and lecture. You will read scripts from existing television series, pitch ideas
for pilots, and write frequently. Assignments will be reviewed in class, meaning you will receive
criticism on your pages, as well as critique classmates' work.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Learning Goals
This course is designed to provide you with the tools necessary to develop and write your television
pilot. By the end of the class, you will be able to:
1. Display an understanding of effective pitching skills.
2. Create a 3-7 act beat sheet for your entire teleplay.
3. Write a complete teleplay for an original pilot.
4. Develop a teleplay that includes the following key elements:
• The three-act structure
• Character arc and story arc
• Visual storytelling
• Beat outline
• Script format
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text: Class preparation time\nOn average, you can expect to spend 4-6 hours a week preparing for class. This may be through writing\nscript pages, reading the textbook and additional readings, or watching assigned television programs.
spans.label: DESCRIP

Every member of the University community is expected to maintain the highest standards of academic

may include failure on the assignment, failure in the course, suspension from the University or other

TurnItIn.com software to verify originality.
A student who is in doubt regarding standards of academic honesty as they apply to a specific course
or assignment should consult the faculty member responsible for that course or assignment before
submitting the work. Allegations of alleged academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the Department
of Student Rights and Community Standards. Citation and research assistance can be found at Brandeis
Library Guides - Citing Sources (https://guides.library.brandeis.edu/c.php?g=301723).

spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Overview of the Course
Private equity (PE) funds have a sizeable and growing influence on worldwide capital
markets. In this course, we examine the PE industry, with a particular focus on the US
Leveraged Buyout industry – why has it been so successful at attracting capital, and
what do firms do to generate returns for themselves and their investors. That includes
not only finance, but also the strategic and operational factors that create a successful
investment. Topics include:
● The Private Equity industry – its history, structure, players and adjacent
industries
● What kind of company makes an attractive private equity investment?
● How do private equity funds add value to their investments (operational
improvement, management incentives, balance sheet)
● What do private equity funds do when ‘things go wrong’?
● How do private equity funds create successful exits from their investments?
The course is designed to be relevant not only to students considering a career in
private equity, but to those in related careers: investment banking, commercial banking,
fund placement and line management of leveraged companies.
Learning Goals
At the conclusion of this course, the student should be able to understand:
● Why has PE become such a major force in global capital markets?
● How does the PE industry work?
● What are the common themes that define and create PE successes?
● What do PE firms do when ‘things go wrong?’
● What are the differences and commonalities between leveraged buyouts and
venture capital (VC)?
● How can I make better lending and investment decisions by thinking like a PE
pro?
Success in this two-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a
minimum of 9 hours of study time per week in preparation for class (readings, papers,
modeling exercises, preparation for exams, etc.)
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Materials:
The case studies in this course are all provided for free on LATTE. In addition to class
discussions and case study preparation, students are required to complete the Wall
Street Prep LBO modeling modules. The Wall Street Prep materials cost $100. You can
sign up using
https://gv142.infusionsoft.com/app/manageCart/addProduct?productId=8021
[gv142.infusionsoft.com]
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
The final exam for the course is a modeling exercise based on the Wall Street Prep
materials.
Prerequisites: FIN 202a and FIN 212a or equivalents. The course assumes that you
have a working knowledge of accounting and financial ratios. I would strongly urge
you to retake this course at a later date if you do not have a sufficient finance,
accounting and financial statement analysis background.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Class Participation: Class participation is expected of everyone in this course, and
synchronous class attendance is required. If you have obligations that will require you
to miss more than one class during the term, you should not enroll in this class. You are
permitted one absence for no reason (i.e. it does not need to be excused) and your TA
and I will keep a record of class participation. Value will be placed on the quality, not
the quantity, of your comments.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Perfect attendance but no participation receives a grade of “C+“ toward the overall\ngrade.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Contributions during Guest Speaker Sessions : You will be expected to ask relevant
questions during the guest speaker sessions. Please come prepared with questions in
advance (so you are ready to ask something if I prompt you). Also, during the session,
think of questions that relate to what the speaker is sharing.
Value will be placed on the quality, not the quantity, of your questions/contributions.
Grading in each speaker session is based on your best contribution for the day and is
not cumulative.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text: Short Assignments: All written assignments must be submitted on MOODLE.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: Individual assignments are to be completed with NO OUTSIDE ASSISTANCE – no data\nsources beyond what is provided in the case, no discussion with current or former class\nmembers – if you have questions or need help, you should talk to me or the TA only.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Short assignment answers will be graded on a three point scale with 0.5 points awarded
for an incomplete answer, 1 point for a basic answer, 2 points for a good
analysis/response, and 3 points for an excellent analysis/response (this is rare). A score
of 2 points across all questions translates into a 93 on a point scale.

spans.text:
Take-private deal proposal & short pitch. In recent years, Private Equity firms have
increasingly turned to public markets as a deal source. Rather than buying companies in
the private market, they instead choose to take a public company private. In 2022, for
instance, Vista Equity Partners and Elliott Management teamed up to acquire
cloud-computing company Citrix Systems. This short pitch will be graded as part of
your take-private deal proposal.
For this assignment you will be assigned (by Tues, Jan 30) to a team of 4-6 students.
Your team is required to put together a pitch deck presenting a take-private deal
proposal to the investment committee at your private equity firm. The deck should
include approximately 12 - 18 slides (plus appendix materials if needed). Include the
following points:
● Company to be acquired and acquisition price range
● Industry overview
○ What makes this industry attractive for PE?
○ Competitor analysis
○ Industry trends
○ Drivers, Risks
● Comparable deals (comps), if applicable
● Value creation strategy/Deal Thesis
○ There are many different ways to create value. What makes most sense
here?
4

■ Are there any comparables, i.e. other companies that have pursued
similar or related strategies? Other PE deals in this sector that you
think provide guideposts for what can be done?
● Exit strategy
● LBO model with assumptions
● MOIC and IRR calculations
Your team will also prepare a no more than 7
minute pitch to present in class. You can designate two team members to present.
Choose 3-5 slides from your full pitch deck to present the highlights and key points
underlying your idea.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Class Sessions
WED, JAN 15
Introduction to Private Equity Funds
In this introductory lecture we will answer the following questions, which will provide
the foundation for our remaining sessions:
o Why do we have a ‘Private Equity’ industry? Where did it come from and what
is its unique role in capital markets? How does it differ from other family run or
other private businesses?
5

o What is the structure of the global Leveraged Buyout (LBO) industry? How is
the same and how is it different in major geographies around the world?
o Where does Private Equity money come from? Why do limited partners invest?
What are they trying to achieve?
o Given the above, what are the motivations for a PE investor? Where are they
convergent and divergent vs. their limited partners and financing sources?
TUES (Brandeis Monday), JAN 21
What makes an attractive Private Equity Deal?
Case: Hubbardton Forge (A) (via MOODLE)
<> Hubbardton Forge LBO model due <>
Bunker Hill Capital is considering an acquisition of Hubbardton Forge, a lighting
company. Is Hubbardton Forge a “good business”? What valuation would you put on
the business? What are your primary risks and concerns?
We will introduce the class to ‘LBO math’, using a real LBO deal as an example,
answering the following:
o What types of businesses make attractive LBO investments and why?
o How does LBO financing produce such high returns?
o What are the similarities and differences between an attractive LBO and an
attractive corporate M&A transaction?
We will also establish the basic ‘Private Equity Success Formula’ that we will explore in
subsequent classes, using the example of Hubbardton Forge:
Buy Right + Add Value + Exit Well = Attractive PE Returns
WED, JAN 22
Jon Ginns, ACON Investments - Guest Speaker
MON, JAN 27
Add Value & Growth Equity - Exit or No Exit?
Case: Marlin Outdoor (via MOODLE)
We will go over the Marlin Outdoor case == Birmingham Capital has the opportunity to
take a quick gain on its Marlin Outdoor investment. Should it sell now, or hold on for
the hope of a higher return later? Read the case and come to class prepared to discuss.
6

WED, JAN 29
Add Value Approaches / Strategics
We will explore how private equity funds add value to their portfolio companies,
discussing:
o How are PE firms organizing themselves to add value to their portfolios?
o What are the most important tools that a PE owner will use to add value?
o How can acquisitions add value to a portfolio company?
o How do PE firms incent their managers to add value?
o What governance structures do funds employ to provide oversight to their
portfolio company investments?
MON, FEB 3
Hugh MacArthur, Bain & Company PE practice - Guest Speaker
WED, FEB 5
Exit or Add Value?
Case: Construction Partners (via MOODLE)
<> Construction Partners writeup due <>
Guest Speaker: Mike McKay, Bain and Company. CPI has been an awesome
investment, but – is it time to exit? Or recap? Or IPO? Wow – so many choices!
MON, FEB 10
Buy and Build / Dividend Recaps
The need to exit and the positioning for exit is a critical difference between a PE buyer
and a corporate buyer. We will address the following:
o What are the major opportunities for exit, and when is each most appropriate?
o What are the specific things that a private equity owner will do to position a
portfolio company for attractive sale?
o When is the ‘optimal’ time for exit? How do PE funds manage the ongoing
tension between ‘declaring victory’ and getting cash back to investors vs.
continuing to compound capital and hope for a larger payday later?
7

What is a dividend recap, and when is it an appropriate part of an exit strategy?
WED, FEB 12
Riddhish Rege, JLC Infrastructure - Guest Speaker.
MON, FEB 24
Alumni Day / Take-private deal presentations
<> Full deal proposals due noon, today <>
Today, each team will have no more than 7 minutes to present their take-private deal
proposal. Use this time efficiently to emphasize what makes this a good deal. Please do
not run over the 7 minute maximum time allotment. Your grade will be reduced if you
do.
WED, FEB 26
Alumni Day / Take-private deal presentations continued
Today we will welcome a few Fin231 alumns who are now working in PE. They will
share their story and you will have an opportunity to ask questions about their work
and how they got to where they are.
MON, MAR 3
Venture Capital
Case: ShopKeep - Taking Back Mainstreet through Payments and Points of Sale. (via
MOODLE)
<> ShopKeep short answers due <>
Guest Speaker: Brian Hirsch, Tribeca Venture Capital
WED, MAR 5
Final Exam (70 minutes) - in class.

Linguistics 120b: Syntax I
Course Information and Syllabus — Wednesday, August 27, 2025
NOTE: This course was previously called Syntactic Theory. It has the same content as the prior course.
Class Meetings: Mon, Wed, & Thurs, 10:10-11:00am – Volen 119
Obligatory Recitation: Fridays 2:20-3:40pm – Volen 119
This is an in-person course.
Instructor: Lotus Goldberg (she/her) lmgold@brandeis.edu
Phone: (781) 736-3265 Mailbox: Volen 261 [Computer Science/Ling Office]
Office: Volen 252
• Office hours for the first week of classes:
– Thursday, August 28, 1:30-2:30pm (by appointment at calendly.com/lotusgoldberg)
– Friday August 29, 10:30-11:30am (drop in; no appointment needed!)
• Exceptionally, if you need to join office hours on Zoom, the link is here (Password 873256)
and on our Moodle page
Teaching Assistant: Dya Acharjee (he/they), acharjee@brandeis.edu
Group Address for Emailing All TA(s) + Prof. Goldberg: syntax-prof-and-tas-group@brandeis.edu
Regular Instructor & TA office hours times will be posted soon!
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
COURSE DESCRIPTION AND OBJECTIVES
This course provides an introduction to the process of syntactic analysis, to generative syntactic theory, and
to many major syntactic phenomena of English and other languages. Topics covered include a detailed
examination of the basic clausal architecture, the lexicon, and various types of syntactic movement,
focusing primarily on A-Movement (also called NP Movement)—including passives. We will finish with
an examination of a portion of English infinitival structures.
Evaluation of work in this course will be primarily through solutions (written up in full prose) to weekly
problem set assignments, some done individually and some done in small groups (read on for details!)—
plus longer take-home problem set assignments, done individually, which will serve as the Midterm and
Final Exam. Homework, Midterm, and Final Exam solutions will be evaluated equally on their empirical
and analytic content. Both should be laid out fully, explicitly, and clearly, in full-paragraph prose format.
Within the grading for analytic content, equal weight is given to the actual content of the proposal and to
the clarity and logic with which it is motivated and argued for. For full details, see the detailed guidelines
handouts for homework solutions and the sample assignment write-up to be given out during the first few
weeks of the term, and which we will refer to and discuss all semester.
Learning Goals
As a result of taking this course and fully engaging with the material and required work, students will:
• significantly deepen their understanding of the descriptive (rather than prescriptive) approach used in
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
linguistics; the sorts of knowledge that speakers have of the syntactic patterns in their native language,
usually without being aware of it; the defining empirical traits of English syntax, and of the major
syntactic phenomena of English; major ways in which syntactic structures vary from one language to
another;
• attain a solid foundation in the analytic approaches to natural language syntax used in modern
generative linguistics, including the fundamental tenets and assumptions of Chomskyan generative
syntactic theory (i.e. of generative theories of natural language syntax that use movement);
• strengthen and deepen their ability to identify and clearly articulate the patterns and generalizations
present in a given set of syntactic data; to formulate reasonable next questions to ask, or identify the
sort of additional examples that would need to be gathered and examined, in order to fully discover
the empirical generalizations present in a set of syntactic data, or to choose between competing
hypotheses about the data;
• develop and refine the ability to formulate well-reasoned hypotheses and analyses of the facts present
in a set of syntactic data; to demonstrate how well a given analysis does or does not work to fully
capture the facts that it aims to explain; to evaluate competing analyses as to which should be chosen
as the best account available, in terms of both empirical adequacy and theoretical merits;
• improve and deepen their ability to write clearly and concisely, in the scientific style of writing used
throughout linguistics (and in many other scientifically oriented fields)—with prose that is lucid,
logical, careful, precise, and complete, both in laying out its empirical descriptions and in proposing
and arguing for an analysis over plausible alternatives; this goal helps the course to satisfy the Writing
Intensive requirement for the Linguistics major.
These goals are advanced in all work for the course, and the grading criteria for each assignment are
designed to provide explicit feedback on each of these goals. This allows students to gradually build up
their strengths in each area as the semester proceeds.
For only the Computational Linguistics (CL) MS students enrolled in the course, additional learning goals
include:
• attain a general familiarity with how syntactic constructions learned in our class for English work in
an array other languages, and with some additional major constructions not studied in the regular part
of the course;
• begin thinking about some of the basic initial issues involved in implementing a syntactic generator or
parser for a natural language such as English in a computer programming language such as Python.
As graduate students, the enrolled CL MS students will have a slightly different workload for the
assignments, and will also attend several separate CL MS-only recitation meetings with content specifically
for them. This will be explained at the end of the first recitation meeting.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Credit hours and Workload
Success in this 4-credit course is based on the expectation that students will spend a minimum of 12 hours
of study time per week in preparation for class (going over your notes, working out solutions to the assigned
work, writing these solutions up, etc.). Note that this is just a minimum expectation of outside class time.
Like the other linguistics core courses beyond LING 100-Intro to Linguistics, this is a more intensive
course, and students typically spend a good deal of time on its assigned work: more than in the average
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Brandeis course, and more than in the non-core linguistics courses. It is important to plan for this each
week, and in your other course selections for this semester. Because of this course’s significant and rigorous
workload, it is not recommended for undergraduates to take 5 courses during the semester in which a student
takes Syntax I.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
Prerequisites
This course can be taken without completing specific prerequisites. However, for undergraduate students,
having taken and done well in LING 100–Introduction to Linguistics is strongly recommended. Students
who earned below a B+ or B in LING 100 and/or who struggled with its content will find that Syntax I goes
better if taken after first completing LING 110-Phonology I.
spans.label: ADMIN

Textbook/Readings, Other Class Materials, the Use of Moodle as Our Class’ Electric Home
There is no textbook nor outside class readings to buy or do, and, as explained below, it is not permitted
for you to find or use any. I will frequently give out handouts (in hard copy form in class and posted on
Moodle) that you do need to read and absorb.
Moodle will be the electric home for our course: all major course announcements will be posted on Moodle,
as well as emailed from Moodle to each individual student. All handouts and assignments will be posted
there. Coursework will be handed in by both uploading it to Moodle and either also uploaded to
Gradescope, or submitted in hard copy form; the details will be announced for each assignment.
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text: As noted above, there will be roughly one problem set due each week. We will then solve the assignment\n
spans.label: ASSIGN

together in class on the day due (generally on Wednesdays and Thursdays, for work due on a Wednesday),
decide on the best analysis of the data, and add that to a working class model of English syntax that we
gradually build up over the course of the term. You will then get the next assignment generally on
Thursdays (again with possible adjustments on weeks with holidays), and typically have 6 days to solve
it—nearly a full week. Explicit guidelines on how to do these assignment write-ups will be given out as
we proceed.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
When to Hand in Assignments
All assigned work is due at the start of class on its due date. No late work is accepted, since we will
discuss the content of the assignment in class on the day it is handed in. You are responsible for submitting
all assigned work by the due date and time, even if you are not in class that day, or arrive late. Please
contact me before an assignment is due if illness or other extraordinary circumstances prevent you from
handing in your work on time.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
A note for those with accommodations for limited extensions on assignment deadlines: for nearly all of the
assigned work in this course, the Student Accessibility Services office has explained that this
accommodation unfortunately does not apply, since nearly all are solved in class the day they are handed
in (and additionally, for the Group Exercise Assignments, since they are done as a group). The one
assignment for which this accommodation can be used is the Rewrite of Assignment 2, since it involves a
problem set that we will have already solved as a class. Please let me know if you have any questions.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Students who have time conflicts or delays due to religious observance, significant obligations to care for
family, student athletics, or have other significant personal issues are encouraged to contact me as soon as
they see a problem. However, the fact that there will be at least 6 days to complete each full assignment
will generally still give enough time to complete work, by planning accordingly.
spans.label: LATE

spans.text:
Take-Home Midterm and Final Exams
There will be a take-home Midterm Exam between half-way and two-thirds of the way through the course,
and a take-home Final Exam due during Finals Week. Both may have a short in-class component, and will
involve slightly longer problem sets than the usual assignments, but be solved and written up in the same
fashion. Therefore, the best ways to prepare for these exams and do well on them involve the same
things that will allow you to well in the course overall.
Main Recommendations for Doing Well in All Work for the Course
• keep up with the regular group and individual assignments in the course;
• carefully read any associated handouts;
• follow along with the class discussion of each assignment;
• carefully read the grading feedback we give you on each assignment;
• come to office hours when you have questions or need to better understand any of the above;
• incorporate what you learn into your future work, so that your skills and understanding grow and
develop as the course proceeds.
Submitting Work
Unless stated otherwise, each piece of assigned work is due at the start of class the day it is due (or other
stated deadline, if not due on a day when class or recitation meets), in two forms:
• Moodle submission is required for all assigned work (both group and individual)
• Additionally, it is required to submit the assignment either on Gradescope or as a hard copy. I

spans.text:
Laptops and Other Electronic Devices
It is very important to me that students who come to course meetings be able to be here—or watch a
recording—with full presence and attention, engaging as fully as possible with the class material and
discussions. Therefore, there is a strict policy that no laptops, phones, tablets, or other electronic devices
are allowed to be open or used during class, with the exception of tablets used solely for taking notes.
Devices used to take notes cannot be used for any other purpose. No apps are allowed to be open
other than the one being used to take notes. It is crucial to draw many syntactic trees in your notes, and
so being able to use a stylus if taking notes electronically is very helpful; typing does not work well.
Absolutely no web browsing, texting, or other device use is allowed during class, with no exceptions.
If you watch any class recordings, I also strongly recommend that you keep all programs and apps closed
besides what you use to watch the recording, have no other tabs or windows open in that program/app, and
take notes as you watch. This will allow you to absorb the content with greatest success.
One reason why taking notes by hand is so important in this course is that the detailed tree representations
of syntactic structures that we will be drawing all semester are done much faster and more accurately by
hand, and the exercise of drawing them by hand greatly speeds up and deepens your ability to understand
these structures. For this reason, drawing the trees in your assignment by hand (and despite typing the prose
in your assignments), rather than generating them electronically, is also strongly recommended.
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
I am interested in your solution, and the thought process that went in to it.
You are expected to be familiar with and follow the University’s policies on academic integrity. You are
expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Allegations of alleged
academic dishonesty will be forwarded to Student Rights and Community Standards. Sanctions for
academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension or withdrawal from the
university. Citation and research assistance can be found on the library website, but you will not cite work
since NO accessing of outside resources is allowed. If you have any questions about Brandeis’ rules, please
contact Alex Rossett (arossett@brandeis.edu), Assistant Dean, Student Rights and Community Standards.
Assignments must all be submitted to Compilatio software, to verify originality.
Absolutely No Use of ChatGPT or Any Other Language Generation Tools or Methods Allowed
This course’s Learning Goals include developing and refining your ability to write scientifically. Therefore,
all writing handed in must be your own, devised and carried out solely by you, from start to finish. The use
of any tools to create your work is strictly prohibited, severely violates the Brandeis academic integrity
policy, and will be forwarded to Student Rights and Community Standards. Such use also deprives you of
the learning opportunity to improve your writing abilities that this course offers.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
Seeking Support at Brandeis beyond Our Course
Brandeis University is committed to supporting all of our students so that they can thrive. Resources are
available to help with the many academic and non-academic factors that contribute to student success,
including finances, health, food supply, housing, mental health counseling, academic advising, and physical
and social activities. Please consult the Support at Brandeis webpage to find out more about the resources
that Brandeis provides to help you and your classmates to achieve success.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Accommodations
Brandeis seeks to create a learning environment that is welcoming and inclusive of all students, and I want
to support you in your learning. If you think you may require disability accommodations, you will need to
work with Student Accessibility Support (SAS). You can contact them at 781-736-3470, email them
at access@brandeis.edu, or visit the Student Accessibility Support home page. You can find helpful student
FAQs and other resources on the SAS website, including guidance on how to know whether you might be
eligible for support from SAS.
If you already have an accommodation letter from SAS, please provide me with a copy as soon as you can
so that I can ensure effective implementation of accommodations for this class. In order to coordinate exam
accommodations, ideally you should provide the accommodation letter at least 48 hours before an exam.
Again, as noted in the Assigned Work section above, accommodations for limited extensions on assignment
deadlines can only be used in this course on the Rewrite of Assignment 2 and the Final Exam, since all
other assigned work involves a problem set that will be solved in class the day it’s due (and the Group
Exercise Assignments, furthermore, involve group work).
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Academic Integrity and Plagiarism
As in some other Brandeis linguistics courses, you are allowed (and encouraged) to work on Individual
Assignments with other students in our class. If you do this, you must list their name(s) at the top of your
assignment, and you must then leave the group, and plan out and write up your answer entirely on your
own, all in your own words, in full adherence to the Brandeis policies on academic integrity.
This means that it is not permitted to share—whether by hard copy or electronically, e.g. via email or
Facebook—any portion of your written-up assignment or assignment draft with any other student(s).
Handing in work as your own that has been reworded from the work of another student is a violation of
academic honesty, and is not permitted. In this course, you are also not permitted to consult outside
books, readings, websites, or students outside our class for any reason whatsoever, including
regarding assigned work. I take violations of this policy very seriously. For each homework assignment,
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
listing of all office hour times, locations, and Zoom links can always be found at the top of our Moodle
homepage.
Contacting Us and Getting Help
The best way to contact us is either by dropping in to any office hour (no appointment needed!), or by
writing to our single group email for Prof. Goldberg and the TA(s), syntax-prof-and-tas-
group@brandeis.edu, which you can always find at the top of this handout and our Moodle page. We will
aim to reply to all emails that arrive during the week within one business day, i.e. 24 hours. For emails that
arrive Friday nights, over the weekend, or on holidays, we will do our best to reply as soon as we can, but
at the latest these will receive a reply on Monday or whatever day we return from the holiday. Emails sent
at night will not receive a reply until the next morning, at the earliest. This unfortunately means that, for
emails sent the night before an assignment is due, we will generally not be able to reply before class meets
the next day.
Please do your best to see us during the scheduled office hours times, if at all possible, when you need help
with the material. If you are unable to attend any of the coming scheduled office hour times, or you need
more one-on-one time than is possible during regular office hours, please send an email to our group
address, listing some times that you can make over the next several days, and one of us will do our best to
see you in an individual appointment within a week of receiving your email.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Class Participation, Absences, and the Importance of Attending Classes and Recitations
You are responsible for the content of the entire session at every class meeting and recitation, whether or
not you are present. You are also responsible for participating in each Group Exercise Assignment,
including working as directed with the other member(s) of your assigned group.
We take attendance at each class and recitation, and your final grade may be lowered by 5-10% for failure
to attend and/or participate regularly. I construe participation, minimally, as being present in all classes
and recitation, actively and attentively listening to the discussion, and participating fully in all Group
Assignments. Asking questions and contributing observations is also very encouraged. Any credit/no
credit assignments I may give are considered part of this assessment of your attendance and participation.
Since there is no textbook for this course—and, as described below, no consulting of outside textbooks,
other books, websites, or any other sources or people outside our class is allowed—it is class meetings,
recitations, and assignment work (rather than outside sources or reading) that present all course content.
This means that the class and recitation content is extremely important. It is not possible to pass the course
without attending and absorbing the class and recitation material. This course involves learning about
natural language syntax—and about how to make generalizations and form theories about syntactic
patterns—not from me telling you about this, but instead by teaching you how to do this yourself. It is not
possible to recover class material from outside reading.
Notes on missing class due to illness (including COVID), or religious observances:
• This is an in-person course. Classes/recitations will NOT be streamed live; you must attend in person.
I have set up our classroom to automatically record each class and recitation session, and you can
access these recordings from a link on our Moodle page. Watching the recording does NOT give you
credit for attendance in class that day, but it will be crucial to watch the recording and/or get the class
notes from another student for any sessions that you come to late, leave early, or miss.
• If you come down with COVID or another illness and are not able to be in class for more than two
sessions, let me know, and I will work with you on ways to keep up with the class material. I will take
your efforts in catching up and/or staying caught up into account in assessing attendance and
participation at the end of the semester.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:

In the event that campus is closed, has a delayed start, or closes early due to weather issues or other
emergencies, please check your email for an announcement from me or the TA(s) regarding any ways that
our course is impacted, including affected class meetings, recitations, office hours, and assignment due
dates. I will generally try to hold class on Zoom if I can, in such situations. In the event that this happens,
students will be encouraged to attend synchronously (i.e. live) if possible, but will not be penalized if they
need to join asynchronously by watching the recording.

PHILOSOPHY 107B - KANT'S MORAL THEORY
Mondays, Wednesdays, ursdays 11:15–12:05
Prof. Kate Moran (kmoran@brandeis.edu)
OFFICE HOURS : Mondays, 9-11a
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
COURSE OVERVIEW
is course focuses on Immanuel Kant's moral philosophy, which he took to include both ethics
and legal/political philosophy. e first few weeks of the course will be devoted to understanding
his foundational ethical arguments in the Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals. Aer that,
we will focus on specific topics in Kant's moral philosophy. Your major project in this class is a
term paper on a topic of your choosing.
LEARNING GOALS
Content – By the end of the semester, you should have a clear understanding of the foundational
claims and arguments in Kant's moral philosophy, how these compare to other moral theories,
and how these foundational claims inform specific moral questions.
Skills–iscourseshouldhelpyoudevelopskillsinreadingandanalyzingtexts,hearingandan-
alyzingarguments,organizingideasinwritingandspeech,anddevisinganddevelopingawritten
argument. is course also fulfills the digital literacy requirement for the philosophy major. You
will learn about various digital research and writing methods useful in philosophy.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Attendance is essential to your success in this course. In past semesters there has been a direct cor-
relation between students' attendance and their course grade. It is very difficult to understand
Kant the first time around just by reading the text!
Students are expected to come to class having done the reading carefully. A good sign that you
havedonethereadingcarefullyisthatyouhaveawell-formulatedquestionorcommentaboutthe
reading.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Studentsshouldalwayshavetherequiredreadingsforthatclasssessionwiththeminclass.Itistypi-
cal for students in philosophy seminars to annotate their readings with comments and questions
before class and during class discussion.
Success in this 4 credit hour course is based on the expectation that students will spend a
minimum of 9 hours of study time per week in preparation for class (readings, papers, discussion
sections, preparation for exams, etc.).
spans.label: CONDUCT

spans.text:
AUGUST 27 – SEPT 29: THE GROUNDWORK FOR THE METAPHYSICS OF MORALS
During this section of class, we will work our way through Kant's
Groundwork. Readings will be anounced as we go along. Please
start by reading the Preface for the first two days of class.
OCTOBER 6–10: ORAL MIDTERMS
ese will take place in my office (Mandel 111) or via Zoom, in
case of illness or emergency.
OCTOBER 15: FINAL PAPER DISCUSSION
We will discuss the final paper and how to use digital resources to
find sources for philosophy papers, etc.
OCTOBER 20–23: (WHY) DOES THE MORAL LAW APPLY TO ME? THE 'FACT OF REASON'
(i) Review section III of the Groundwork
(ii) Critique of Practical Reason, V 19–41
OCTOBER 27–30: MORAL MOTIVATION: RESPECT AND SYMPATHY
(i) Critique of Practical Reason Book 1, Chapter III (On the
Incentives of Pure Practical Reason), V 71–89
(ii) Doctrine of Virtue (second part of e Metaphysics of Morals),
§34 and 35.
NOVEMBER 3–6: ETHICS VS. RIGHT
(i) Introduction to e Metaphysics of Morals
(i) Introduction to the Doctrine of Right (first part of the MM)
NOVEMBER 10–13: LYING - TO YOURSELF AND OTHERS
(i) Doctrine of Virtue, VI 429–431
(ii) "On a Supposed Right to Lie from Philanthropy" (LATTE)
NOVEMBER 13: Guest Speaker/Q&A, Jens Timmermann, author of Kant and e
Supposed Right to Lie (Cambridge, 2025)
NOVEMBER 17–20: SEX AND MARRIAGE
(i) Doctrine of Right, §1-10 and §22-27
(ii) Doctrine of Virtue VI 417–428
NOVEMBER 24: CATH-UP DAY OR DISCUSSION
- 4 -

DECEMBER 1–4: CHARITY AND DEMANDINGNESS
(i) Review discussions of imperfect duty from Groundwork 1 & 2
(ii) Doctrine of Virtue, VI 386–394 (from the Introduction to the
Doctrine of Virtue) and §23–33 (duties of love, beneficence,
gratitude)
DECEMBER 8–11: COSMOPOLITAN RIGHT
Doctrine of Right, §53–Conclusion (not including appendix)
"Toward Perpetual Peace" (appendix optional) (LATTE)

spans.text:
SENIOR STUDIO CLASS OBJECTIVES
In this course, students will be expected to develop a thorough body of work throughout the
semester. Student work can be undertaken in sculpture, painting, or intermedia. Students
should work within a consistent subject matter across a grouping of numerous works of art. At
the same time, students will be expected to challenge the limits of their own subject matter.
The body of work is the equivalent of a thesis project in other studies, which will culminate in
the final exhibition at the end of the semester. All students enrolled in Senior Studio must
participate in the final exhibition. By the end of the semester, students should be confident in
their ability to be self-directed, critical thinkers, and creative makers. This should be evident in
the high quality of artworks in the final exhibition in both concept and craft.
COURSE EXPECTATIONS
• Attendance: Be in class and be on time
• Openness to risk-taking and experimentation
• Timeliness: Completion of projects to meet course schedule
• Full engagement in all critiques (individual and group)
• Thoughtful and skillful documentation of all artworks*
• Willingness to contribute to and care for our creative studio community
• Willingness to maintain our shared studio workspaces
• Commitment to work outside of class (minimum 8 hours per week for 4 credit course)

*Documentation: Students are required to upload their images/files to a cloud-based storage
(Google Drive) as designated by the instructor.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
ACADEMIC INTEGRITY
You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. Please consult Brandeis
University's Rights and Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic
integrity. Citation and pulling from other artists is a welcome practice, but you must make your
mark on ideas.
Artificial Intelligence
AI may help generate ideas, but you must simultaneously learn to find inspiration and unique
creative ideas for yourself. Also consider the energy and environmental effects of using AI and
deciding when the cost is worth the reward.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:
ACCOMODATIONS
Brandeis (and I) seek to welcome and include all students. If you are a student who needs
accommodations as outlined in an accommodation letter, please talk with me and present your
letter of accommodation as soon as possible. I want to provide your accommodations, but
cannot do so retroactively. I want to support and work with you to establish how your
accommodations can be used for our course. If you have questions about documenting a
disability or requesting accommodations, please contact Student Accessibility Support at 781-
736-3470 or access@brandeis.edu.
spans.label: ACCOM

spans.text:
Attendance Policy
• Chronic tardiness (more than five tardies) results in an absence.
• 30 minutes late is an absence
• Leaving early, 30 minutes or more, is an absence
• 2 unexcused absences will automatically decrease your final grade
• If you know you will be late or absent due to religious observance or extenuating
circumstance, email us in advance. It is your responsibility to discuss your absence with
us, and to make-up missed work.
• Absences may be excused in the event of religious observance or extenuating
circumstances: serious illness, hospitalization, family emergency. Plan your holiday
travel wisely to avoid missing class.
Late work and extensions
Due dates are hard deadlines. Deadlines are a part of life. Learn to love and abide by them.
If an emergency or illness arises that does not allow you to complete your work on time, then
you must communicate the need for an extension at least one class ahead of a due date.
Depending on the circumstances, you may or may not be granted an extension.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
GRADING
Grading in this course will be based primarily on the quality of and devotion to the body of
work generated throughout the semester. All completed course work is due at the end of the
semester prior to the last scheduled day of finals. Attendance is mandatory and participation in
individual critiques and group critiques is required. Students who do not work outside of the
Monday and Wednesday 2:30-5:30pm class time will be ineligible to receive an “A” grade in
the course. Exhibition at the end of the semester is equivalent to a final exam.
Projects are marked as complete or incomplete. Projects and assignments are considered
complete when they have been critiqued. The level of engagement and feedback during crit is
a measure of its success as an artwork.
Major Projects 40%
Based on ambition, quality of execution, depth of thinking, and timeliness.
Assignments 20%
Based on experimentation, depth of thinking, and timeliness.
Attendance/Engagement 20%
Critiques 20%
Based on the effort to give feedback and grow from feedback.

**You must complete all projects, assignments, engage in critiques, and show in the final
exhibition in order to pass this class.
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Course Announcements
Important course updates come in the form of Moodle message posts. Please note that all
Moodle communications and my messages will go only to your Brandeis email account. You are
responsible for checking Moodle each week to keep up with course announcements.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
CRITIQUES
“While creativity is strongly bound to individualism, it is entirely dependent, especially within
an art context, on the collective.” Allan DeSouza How Art Can be Thought
GROUP CRITS
Artists do not live in a vacuum, and critique is a healthy and necessary way to work through
ideas. It is not a space to rip people apart, nor is it a space for empty compliments. Through
group critique, we will be working together to discuss each of your works and how they are
developing, and how they can continue to build. We will have a series of small group critiques,
wrapping up just after midterm.
INDIVIDUAL CRITS
Throughout the semester, individual critiques will be conducted with the professor during class
time. Students will be expected to respond to suggestions from the professor within the
framework of their studio practice. In addition, students will be required to contact other faculty
from the fine arts department and schedule two individual visits with studio faculty.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
REQUIRED COURSEWORK
• Minimum of five significant artworks in mediums of your choosing
Students are expected to complete at least four to five significant works of art. All work should
be generally impressive in both scale and ambition. Numerous exceptions for larger work are
acceptable
• Three works based on assignment prompts
• Process work
Students are expected to conduct creative research and make a range of drawings,
photographs, small sculptures, and/or small paintings that serve to feed, question, and nurture
the more ambitious works of art along. These works are made in addition to the five significant
works and are based on assigned projects from the assignment categories listed below (these
assignments must be done in conjunction with larger works and count towards the final grade).
• Documentation of artworks
Assignments Prompts
1. Small Gestures series
Provocation toward issues of intent, time, scale, commitment, practice and play
10 small works, one per day, one organizing principle.
2. Antidote
Provocation toward issues of affinity, taste, aesthetics, collapse and repair
3. Five impossible Actions
Provocation toward issues of means, method, obstacle, determination and desire
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
CLASS STRUCTURE
As an advanced studio class, you must take the reins of your art practice and work on your own
volition. Class is based in studio time which is student-led, self-motivated, mandatory time
during which students can work towards completing significant artworks and assignments. You
must be accountable, prepared with all necessary materials, and on time to maximize your use
of the valuable studio time. During classes, Lu and Joe will meet with students to provide 1:1
in-progress guidance and feedback. Classes may involve class check-ins, presentations, guest
visitors, and documentation time. Creative research assignments will be initiated through in-
class workshops. Making significant artworks is a privilege of time and requires hard, dedicated
work. This means you will have to work outside of class time.

spans.text:
HS249F-1: Social Justice, Management, and Policy
Summer 2024 – Module
5/29; 6/5; 6/10; 6/26; 7/3; 7/10
2:00pm to 4:50pm
Instructor: Monica L. Zeno-Martin Room: G1
Email: mzenomartin@brandeis.edu
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Description and Purpose:
Social Justice means different things to different people, in different places, and at different times. It is
invoked to support and justify a multiplicity of causes that range along the entire continuum of political,
economic, social, technological, and environmental (PESTE) issues. In order for managers to be effective
social and organizational change agents and be prepared for managing in environments with value
conflicts and diversity of all kinds, they must first understand what social justice means to them, how
definitions and meanings are derived, and how, why, when and where they develop their own values
and meanings of social justice. How do our own identities, shaped by the intersecting forces of class,
gender, race, sexuality, immigration etc., mold our social justice lens and our subsequent decisions as
managers?
This course will examine the mission driven sector – what does the sector and its ecosystem look like
and how do social justice missions shape the work of organizations? What choices are made or not
made that either strengthen or dilute the impact of what an organization sets out to do? How do
managers lead in organizations with social justice missions? Are there specific considerations,
challenges, and opportunities in such settings? Are managers called upon to use a different set of
decision-making tools and filters in these settings?
As current and aspiring leaders in business, government, philanthropy, and the non-profit/social impact
sector, it is critical to understand the implications of social justice in the work you do; and strategies and
tactics that facilitate or hinder social justice in the workplace and society as a whole. In other words, this
course is an examination of some of the management and policy opportunities and challenges inherent
in the Heller mission “Knowledge Advancing Social Justice.” It has the primary goal of preparing students
to transition to careers that require leadership at the intersection of management and social justice. This
course is less about concrete tools and more about a “way of thinking”, about the questions you bring to
the management problems at hand, and about the lens you use to deconstruct the exercise of power
and agency in organizational settings.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
LEADERSHIP AT THE INTERSECTION OF MANAGEMENT SCIENCE AND SOCIAL JUSTICE
"KNOWLEDGE ADVANCING SOCIAL JUSTICE"
Teaching and Learning Culture and Expectations:
This class will be a combination of facilitated discussion, lecture on key concepts, small group work, and
reflections on readings and speakers’ presentations. The experience of the class will be only as positive
as what you plan to bring to the “learning community” that we will create – as such, all of us must take
responsibility for making it the best learning environment possible. Facilitated discussion will raise
challenges and issues that will feel hard at some moments – the goal of the class is to push each one of
us beyond our comfort zone to a place of engagement with new ideas and ways of thinking. The
presumption is that transformation/change is never a smooth/easy process, but it is a necessary process
if we are to grow as managers and people.
We understand that the learning environment is made more complicated by the COVID 19 realities and
will make appropriate adjustments accordingly. The expectation is that we will create a community of
caring colleagues and hold the principles of inclusion, respect for others’ ideas, and responsibility for
one’s own and each other’s learning.
Some guiding principles for our learning culture:
● The class environment will reflect respect for different opinions, even as we push each other
constructively to learn new ways of thinking and examining our own positions on important
social justice issues;
● We will have read and thought about the material, class, and speaker presentations and will
take our own and each other’s learning seriously;
● All written work will be original and carefully completed – there are no shortcuts to learning;
rev. 5/31/24 Page 2 of 9

● Speaking in class is not the only way to show participation – but engagement is important;
● We will bring our whole selves to class – it is the only way to learn and we all need the practice
to become impactful managers.
We will use a combination of strategies to make class engaging and productive. I will invite speakers
from mission-driven organizations during some of the classes. We will take breaks at least every 60 to 75
minutes; use small group discussions; watch short video clips to break up the format. Sometimes I will
assign a video to watch outside of class, on your own time, and shorten the class for that day.

spans.text: Social Justice Organization Checklist – 30% of total grade
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Assignment due no later than 11:59pm ET on Friday, June 14, 2024
Populate the categories of a template that I will provide, with four to five questions
under each category that will help you to assess a social justice organization’s alignment
of practice with its stated mission and goals.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: • Social Justice Organizational Research – 50% of total grade
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Assignment due by 11:59pm ET on Friday, July 18, 2024
Based on the framework that you build in the checklist, pick four categories that you are
most interested in analyzing, and research a non-profit organization of your
choice/interest to see how well that organization fits or does not fit into the categories
(in the framework) that you have picked to analyze. Explain whether the organization is
“aligned” or “misaligned” with its mission and goals for social justice.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Prepare four to five slides to show how the organization is aligned or misaligned based
on your framework.
The research should consider the organization’s website, its publicly available materials
such as Form 990 and other organizational documents, media items/stories, any
additional information that students may already have about the organization, or any
additional material that you are able to access from previous knowledge of the
organization. What questions were you not able to answer based on public information?
Please refer to and incorporate at least three to four references to readings/ class
discussions to meaningfully ground your responses.
On the last day of class, you will be paired with one other group or individual and
discuss your projects with each other. The goal will be to provide feedback on your
classmates’ work and thinking, serve as a critical friend to help strengthen their thinking,
and learn how you might incorporate something valuable into your project.
Following this opportunity for sharing and discussion, you will finalize your slides and
submit by the final project submission deadline.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
Academic Integrity:
You are responsible for learning and understanding the Heller School policy on Citation and Academic
Integrity as established by the Brandeis guide for Student Rights and Responsibilities. Violations of
University policies on academic integrity, described in Section 3 of Rights and Responsibilities, may
result in failure in the course or on the assignment, and could end in suspension from the University. If
you are in doubt about the completion of any assignment in this course, you must ask for clarification.
spans.label: INTEGRITY

spans.text:

The Heller School seeks to welcome and include all students. If you are a student who needs
accommodations, please talk with the Professor as soon as possible.
If you have questions about documenting a disability or requesting accommodations, please
contact Student Accessibility Support at 781.736.3470 or

spans.text: In-Person Attendance: This is an in-person class and there is no zoom link available for it. If you are not\nwell, please visit the University Health Services. There may be out-of-town class speakers who will\npresent on Zoom since they are not available to travel for our presentation.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: Attendance is Required: If you are going to miss one class, or are going to be late or need to leave early,\nplease let the Professor know ahead of time. The class only meets six times this summer and your\nattendance and involvement is important. Please be prepared to begin on time.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text:
Cell phones: Please turn down all cell phones before joining class. If you are experiencing an emergency
and need to be reachable, please let the Professor know before class.
Laptops: Although it is fine to take notes on your laptop, there is enough of a temptation to check and
respond to emails and other messages and surf the web while in class. Please exercise self-discipline if
you need to have your laptop open so that you can be fully present for class participation.

Class 2: Wednesday, June 5
Social Justice in Practice I
Organizations: Social Justice Missions, Values, Practices - Frameworks and Key Questions
Guest Speaker: Marji Erickson Warfield, Senior Scientist, Lurie Institute on Disability Policy,
Center for Youth and Communities, Heller School for Social Policy and Management, Brandeis
University
As a researcher of social policy with a focus on disabilities, children, youth, and families, Marji
Erickson Warfield's work seeks to understand and evaluate ways to promote the well-being of
children, youth and young adults from marginalized communities, often centering individuals
with disabilities and youth in foster care. Current work is investigating an intensive in-home
tutoring program designed to enhance the social-emotional and literacy skills of young children
in foster care, examining various approaches to connect youth to the workforce in general and
STEM and technical fields in particular, and exploring how to provide more inclusive
opportunities for youth with disabilities through connections to college-age mentors.
Dr. Erickson Warfield has expertise in a variety of qualitative, quantitative and mixed methods
approaches to research and evaluation as well as significant experience in survey research. Her
work has been funded by a range of federal and foundation sources.
Required Readings/Resources:
HopeWell website
What Does It take to Deliver on HopeWell’s Promise? Frameworks for Leadership and Management for A
Social Justice and Learning Organization, Hughes, D.M. and Warfield, M.E., 2021.
Nonprofits in the United States, Robert Glavin and Marco Tavanti, in Nonprofit Management 101: A
Complete and Practical Guide for Leaders and Professionals, Second Edition, Wiley Publishers. Eds.,
Heyman, Darian Rodriguez and Brenner, Laila, 2019. pp. 5 – 19
Introduction: What is Social Entrepreneurship? Roger Martin and Sally Osberg in Getting Beyond Better,
Harvard Business Review Press, 2015. pp: 1 – 24
Building Resilient Organizations, Maurice Mitchell. The Forge, November 29, 2022
Inequality Regimes: Gender, Class, and Race in Organizations, Joan Acker. Gender and Society 20.4: pp.
441 – 464.
LaMarche, Gara. (4 December 2009). Speech: The Corner Office, Non-Profit Edition: A Practitioner’s
Thoughts on Social Justice Management. The Atlantic Philanthropies.
Democratic Management: Lessons from Worker Cooperatives, Rebecca Bauen. Non-Profit Quarterly,
June 2, 2021.
rev. 5/31/24 Page 6 of 9

Class 3: Monday, June 10
Social Justice in Practice II
Organizations: Social Justice Missions, Values, Practices - Frameworks and Key Questions
Guest Speaker: Heidi Brooks, Chief Operating Officer, National Institute for Children’s Health
Quality
Heidi Brooks (she/her) serves as Chief Operating Officer (COO) of the National Institute for Children’s
Health Quality (NICHQ), a public health nonprofit driving change to improve children’s health. In this
role, her key responsibilities include overseeing finance, communications, operations, IT and HR. She
most recently served as the COO of YouthBuild USA, the support center for nearly 300 programs in the
US and abroad serving 16- to 24-year-old “opportunity youth” who are not working or in school. Prior to
joining YouthBuild, Brooks led finance, operations, programs, strategy and evaluation as COO of the
Schott Foundation for Public Education, a charitable intermediary investing in education justice.
The thread that runs through her career is helping people get out of poverty. Brooks started her career
in international development, working in 15 countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America managing,
designing, and auditing anti-poverty programs for the World Bank and several large NGOs. After moving
back to the US for business school, she worked for The Bridgespan Group as a consultant and was then
recruited to become the Director of Community Relations at John Hancock Financial Services, where she
oversaw $11 million in cash and in-kind annual giving and expanded the MLK Summer Scholars program
to provide a record 650 summer jobs for Boston teens. From there she served as head of the Citizens
Bank Foundation, where she worked with the CEO to set strategy for $18 million in annual giving the
innovative Citizens Helping Citizens employee giving campaign and more than doubled volunteer hours.
Brooks holds a B.A. from the University of South Carolina, an M.A. from the Johns Hopkins School of
Advanced International Studies, and an M.B.A. from Harvard Business School. She has been active with a
number of nonprofits and foundations, and currently serves as vice-chair for the of the Point32 Health
Care Foundation and is on the investment committee for an HBS impact investing class. Brooks loves
scuba diving, CrossFit and outdoor exploration with her family and is trying to visit 100 countries.
Required Readings/Resources:
The Nonprofit Starvation Cycle, Ann Goggins Gregory & Don Howard, Stanford Social Innovation Review,
Fall 2009
Unleashing Philanthropy’s Big Bets for Social Change. William Foster, Gail Perreault, Bradley
Seeman. The Bridgespan Group. Spring 2019.
Class 4: Wednesday, June 26
Organizations: The Mission Driven Sector - How to Think About Scale, Sustainability, and Impact
Required Readings/Resources:
Earning the Right to Scale, Meehan III and Jonker, Stanford Social Innovation Review, March 30, 2018
rev. 5/31/24 Page 7 of 9

Scaling the Solution, in Roger Martin and Sally Osberg in Getting Beyond Better, Harvard Business
Review Press, 2015. pp. 161 – 181
Five Programmatic Lessons in Scaling Impact. Katie Mounts, Stanford Social Innovation Review, Jul. 21,
2015
Class 5: Wednesday, July 3
Social Justice in Practice III
Organizations: Social Justice Missions, Values, Practices - Frameworks and Key Questions
Guest Speaker: Tiffany Murphy, Vice President, Program Impact, YouthBuild USA
Tiffany S. Murphy is the Vice President of DOL YouthBuild Program Impact at YouthBuild USA,
where she drives the strategy and operations of a $23 million, multi-year, federal technical
assistance contract for 200+ U.S. Department of Labor-funded YouthBuild programs. She
oversees teams responsible for providing comprehensive technical assistance to implement the
DOL YouthBuild pre-apprenticeship model. This includes content innovation to scale promising
practices, performance data support and impact evaluation, peer-driven in-person and virtual
trainings, coaching, and more.
Tiffany has over sixteen years of progressive nonprofit leadership experience in criminal justice,
postsecondary education, and youth and workforce development. This includes providing
thought partnership and leadership to organizational leaders in the private, nonprofit, and
government sectors. In her previous role as YouthBuild USA's Associate Director, Postsecondary
Access and Success, Tiffany managed and scaled a national advisory council of first-generation
college students. This consisted of recommending and refining policies supporting opportunity
youth academically, professionally, and personally as they persist in their educational journeys.
Tiffany has a Master of Science in nonprofit management from Northeastern University and a
Bachelor of Science in sociology with a minor in criminal justice from Salem State University.
She also has a paralegal professional certification from Northeastern University.
Required Readings/Resources:
YouthBuild USA website
David P. Weikert Center for Youth Program Quality at the Forum for Youth Investment website

Identity and Racism (Part 2), Turnaround for Children

doc_id: 261ENG 22A 1 South Asian Ways of Watching Films and Graphic Narratives Sahid Mondal
school: School of Arts, Humanities, and Culture
course_name: 261ENG 22A 1 South Asian Ways of Watching Films and Graphic Narratives Sahid Mondal

spans.text:
South Asian Ways of Watching: Films & Graphic Narratives
Instructor: Sahid Mondal (English)
Spring 2026/ Tuesday-Thursday 2:20 PM -3:40 PM/ Shiffman: 120
Office: 143 Rabb
Office hours: Monday/Thursday - 4 PM – 5 PM/By Appointment
Appointment Link: calendly.com/sahidmondal/book
Contact: sahidmondal@brandeis.edu
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text: Fulfills the following requirements: [ djw hum ] [ENG Post-1800, ENG Multicultural\nLiterature, World Anglophone, ENG Media Film, SAS Elective]
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Prerequisites: None
Credit Hours: Success in this four-credit course is based on the expectation that students will
spend a minimum of nine hours of study time per week in preparation for class (reading and
watching texts, discussions, preparation for exams, etc.).
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Communications: Any changes to the syllabus or class schedule, including accommodations for
weather or developments related to COVID-19, will be communicated via the class email list and
posted on Moodle. Please plan to check your Brandeis email account and Moodle daily to stay
informed. Please contact me by email or come to office hours (virtually or in person) to discuss
any additional questions and concerns. On weekdays I check email periodically between the
hours of 10 a.m. and 5 p.m.; I make every effort to respond to a message within 24 hours. On
weekends, I check my email once or twice a day, but I may go a day without checking. Please
plan accordingly.
spans.label: ADMIN

spans.text:
Course Description:
How do we watch South Asia and how does South Asia watch itself? This course invites you into
a vivid journey through film and graphic storytelling. Each week, we’ll pair a film with a graphic
narrative, crafting unexpected conversations across mediums. We’ll trace national borders and
fractures, negotiations of caste and care, love and sex, displacement and diaspora, ecological
entanglements, and the defiance of protest movements in South Asia to watch how South Asia
imagines future for itself.
What happens when the same story of love or protest is told on a movie screen versus across the
panels of a graphic narrative? How do South Asian artists use images, whether moving or drawn,
to make us feel the weight of Partition, caste, war, or ecological collapse? Can watching a rap
battle in Mumbai or reading about women’s sit-in protest in Delhi change how we imagine the
future? This class will hold lively discussions and comparative analysis along with creative
opportunities to experiment with your own visual storytelling. By the end of the semester, you’ll
have sharpened your visual literacy and gained fresh insights into how visual stories themselves
make South Asian worlds possible.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Intellectual & Learning Goals:
1. Develop critical tools to watch and read films and graphic narratives as languages
forming the visual economy of South Asia.
2. Examine how rhythm, silence, framing, and color create meaning across graphic
narratives and films.
3. Explore how histories, caste, gender, ecology, diaspora, and protest have been visually
imagined and contested.
4. Put film and graphic narrative into conversation to uncover what each makes visible or
leaves unseen.
5. Compare not only medium, but how artists from India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka,
and the diaspora “watch” their worlds differently.
6. Work critically and creatively by pairing traditional analysis with opportunities to
storyboard, sketch, record or remix scenes yourself.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Terminology: In this course, we will encounter and use a range of terms related to South Asia,
including—but not limited to—“South Asian,” “Indian,” “Pakistani,” “Bangladeshi,” “Sri
Lankan,” “diaspora,” “Muslim,” “Hindu,” “Dalit,” “Adivasi,” “caste,” “tribe,” and “Indigenous.”
We will also engage with regionally specific, linguistic, and political identifiers as they appear in
films, graphic narratives, and historical contexts. Racial slurs, including the N-word, may appear
in course materials for critical analysis. They will be discussed with care and context and not used
casually or repeated aloud.
Each of these terms has a particular history and set of political, cultural, and affective meanings.
While some terms are sometimes used interchangeably in everyday speech, their implications
shift depending on context, medium, and speaker. In this class, we will use these terms carefully
and critically, attending to how they are mobilized within visual texts and how they have been
shaped by colonial histories, nationalist projects, caste hierarchies, religious majoritarianism, and
global migration.
In particular, the term “Indian” may refer to citizenship within the nation-state of India, to
cultural or diasporic affiliation, or to a historical category produced through colonial rule. We will
be attentive to these distinctions. When necessary, we will pause to clarify how a term is being
used, by whom, and to what effect. This course encourages students to ask what images show
and what language accompanies them, and how naming, labeling, and categorization shape
visibility, belonging, and power. Respectful, thoughtful engagement with terminology is an
essential part of our shared learning environment.
spans.label: DESCRIP

spans.text:
Course Materials:
The following graphic narrative texts (not films) are available for purchase at the Brandeis
University Bookstore. I have also attached links to some vendors on the Moodle page. The films
will be available on the course page on Moodle under Course Videos. If you are having difficulty
purchasing course materials, please make an appointment with your student financial services or
academic services advisor to discuss possible funding options and/or textbook alternatives.
Please connect with me if nothing works.
Films
1. Supermen of Malegaon (2008) – Faiza Ahmad Khan
2. Garm Hava (1974) – M.S. Sathyu
3. Joyland (2022) – Saim Sadiq
4. The Big Sick (2017) – Michael Showalter
5. All That Breathes (2022) – Shaunak Sen
6. Gully Boy (2019) – Zoya Akhtar
Graphic Narratives
1. This Side That Side (2013) – Vishwajyoti Ghosh
2. Bhimayana (2011) – Durgabai Vyam, Subhash Vyam, Srividya Natarajan, S. Anand
3. Vanni: A Family’s Struggle Through the Sri Lankan Conflict (2019) – Benjamin Dix &
Lindsay Pollock
4. All Quiet in Vikaspuri (2015) – Sarnath Banerjee
5. Shaheen Bagh: A Graphic Recollection (2021) – Ita Mehrotra
spans.label: MATERIAL

spans.text:
This course is structured around 10 films and graphic narratives, with no secondary reading. The
bulk of the material will unfold through class discussions and collaborative visual analyses. Since
the course develops cumulatively, attendance is essential for your own understanding and for
your peers’ collective experience. Four (4) unexcused absences will result in failure of the course.
If you miss more than three classes, you must meet with the instructor to discuss how to
demonstrate continued engagement (e.g., through extra work).
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: 1. Attendance (15 points) - Ongoing
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text: 2. Participation (15 points) - Ongoing
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Participation includes attentive and engaged presence, active contribution to discussions,
engagement in class activities, and peer feedback. We will hold small-group discussions to
encourage in-depth dialogue. You are also encouraged to visit office hours to discuss the
material, clarify ideas, or brainstorm project topics. If you are hesitant to speak in class, visiting
office hours is an alternate way to demonstrate engagement.
spans.label: ATTEND

spans.text: 3. Zine Club Collective Sketch Activity (10 points) – Feb 10
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
This collaborative creative session will take place during one of our class meetings, in partnership
with Brandeis Zine Club. Together, we will complete a guided visual prompt linked to course
themes. In a circle, each participant begins with a sketch in response to the prompt; drawings are
then passed along for others to add text (captions or dialogue/narrative commentary) to create a
short, collectively authored zine. Through this process, we will practice peer-to-peer creativity
and collective storytelling, and core skills for reading and making visual media.
To receive full credit, you must:
1. Be present during the Zine Club session.
2. Participate fully in the collaborative prompt activity.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 4. In-Class Visual Workshop (10 points) – Mar 5
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
We will participate in an in-class visual workshop for this assignment. We will explore a text (of
your choice) from the course through a short creative reflection.
For this workshop we will try to create one of the following forms:
A. A short comic or graphic sketch (minimum four panels, with text integrated as captions
or speech bubbles).
B. A photo collage or visual assemblage (minimum four elements such as photos, sketches,
or drawings).
C. A single image or drawing with a 150–200-word reflective paragraph connecting it to a
film or graphic narrative from the course.
The produced creative material must:
1. Explicitly refer or connect to a course text.
2. Demonstrate thoughtful engagement with form and theme.
At the end of the workshop, you will upload the produced visual material to Moodle under the
Assignments tab by Mar 7. You can revise the work you create in the class before the
submission. Your classmates may view and respond to your posts, but responses are optional
and not graded—but highly encouraged.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 5. Prospectus Roundtable & Submission (5+10 points) – Mar 26
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
On March 26, each student will come with a prepared 500-word prospectus for the final
project. This short piece should outline your final project idea [SEE THE NEXT
ASSIGNMENT]—how you plan to analyze, reimagine, or juxtapose two visual forms (e.g., a

film scene and a graphic panel). You will be divided into groups to share your ideas with your
peers. You can incorporate any feedback you receive from your peers before you submit the
prospectus on March 27 on Moodle under Assignments tab. You will receive feedback
from the instructor by March 30.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text: 6. Final Project Presentation/Exhibition (Draft) & Submission (10+20 points)
spans.label: GRADE

spans.text:
Your final project extends your creative and analytical work into a sustained exploration of visual
form, affect, and media. You will choose one format from the list below and develop it in
conversation with the instructor. Each project must engage at least one film and one graphic
narrative – (one of those (either the film or the graphic text) must be from the course), and it
should build on insights developed through your visual workshop and activities and the
prospectus.
Possible formats include:
1. A Graphic Narrative – Extend your workshopped visual material into a longer or more
complex sequence – at least 4 pages in A5 size (Dividing an A4 paper in half results in
two smaller sheets of the A5 size). The length, scale, and medium (digital or hand-drawn)
can be discussed during office hours.
2. A Short Video Film – Create a 3–5-minute short that reimagines or responds to a
course text and interacts with a text out of the syllabus. This may remix existing footage,
including original material, or combine both.
3. A Collage of Graphic Narratives and Film Stills – Combine drawn panels, film stills,
and/or text to trace a comparative visual argument – one of the texts must be from out
of the syllabus. We will discuss the balance of components during office hours.
4. A Photo Series – Develop a photographic sequence (6–10 images) that stages, mirrors,
or challenges a scene, motif, or affect from the course. The curatorial statement must be
2-page long.
5. An Affective Audio Analysis – Produce a 5-7-minute sound essay that interprets a
scene or visual work through its emotional and sensory registers. May include voice-over,
ambient sound, or archival audio.
6. A Video Presentation – A 5–7-minute multimedia presentation that brings together
clips, stills, and commentary to perform your comparative analysis of a film and a graphic
narrative.
All final projects must:
• Incorporate a minimum of one film and one graphic narrative. At least one of these texts
must come from the course syllabus, while the other may be chosen independently or
during office hours.
• Demonstrate awareness of form, composition, rhythm, and affect.
• Be accompanied by a brief Curatorial Statement (500 - 1000 words) explaining your
concept, choices, and critical insights.
spans.label: ASSIGN

spans.text:
A NOTE ON THE PRESENTATION –
We will present or exhibit the final projects on Apr 21 and Apr 23 (Th) – We will discuss
the possible presentation/exhibition format during your one-on-one meeting, and the
presentation duration will be decided after the first two weeks of the classAssignment

spans.text:
Academic Integrity: You are expected to be honest in all of your academic work. This includes
avoiding plagiarism, which is the use of someone else’s ideas or words in your writing without
acknowledging the source. Students may be required to submit work to plagiarism detection
software to verify originality. The use of AI is not permitted in this course for all the

assignments; if I suspect that any part of an assignment was composed using AI, I will ask you to
verify the work as your own; please document all stages of your research and writing process
(including notes, outlines, and drafts). If you cannot demonstrate that it is your own work, you
will not receive credit for the assignment. Please consult Brandeis University Rights and
Responsibilities for all policies and procedures related to academic integrity. Cases of alleged
academic dishonesty will be forwarded to the director of academic integrity. Sanctions for
academic dishonesty can include failing grades and/or suspension from the university. A guide
to Citing Sources can be found on the Brandeis Library website. Please see me if you have
additional questions about proper attribution and citation of sources.
